## PAGE 00001: 2024x Version News

- page_id: `175472689`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/175472689/2024x+Version+News
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-06-10T16:31:46.048+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

top

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Released on: November 10, 2023

In this release, Cameo Simulation Toolkit introduces a number of powerful functionalities. The newproductversion allows you to run server-side simulation with modified Simulation Configuration properties and even employ a virtual Simulation Configuration without modifying the Teamwork Cloud project.The product also enables live unit conversion tosimplifymodel analysis and reduce the likelihood of errors. Beyond these enhancements, you can customize the Histogram appearance, control the simulation termination logic with the help of a new Auto Terminate property, and benefit from other smaller improvements.

- 
- 

### Server-Side Simulation

##### Modifying Simulation Configuration Properties

Now, you can run server-side simulations with modified properties of a Simulation Configuration without having to change the Teamwork Cloud project. This approach provides a more convenient method to modify simulation properties without a modeling tool. 
It's even feasible to run a simulation without explicitly specifying a Simulation Configuration; instead, a virtual Simulation Configuration can be employed. This gives you the possibility to quickly choose a different execution target and define its simulation properties.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The REST API request body with the 'simconfig' parameter which allows specifying simulation properties.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Enablingthe autocompletion option for the simulation using Jupyter Notebook.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation using REST API' space='']>]]>

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation using Jupyter Notebook' space='']>]]>

##### DSLS Licensing Server Support

From version 2024x, in addition to FlexNet, server-side simulation supports the DSLS licensing server.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='DSLS installation and licensing' space='IL2024x']>]]>

### Live Unit Conversion for Runtime Objects

Cameo Simulation Toolkit 2024x introduces live unit conversion for simulation runtime objects. When Value Properties are connected using a Binding Connector, their runtime values will be automatically converted if they have compatible Value Types (like grams and kilograms). This feature simplifies model analysis, especially when combining elements from various sources, and reduces the possibility of errors.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Parametric Diagram with runtime values of Value Properties being automatically converted according to their Value Types during simulation.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Unit Conversion' space='']>]]>

### Histogram Improvements

We're happy to present a new functionality that will allow you to tailor the Histogram appearance. With this improvement, you can define a custom number of data bins in which the results will be distributed and achieve the desired Histogram shape and precision. Furthermore, the edge of the last data bin will now match the maximum value of the value array, ensuring accurate representation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A sample Histogram with the custom number of data bins. Here, the **Number Of Bins** property is set to 50.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Histogram' space='']>]]>

### Auto-Terminating Simulation Configurations

Simulation Configurations now have a new **Auto Terminate** property, allowing you to manage the simulation termination logic. If you set this property to *true*, the simulation is terminated when it completes or after the initialization phase if it does not start automatically. When the property is set to *false*, the simulation continues to run without termination, hanging at the end or after the initialization phase if it does not start automatically.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A new **Auto Terminate** property of a Simulation Configuration.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='']>]]>

### Other Improvements

- Generating HTML has become easier, thanks to the availability of the **Generate HTML** and **Generate and Attach HTML** actions in new, convenient locations. These actions can now be accessed from the context menu of a User Interface Modeling Diagram or a UI element in the Containment tree. Additionally, you can invoke the actions by right-clicking a property symbol, provided its type is a Widget. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
- The simulation now considers the Decimal Places project option.
- The Parametric Execution Profile has been eliminated. The constraints for parametric execution have been moved to the Simulation Profile .

**Documentation**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation' space='']

## PAGE 00002: Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action

- page_id: `136729802`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729802/Accept+Change+Structural+Feature+Event+Action
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:31.557+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['accept-change-structural-feature-event-action']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action is an Accept Event Action that waits for a Change Structural Feature Event. The Event will be sent to an object when the value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. When the Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action is activated, the simulation stays on the Action until it accepts the Change Structural Feature Event that will be sent once the value of the specified Structural Feature changes. The following figure shows the Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action that waits for the Change Structural Feature Event of value property, in this example, **time**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
An Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action in an Activity diagram.

When the value of the value property **time** changes, the Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action will be activated. The token that flows through the pin **before** is the old value of the value property **time** and the token that flows to the pin **after** is the current value of the value property **time**. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will then simulate the next Action.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action is an Accept Event Action that waits for a Change Structural Feature Event. The Event will be sent to an object when the value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. When the Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action is activated, the simulation stays on the Action until it accepts the Change Structural Feature Event that will be sent once the value of the specified Structural Feature changes. The following figure shows the Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action that waits for the Change Structural Feature Event of value property, in this example, <strong>time</strong>.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="An Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action in an Activity Diagram" ac:alt="An Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action in an Activity Diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AcceptChangeStructuralFeatureEvent.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />An Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action in an Activity diagram.</h6><p>When the value of the value property <strong>time</strong> changes, the Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action will be activated. The token that flows through the pin <strong>before</strong> is the old value of the value property <strong>time</strong> and the token that flows to the pin <strong>after</strong> is the current value of the value property <strong>time</strong>. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will then simulate the next Action. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729869 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00003: Action languages

- page_id: `136729869`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729869/Action+languages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:38.628+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide
- labels: ['action-languages']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

UML and SysML allow you to define an Action or a Behavior using an expression or a scripting language, for example, opaque Action, opaque Behavior, and function Behavior. Cameo Simulation Toolkit can simulate the script or the expression in your model during the model simulation, when an Element that contains the script is activated.

Normally, a script will be defined in an opaque expression. Therefore, if your model has any value specifications (like guards, constraints, decisions, default values, and opaque Behaviors) that can be defined with opaque expressions, they will be simulated during the model simulation.

Some special cases, however, apply for Activity edges that do not have any guard expression and decision nodes that do not have any Decision Input specified. The name of the Activity edge and the decision node will be evaluated by Cameo Simulation Toolkit using a default scripting language as shown in the figure as follows.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The decision input that is unspecified and the guards that are empty.

You can use different languages for different opaque expressions in your model. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use a scripting engine that matches the specified language of the opaque expression to simulate the script content.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><br /></p><p>UML and SysML allow you to define an Action or a Behavior using an expression or a scripting language, for example, opaque Action, opaque Behavior, and function Behavior. Cameo Simulation Toolkit can simulate the script or the expression in your model during the model simulation, when an Element that contains the script is activated. </p><p>Normally, a script will be defined in an opaque expression. Therefore, if your model has any value specifications (like guards, constraints, decisions, default values, and opaque Behaviors) that can be defined with opaque expressions, they will be simulated during the model simulation.</p><p>Some special cases, however, apply for Activity edges that do not have any guard expression and decision nodes that do not have any Decision Input specified. The name of the Activity edge and the decision node will be evaluated by Cameo Simulation Toolkit using a default scripting language as shown in the figure as follows.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="scriptingLanguage.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Action languages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The decision input that is unspecified and the guards that are empty.</h6><p>You can use different languages for different opaque expressions in your model. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use a scripting engine that matches the specified language of the opaque expression to simulate the script content. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729888 space=CST2024x version=6 -->
## PAGE 00004: Action scripts APIs

- page_id: `136729888`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729888/Action+scripts+APIs
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-04-07T12:52:48.086+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages
- labels: ['action-script-api']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses Java Scripting API to evaluate scripting languages. It is a scripting language-independent framework that uses script engines from Java code.

Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports the following scripting languages

- ECMLScript/JavaScript Rhino
- Beanshell
- Groovy
- Python

Predefined variables in an Action script represent a set of variables defined by the script engine before a script evaluation is executed. You can refer to these predefined variables from any action script API.

| Variable | Description |
| --- | --- |
| $context$ or _context_ | A context or runtime object of current script evaluation. For a Constraint Block, it is the runtime object of the owner of the Block and not of the Constraint Block itself. |
| $element$ or _element_ | An owner element of the script. |
| $signal$ or _signal_ | The last signal Instance in an Event pool of a specified object. |
| $state$ or _state_ | An active State of a context or a runtime object of a current script evaluation. |
| $token$ or _token_ | A token value of an active model element. |
| self | Equivalent to $context$ or _context_. For a Constraint Block, it is the runtime object of the Constraint Block itself. |
| sig_ + "property name" | The specified property value of the last signal. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It is a scripting language-independent framework that uses script engines from Java code.</p><p>Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports the following scripting languages</p><ul><li>ECMLScript/JavaScript Rhino</li><li>Beanshell</li><li>Groovy</li><li>Python</li></ul><p>Predefined variables in an Action script represent a set of variables defined by the script engine before a script evaluation is executed. For a Constraint Block, it is the runtime object of the owner of the Block and not of the Constraint Block itself.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><span class="confluence-link">$element$ or _element_<br /></span></p></div></td><td><p>An owner element of the script.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><span class="confluence-link">$signal$ or _signal_</span></p></div></td><td><p>The last signal Instance in an Event pool of a specified object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><span class="confluence-link">$state$ or _state_<br /></span></p></div></td><td><p>An active State of a context or a runtime object of a current script evaluation.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><span class="confluence-link">$token$ or _token_<br /></span></p></div></td><td><p>A token value of an active model element.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><span class="confluence-link">self</span></div></td><td><p>Equivalent to $context$ or _context_. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730001 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00005: Activating and deactivating an element

- page_id: `136730001`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730001/Activating+and+deactivating+an+element
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:43.206+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Developer Guide > Java APIs > Engine
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An execution engine can activate a specific element with values by calling an activateElement(Element element, Collection values)) of the ExecutionEngine. To deactivate the element, it will call a deactivateElement(Element element, Collection values)) of the same class.

These two methods are mostly called in the ExecutionEngine.execute(element). For example, if an Activity element on an Activity diagram is passed from the ExecutionEngine.execute(element), we use the activateElement() and deactivateElement() methods to activate and deactivate the Actions in that particular Activity respectively.

If activateElement() or deactivateElement() is called to a specific element, it will trigger all registered engine listeners including both predefined and user-defined listeners. For example, an AnimationListener is a predefined listener that is the Subclass of an EngineListener. It is used to display a specific element animation (in highlight) on a diagram in MagicDraw.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An execution engine can activate a specific element with values by calling an <span style="text-decoration: underline;">activateElement</span>(Element element, Collection values)) of the ExecutionEngine. To deactivate the element, it will call a deactivateElement(Element element, Collection values)) of the same class.</p><p>These two methods are mostly called in the ExecutionEngine.execute(element). For example, if an Activity element on an Activity diagram is passed from the ExecutionEngine.execute(element), we use the activateElement() and deactivateElement() methods to activate and deactivate the Actions in that particular Activity respectively.</p><p>If activateElement() or deactivateElement() is called to a specific element, it will trigger all registered engine listeners including both predefined and user-defined listeners. For example, an AnimationListener is a predefined listener that is the Subclass of an EngineListener. It is used to display a specific element animation (in highlight) on a diagram in MagicDraw.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729103 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00006: Active, visited, and last visited elements

- page_id: `136729103`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729103/Active+visited+and+last+visited+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:51.110+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Animation
- labels: ['active-elements', 'visited-elements', 'last-visited-elements']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1441097052

1441097054

304843218

**On this page**

33

1441097053

Active Elements are the Elements a simulation session is focusing on (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding simulation sessions' space='CST2024x'] for more information). They can also be considered as the Elements that are currently being simulated in a simulation session. They will be annotated in red by default. Once an active Element has been simulated, it will become a visited Element and will be annotated in green by default. The last visited element will be annotated in orange.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
Element animation: active Element is red, last visited Element is orange, and visited Elements are green.

##### Animation of value propagation in the Internal Block diagram

Animation of Ports and Connectors in the Internal Block diagram (IBD) when value flows through them is annotated from activating the source part first, and then the Port, Connector, target Port, and target part, as shown in the figure below. Those Ports include regular Ports, flow Ports, and proxy Ports with flow properties. However, initialization will be at full speed and optimized without animation or delays. The value will also be shown as an annotation during the animation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Animation of value propagation in the Internal Block diagram.

1441097051

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding simulation sessions' space='CST2024x']

## PAGE 00007: Activity

- page_id: `136728752`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728752/Activity
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:37.361+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation > Elements simulation > Behavior
- labels: ['activity-simulation', 'simulating-activities']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1337480776

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1337480778

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1337480777

Whenever you run an Activity, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create a new session for the Activity simulation. If you run the Activity with its context, it will create another session under the session of the context object in the **Sessions** pane. If you click it, the object of the Activity will appear in the **Variables** pane. If there is an Activity diagram whose context is the simulating Activity, animation effect on such diagram (for non-silent simulation) will begin playing.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

When you use a read self action in an Activity, normally it returns an object, which is the context of the Activity. However, if the context object does not exist, it will return an Activity execution, which is the Activity's object.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Running an Activity.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Animation of fan Activity Simulation.

1337480775

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='CST2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729448 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00008: Activity duration simulation

- page_id: `136729448`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729448/Activity+duration+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:11.727+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['activity-duration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

663906223

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

663906226

663906222

**On this page**

**42**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

663906225

Cameo Simulation Toolkit can simulate the time required to implement an Activity as specified by the duration constraints. It calculates the total Activity duration by adding the simulation time of all visited Actions, and aids in accurately identifying which activities take the longest time to complete. You can see full details about the Activity duration for simulation, including the Activity names and the time between the start and finish of an Activity, in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation console' space='CST2024x'] by setting the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title="Console log's filter options" space='CST2024x'] to **Info**. An Activity can contain many Actions. You can specify a duration constraint on any Action in the Activity. The minimum and maximum duration of an Action can be provided through the **min** and **max** of the duration constraint. If you simulate the Activity with a specific duration mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will compute the total time spent when simulating the Activity for you.

When a Behavior's duration is empty or the time is unspecified, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use the duration of called Actions as the duration of the Behavior.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If a Behavior has a property or a structural feature value named **duration**, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will automatically assign the duration value to that particular feature value at runtime. The duration value property must be defined in the Activity, e.g., Change Color as shown in the following figure, or the parent of the duration value property. You can have only one duration value property in the parent Activity to avoid creating multiple duration value properties.

###### The duration value property is automatically assigned during simulation runtime.

##### Supported time units

Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports extensive Time Units (suffixes) in duration constraints, as well as Time Units of [Simulation Configuration](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/SimulationConfig+stereotype), accept time Event, and others. The following list summarizes these suffixes

- ns, nsec, nanosecond, nanoseconds
- µs, microsec, microsecond, microseconds
- ms, millisec, millisecond, milliseconds (by default)
- s, sec, second, seconds
- m, min, minute, minutes (All UI, e.g. Charts, CSVExport, etc., will be shown as "min" instead of "m" for meters.)
- h, hr, hrs, hour, hours
- d, day, days
- wk, week, weeks
- mo, month, months
- y, a, yr, year, years

You can parse the Time Unit specified as a suffix after a number or at the end of an expression string. However, floating numbers in duration constraints are not supported. Floating numbers are rounded to integers instead of automatically converted to smaller units. If you do not specify a suffix, the millisecond will be used by default. The examples are as follows

- 100m or 100min is considered 100 minutes.
- xs is considered the value of the property x in seconds.
- 200 is considered 200 milliseconds. NoteIf the Time Unit is less than 1 nanosecond, e.g. 0.5 nanosecond or 0.0005 microsecond, a warning message is displayed in the **Console** pane stating that the default value of 1 nanosecond is applied.Nanosecond and microsecond are supported only for the simulation clock and model based clock, but they are not supported for the built-in PC clock because the returned time of the PC clock using System.currentTimeMillis() is in millisecond. See also in [Simulation time and simulation clock](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Simulation+time+and+simulation+clock).

##### Duration simulation modes

Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports four duration simulation modes.

| Duration Simulation Mode | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Min | When you select min as the duration simulation mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will increase the time spent on an Activity by the min duration specified on the duration constraint when an element with an applied duration constraint is activated. |
| Max | If you select max as the duration simulation mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will increase the time spent on an Activity by the max duration specified on the duration constraint when an element with an applied duration constraint is activated. |
| Average | When Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates your model with the average duration simulation mode, it will use the average value between the max and min duration of the duration constraint as the duration of time spent on simulating an element with an applied duration constraint. |
| Random | The random mode allows Cameo Simulation Toolkit to obtain the duration of time spent on simulating an element with an applied duration constraint from a uniformly distributed random number between the min and max duration of the duration constraint. |

If you specify a duration simulation mode, the time spent on an Activity simulation will be calculated from the duration constraint applied to the elements of the Activity. The duration of time spent on simulating elements with no duration constraints will be zero. If you simulate a model without specifying any duration simulation mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will ignore those duration constraints and obtain the time spent on simulating the Actions and activities from the simulation clock.

The Duration Simulation modes (min, max, average, and random) are available in the model's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']. You can specify the duration constraints of the Activity element by entering the value in the element's **Specification** window.

##### Running an Activity simulation

The [DurationConstraint.mdzip](https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/20851829/DurationConstraint.mdzip?version=1&modificationDate=1505277500565&api=v2) sample is used as an example to explain how the Activity duration simulation works.

To specify the duration simulation mode and the duration constraints of an Activity, and run the Activity simulation

1. Either double-click or right-click a «[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='CST2024x']» (e.g., *winter*) on the diagram pane and select Specification to open the Specification window.
2. From the Duration Simulation Mode property, select a duration type (e.g., min). The selected duration mode will appear in the «SimulationConfig». [ATTACHMENT filename='Spec_durationAnalysis.png']
3. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='Close_button.png'] .
4. Open the rainy day scenario on the diagram pane and open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] of each element to specify the duration constraint for the selected duration simulation mode. In this example, double-click 'dig the snow' and specify the duration constraint value. [ATTACHMENT filename='DurationConstraint_analysis.png']
5. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='Close_button.png'] .
6. Right-click the «SimulationConfig» or click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the toolbar to run the simulation. View the simulation results of the Activity duration in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Console pane' space='CST2024x']Simulation window. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Right-clicking the «SimulationConfig» on the diagram pane to select the Simulation menu to run the simulation of the «SimulationConfig».**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** The Run toolbar button triggers the command to run the simulation of the «SimulationConfig». [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Console tab in the Simulation window contains the simulation of Activity duration of the DurationConstraint.mdzip sample from start to finish.

If you simulate a call Behavior Action having a duration constraint with a duration simulation mode and the time spent on the called Behavior is beyond the range of the duration constraint specified on the call Behavior Action, the call Behavior Action will be considered as a broken constraint element. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will then pause the model simulation at the call Behavior Action.

##### Duration constraints on Activities

Cameo Simulation Toolkit also supports simulation when you define a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration constraint' space='CST2024x'] for an Activity itself apart from a Call Behavior Action. The duration constraint on the Activity is interpreted the same as the duration constraint on the Call Behavior Action. The duration constraint can be shown in the Timeline chart or on the **Console** pane. In the following figure, the duration constraint is shown on the **Console** pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']A duration constraint is applied on the Activity as shown on the Console pane.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If the duration constraints are defined on both Activity and Call Behavior Action, the constraints of the Call Behavior Action will be used, and the constraints of the Activity will be ignored.

##### Glossary

| Concept | Description |
| --- | --- |
|  | The duration simulation mode specifies the duration to run the simulation of the elements with applied duration constraints. |
|  | The duration constraint is an interval constraint that refers to a duration interval. Use the duration interval to determine whether the constraint is satisfied. |
|  | The call Behavior Action is a call Action that invokes a Behavior directly rather than invoking an operation that invokes the Behavior. |

##### Sample model

[ATTACHMENT filename='DurationConstraint.mdzip']

663988616

**Related pages**

- Creating a model for Activity simulation
- Duration analysis
- Duration constraint
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']

If you simulate the Activity with a specific duration mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will compute the total time spent when simulating the Activity for you.</span></p><p><span>When a Behavior's duration is empty or the time is unspecified, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use the duration of called Actions as the duration of the Behavior.</span></p><p><span><br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d85f8dd-7ae9-4b0b-abe3-1be4f2c23c6b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>If a Behavior has a property or a structural feature value named <strong>duration</strong>, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will automatically assign the duration value to that particular feature value at runtime. You can have only one duration value property in the parent Activity to avoid creating multiple duration value properties.</span></p><p><span><br /></span></p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Duration Property Value.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><h6 style="text-align: center;">The duration value property is automatically assigned during simulation runtime.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Supported time units</h3><p class="Textbody">Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports extensive Time Units (suffixes) in duration constraints, as well as Time Units of <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/SimulationConfig+stereotype">Simulation Configuration</a>, accept time Event, and others. clock</a>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul><h3>Duration simulation modes</h3><p><span>Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports four duration simulation modes. </span></p><p><br /></p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 100.0%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 18.0589%;" /><col style="width: 81.9411%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Duration Simulation Mode</span></th><th><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Description</span></th></tr><tr><td><span><strong>Min</strong> </span></td><td><span>When you select min as the duration simulation mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will increase the time spent on an Activity by the min duration specified on the duration constraint when an element with an applied duration constraint is activated.</span></td></tr><tr><td><span><strong>Max</strong></span></td><td><span>If you select max as the duration simulation mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will increase the time spent on an Activity by the max duration specified on the duration constraint when an element with an applied duration constraint is activated. </span></td></tr><tr><td><span><strong>Average</strong> </span></td><td><span>When Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates your model with the average duration simulation mode, it will use the average value between the max and min duration of the duration constraint as the duration of time spent on simulating an element with an applied duration constraint. </span></td></tr><tr><td><span><strong>Random</strong> </span></td><td><span>The random mode allows Cameo Simulation Toolkit to obtain the duration of time spent on simulating an element with an applied duration constraint from a uniformly distributed random number between the min and max duration of the duration constraint. </span></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><span>If you specify a duration simulation mode, the time spent on an Activity simulation will be calculated from the duration constraint applied to the elements of the Activity. The duration of time spent on simulating elements with no duration constraints will be zero. If you simulate a model without specifying any duration simulation mode, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will ignore those duration constraints and obtain the time spent on simulating the Actions and activities from the simulation clock.</span></p><p><span>The Duration Simulation modes (min, max, average, and random) are available in the model's <span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></span>. You can specify the duration constraints of the Activity element by entering the value in the element's <strong>Specification</strong> window.<br /></span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Running an Activity simulation</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span>The <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/20851829/DurationConstraint.mdzip?version=1&amp;modificationDate=1505277500565&amp;api=v2">DurationConstraint.mdzip</a> sample is used as an example to explain how the Activity duration simulation works.</span><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">To specify the duration simulation mode and the duration constraints of an Activity, and run the Activity simulation</span> </p><hr /><p><br /></p><ol><li><p>Either double-click or right-click a «<ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="SimulationConfig stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SimulationConfig]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>»  (e.g., <em>winter</em>) on the diagram pane and select Specification to open the Specification window.</p></li><li>From the <strong>Duration Simulation Mode</strong> property, select a duration type (e.g., min). The selected duration mode will appear in the «SimulationConfig».<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Spec_durationAnalysis.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Close_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li>Open the rainy day scenario on the diagram pane and open the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> of each element to specify the duration constraint for the selected duration simulation mode. In this example, double-click 'dig the snow' and specify the duration constraint value.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="DurationConstraint_analysis.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /></li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Close_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li><p>Right-click the «SimulationConfig» or click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Run_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the toolbar to run the simulation. View the simulation results of the Activity duration in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Console pane" /><ac:link-body><span class="inline-comment-marker">Simulation window</span></ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p><h6><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="RunSimulation.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6><span><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Right-clicking the «SimulationConfig» on the diagram pane to select the Simulation menu to run the simulation of the «SimulationConfig».<br /></span></span><span><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);"><strong><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="RunSimulation_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></span></span></h6><h6>The Run toolbar button triggers the command to run the simulation of the «SimulationConfig».</h6><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="DurationConstraint_simulation.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6>The Console tab in the Simulation window contains the simulation of Activity duration of the DurationConstraint.mdzip sample from start to finish.</h6></li></ol><p>If you simulate a call Behavior Action having a duration constraint with a duration simulation mode and the time spent on the called Behavior is beyond the range of the duration constraint specified on the call Behavior Action, the call Behavior Action will be considered as a broken constraint element. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will then pause the model simulation at the call Behavior Action.</p><h3>Duration constraints on Activities</h3><p>Cameo Simulation Toolkit also supports simulation when you define a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Duration constraint" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[duration constraint]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for an Activity itself apart from a Call Behavior Action. The duration constraint on the Activity is interpreted the same as the duration constraint on the Call Behavior Action. The duration constraint can be shown in the Timeline chart or on the <strong>Console</strong> pane. In the following figure, the duration constraint is shown on the <strong>Console</strong> pane.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Duration Constraints on Activities.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>A duration constraint is applied on the Activity as shown on the Console pane.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f953627d-095d-4770-9d74-1822db7fcfa4"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the duration constraints are defined on both Activity and Call Behavior Action, the constraints of the Call Behavior Action will be used, and the constraints of the Activity will be ignored.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Glossary</span></h3><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Concept</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity" /></ac:link></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><span>The duration simulation mode specifies the duration to run the simulation of the elements with applied duration constraints.</span></p></div></td></tr><tr><td><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Duration constraint" /></ac:link></td><td><span>The duration constraint is an interval constraint that refers to a duration interval. Use the duration interval to determine whether the constraint is satisfied.</span></td></tr><tr><td><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Call Behavior Action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></td><td><span>The call Behavior Action is a call Action that invokes a Behavior directly rather than invoking an operation that invokes the Behavior.</span></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><h3>Sample model</h3><hr /><p><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="DurationConstraint.mdzip"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity duration simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0eaa96a2-8a65-4458-9548-0fa1aec20a23"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">663988616</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Creating+a+model+for+Activity+simulation">Creating a model for Activity simulation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Duration+analysis">Duration analysis</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Duration+constraint">Duration constraint</a></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" ri:space-key="MD2024x" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729474 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00009: Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior

- page_id: `136729474`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729474/Activity+Partition+execution+and+allocated+Behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:12.544+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['activity-partition-execution']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Activity Partition is an element in an Activity diagram. It sets the boundary of simulation of an execution. If a Partition represents a property or a Classifier, all Actions and Activity calls inside the Partition will be run using the context of the property or the Classifier, which is represented on the Partition.

If the Activity Partition represents a part/property, the object playing the Partition is the object playing a part/ property that is represented by the Partition. If the Partition represents an InstanceSpecification, the object which is initialized from such instance specification shall be the object playing the Partition (if there is no object initialized from such instance specification yet, Cameo Simulation Toolkit shall initialized when the Behavior execution of the Activity is initialized.

When multiple objects are playing an Activity Partition, only the first object that is not busy shall be the one that performs the Action in the Partition and it shall be the context of the Behavior execution invoked by the Action. This behavior can be changed by setting the **Allow Concurrent Allocated Activities** project option or Simulation Configuration property to *true*. When this property is set to *true*, allocated activities are executed simultaneously.

The following figure shows an example of the **(Activity)** **system** diagram on which a call Behavior Action is placed within an Activity Partition. The context of the Activity Partition is the runtime objects of workers. When the **system** model was run, the call Behavior Action was executed in the context of the Activity Partition: **workers**. But since there were more than one worker, the simulation searched for a worker that was not busy to perform the Behavior. If all the workers were busy, the simulation would wait until one of them was available. If the call Behavior Action were outside the Activity Partition, the context of the Behavior execution would be the **system** itself. The same also goes for an opaque Action. If it is in the Activity Partition, the simulation will run the opaque Action using the context of the property or the Classifier represented on the Partition.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Activity Partition simulation.

When a call Behavior Action is in an Activity Partition, the following conditions apply

- If the Behavior of the Action belongs to a Classifier of the object playing the Partition (or super type of such Classifier), the context of the Behavior execution shall be the object playing the Partition.
- If the Behavior does not belong to the Classifier of the object playing the Partition, but it belongs to the Classifier of the object that is the context of the Activity containing the Action (or super type of such Classifier), the Behavior execution shall be invoked in the context object of the Activity containing the Action.
- For other cases that cannot be categorized to either (i) or (ii), for example, the Behavior is an OpaqueBehavior or an OpaqueFunction, the context of the Behavior execution shall be the object playing the Partition.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Activity Partition is an element in an Activity diagram. It sets the boundary of simulation of an execution. If a Partition represents a property or a Classifier, all Actions and Activity calls inside the Partition will be run using the context of the property or the Classifier, which is represented on the Partition. </p><p>If the Activity Partition represents a part/property, the object playing the Partition is the object playing a part/ property that is represented by the Partition. If the Partition represents an InstanceSpecification, the object which is initialized from such instance specification shall be the object playing the Partition (if there is no object initialized from such instance specification yet, Cameo Simulation Toolkit shall initialized when the Behavior execution of the Activity is initialized. </p><p>When multiple objects are playing an Activity Partition, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="46622b15-c022-4b26-874d-e1241fe4792a">only the first object that is not busy shall be the one that performs</ac:inline-comment-marker> the Action in the Partition and it shall be the context of the Behavior execution invoked by the Action. This behavior can be changed by setting the <strong>Allow Concurrent Allocated Activities</strong> project option or Simulation Configuration property to <em>true</em>. When this property is set to <em>true</em>, allocated activities are executed simultaneously.</p><p>The following figure shows an example of the <strong>(Activity)</strong> <strong>system</strong> diagram on which a call Behavior Action is placed within an Activity Partition. The context of the Activity Partition is the runtime objects of workers. When the <strong>system</strong> model was run, the call Behavior Action was executed in the context of the Activity Partition: <strong>workers</strong>. But since there were more than one worker, the simulation searched for a worker that was not busy to perform the Behavior. If all the workers were busy, the simulation would wait until one of them was available. If the call Behavior Action were outside the Activity Partition, the context of the Behavior execution would be the <strong>system</strong> itself. The same also goes for an opaque Action. If it is in the Activity Partition, the simulation will run the opaque Action using the context of the property or the Classifier represented on the Partition. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Activity Partition Simulation" ac:alt="Activity Partition Simulation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SwimlaneExec_new.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Activity Partition simulation.</h6><p>When a call Behavior Action is in an Activity Partition, the following conditions apply</p><ul><li>If the Behavior of the Action belongs to a Classifier of the object playing the Partition (or super type of such Classifier), the context of the Behavior execution shall be the object playing the Partition.</li><li>If the Behavior does not belong to the Classifier of the object playing the Partition, but it belongs to the Classifier of the object that is the context of the Activity containing the Action (or super type of such Classifier), the Behavior execution shall be invoked in the context object of the Activity containing the Action.</li><li>For other cases that cannot be categorized to either (i) or (ii), for example, the Behavior is an OpaqueBehavior or an OpaqueFunction, the context of the Behavior execution shall be the object playing the Partition. </li></ul><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729384 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00010: Activity simulation

- page_id: `136729384`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729384/Activity+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:10.555+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide
- labels: ['activity-simulation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

128423460

128423462

128423461

Activity simulations essentially require the Activity simulation engine in Cameo Simulation Toolkit to perform all Activity-based simulations. The details are outlined in the following topics:

- Activity simulation engine Running Activity Simulation in Activity diagrams or Activity elements.
- Creating a model for Activity simulation Simulating Classifiers defined by Activities.
- Executing Activities Suspending the simulation with breakpoints.
- Activity duration simulation Calculating and analyzing Activity duration and specifying duration constraints.
- Duration analysis Directing target objects to the callOperationAction.
- Running a Call Action simulation without a target pin Simulating a CallOperationAction that does not have a target pin to select a runtime object as a target.
- Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior Using Activity Partition as an element in the Activity diagram to set the boundary of simulation of an execution.
- Execution of incomplete/dummy models Executing incomplete and dummy models with Behaviors of Actions when input or output pins or any properties of Actions are not specified or present.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using utility functions of Simulation' space='CST2024x']Using utility functions of Simulation Facilitating common tasks through Opaque Behaviors under SimulationProfile::library::utils .
