<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729647 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00026: Automatic and manual initialization of objects/values

- page_id: `136729647`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729647/Automatic+and+manual+initialization+of+objects+values
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:20.791+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['initializing-objects', 'initializing-values']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Solve After Initialization** option allows modelers to control initialization of objects and values automatically or manually from the initial parametric constraints solving. This flexibility improves the parametric debugging experience. You can select the option through the Specification window of the Simulation Config, or by clicking **Options** >**Project** > **Simulation** > **Solve After Initialization** as shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Solve After Initialization option can initialize objects and values automatically.

The value of the **Solve After Initialization** option is **true** by default to enable automatic initial solving. If you wish to manually invoke initial solving, you can do so by clearing the check box (the value will change to **false**), clicking **Refresh** in the **Variables** pane, or pressing **Start** (**F8**).

To invoke initial parametric solving manually:

1. Open/create a project with a parametric diagram.
2. In the Simulation Config, set the “Solve After Initialization” and “Auto Start” option to **false** (see the following figure). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Run simulation via the Simulation Config. All related results will not be calculated automatically, so you need to manually invoke it via the Refresh in Variables pane or Start (F8) (see the following figure). [ATTACHMENT filename='3.PNG']
4. Select true for the Solve After Initialization option, and run the Simulation Config. All related results will be calculated automatically (see the following figure) [ATTACHMENT filename='4.PNG']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The <strong>Solve After Initialization</strong> option allows modelers to control initialization of objects and values automatically or manually from the initial parametric constraints solving. This flexibility improves the parametric debugging experience. You can select the option through the Specification window of the Simulation Config, or by clicking <strong>Options</strong> &gt;<strong> Project</strong> &gt; <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Solve After Initialization</strong> as shown in the following figure.</p><p class="Standard"> </p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Automatic or manually initializing objects using the Solve After Initialization option" ac:alt="Automatic or manually initializing objects using the Solve After Initialization option"><ri:attachment ri:filename="1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic and manual initialization of objects/values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">The Solve After Initialization option can initialize objects and values automatically.</h6><p class="Standard"> </p><p class="Standard">The value of the <strong>Solve After Initialization</strong> option is <strong>true</strong> by default to enable automatic initial solving. If you wish to manually invoke initial solving, you can do so by clearing the check box (the value will change to <strong>false</strong>), clicking <strong>Refresh</strong> in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, or pressing <strong>Start</strong> (<strong>F8</strong>).</p><p class="Standard"> </p><p>To invoke initial parametric solving manually:</p><hr /><ol><li>Open/create a project with a parametric diagram.</li><li><p>In the Simulation Config, set the “Solve After Initialization” and “Auto Start” option to <strong>false</strong> (see the following figure).</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting false to manually invoke objects or values." ac:alt="Selecting false to manually invoke objects or values."><ri:attachment ri:filename="2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic and manual initialization of objects/values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Run simulation via the Simulation Config. All related results will not be calculated automatically, so you need to manually invoke it via the Refresh in Variables pane or Start (F8) (see the following figure).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic and manual initialization of objects/values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong>true</strong> for the <strong>Solve After Initialization</strong> option, and run the Simulation Config. All related results will be calculated automatically (see the following figure)<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Auto initialization of objects/value calculate results automatically."><ri:attachment ri:filename="4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic and manual initialization of objects/values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729247 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00027: Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects

- page_id: `136729247`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729247/Automatic+initialization+of+context+and+runtime+objects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:01.499+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['context-initialization', 'runtime-object-initialization']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

41098601

41098603

41098600

**On this page**

22

41098602

Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of initializing both context and runtime objects automatically even if a simulation context does not exist, and no object is configured to initiate the runtime objects.

#### Context initialization

When executing a Behavior, Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to simulate its context as well.

To simulate a Behavior with its context

- Right-click a Behavior or a Behavior's diagram and select Simulation > Run with Context to execute it together with its context.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To simulate a Behavior without its context

- Right-click a Behavior or a Behavior's diagram and select Simulation > Run to execute it without its context.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

When you simulate a Behavior with its context, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize the context of the selected Behavior and simulate the Classifier Behavior. During the Classifier Behavior simulation, if the runtime object of the context Classifier is stable, the selected Behavior will be simulated.

If a selected Behavior has no context, the **Run** **with** **Context** menu will be disabled.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Running a model simulation using the shortcut menu.

#### Runtime object initialization

When you use an **InstanceSpecification** to initialize a runtime object, you will also need to use the corresponding slot values of such **InstanceSpecification** to initialize the feature values of the runtime object. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use the default value of the corresponding feature (property) of each slot if the slot value is empty.

The value of each feature (property) will be automatically initialized only if its **Lower-value** **Multiplicity** is more than zero or is undefined. Otherwise (the feature's **Lower-value** **Multiplicity** is zero), the feature value will be empty (nothing is initialized). However, characteristic of the initialization depends on the type of the feature. The following sections explain the types of feature that can influence initialization characteristics

##### Structural feature typed by primitive datatype

The default value of a structural feature, which is typed by a primitive datatype, is its initialized value.This default initialization value, which can be set from **Options** **>** **Project** **>****Simulation** as shown in following figure. When true and value property has no default value, the value will be initialized to 0 value. The default value is **false**.

The datatypes which are affected by this option are listed in the table as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation Project Options - Initialize Empty Values to 0.

This initialization value applies to numerical datatypes only. For other types, see the table for values used for automatic initialization of the Structural Feature typed by primitive datatype as follows.**

| Primitive DataType (QualifiedName) | Value |
| --- | --- |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::BooleanPrimitiveValueTypes::Boolean (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::boolean | false |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::IntegerUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::UnlimitedNaturalPrimitiveValueTypes::Integer (SysML Profile.mdzip)PrimitiveValueTypes::UnlimitedNatural (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::intUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::longUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::short | All numerical data types pick up their default initialization value from the Project Options as shown in the above figure. |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::RealPrimitiveValueTypes::Real (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::doubleUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::float | All numerical data types pick up their default initialization value from the Project Options as shown in the above figure. |
| Enumeration | • UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::Real |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::StringPrimitiveValueTypes::String (SysML Profile.mdzip) | "" (Empty String) |

##### Structural feature typed by non-datatype

If the type of a structural feature is not a datatype, the conditions are applied as follows

- If the structural feature has a non-empty slot value, it will be initialized with such value.
- Otherwise, if the structural feature's default value is specified, it will be initialized with the default value.

If the structural feature also has internal feature(s), for each internal feature, the conditions are applied as follows

- If the internal feature has a non-empty slot value, it will be initialized with such value.
- Otherwise, if the internal feature's default value is specified, it will be initialized with the default value.

If an internal feature also has its own internal feature(s), the same rule above will be applied recursively.

##### Structural feature typed by user-defined eatatype

For a structural feature typed by a user-defined datatype, the conditions are applied as follows

- If the Datatype has internal structural feature(s), the value of the structural feature will be initialized as described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects' space='CST2024x'] .
- If the Datatype has no internal structural feature, the following conditions are applied
  - The value of the structural feature will be initialized as described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects' space='CST2024x'] if the Datatype is inherited from a primitive datatype.
  - The initialized value of the structural feature will be its default value if the Datatype is not inherited from any primitive datatype. If the default value of the feature is, however, not specified, the initialized value will be empty.

42669394

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Classifier Behavior property' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manipulating structural values' space='CST2024x']

up their default initialization value from the Project Options as shown in the above figure.</td></tr><tr><td><ul><li>UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::<strong>Real</strong></li><li>PrimitiveValueTypes::<strong>Real</strong> (SysML Profile.mdzip)</li><li>UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::<strong>double</strong></li><li>UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::<strong>float</strong></li></ul></td><td>All numerical data types pick up their default initialization value from the Project Options as shown in the above figure.</td></tr><tr><td><ul><li><strong>Enumeration</strong></li></ul></td><td><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::<strong>Real</strong></td></tr><tr><td><ul><li>UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::<strong>String</strong></li><li>PrimitiveValueTypes::<strong>String</strong> (SysML Profile.mdzip)</li></ul></td><td>&quot;&quot; (Empty String)</td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Structural feature typed by non-datatype</h3><p>If the type of a structural feature is not a datatype, the conditions are applied as follows</p><ul><li>If the structural feature has a non-empty slot value, it will be initialized with such value. </li><li><span style="line-height: 1.42857;">Otherwise, if the structural feature's default value is specified, it will be initialized with the default value. </span></li></ul><p>If the structural feature also has internal feature(s), for each internal feature, the conditions are applied as follows</p><ul><li>If the internal feature has a non-empty slot value, it will be initialized with such value. </li><li><span style="line-height: 1.42857;">Otherwise, if the internal feature's default value is specified, it will be initialized with the default value. </span></li></ul><p>If an internal feature also has its own internal feature(s), the same rule above will be applied recursively. </p><h3>Structural feature typed by user-defined eatatype</h3><p>For a structural feature typed by a user-defined datatype, the conditions are applied as follows</p><ul><li>If the Datatype has internal structural feature(s), the value of the structural feature will be initialized as described in <ac:link ac:anchor="StructuralfeatureTypedbyNon-Datatype"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Structural feature typed by non-datatype]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects" /></ac:link>.</li><li><span style="line-height: 1.42857;">If the Datatype has no internal structural feature, the following conditions are applied <br /></span><ul><li>The value of the structural feature will be initialized as described in <ac:link ac:anchor="Structuralfeaturetypedbyprimitivedatatype"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Structural feature typed by primitive datatype]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects" /></ac:link> if the Datatype is inherited from a primitive datatype.</li><li>The initialized value of the structural feature will be its default value if the Datatype is not inherited from any primitive datatype. If the default value of the feature is, however, not specified, the initialized value will be empty. </li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0975e1f2-410a-4d72-a793-fe6f19405461"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">42669394</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Getting a structural feature value"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Manipulating structural values" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Getting a structural feature value]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728836 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00028: Automatic start of active objects

- page_id: `136728836`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728836/Automatic+start+of+active+objects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:40.483+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['automatic-start-of-active-objects', 'active-objects']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An active object is an object whose Classifier is an active Class, a Class element with isActive = true. When you run a Simulation Configuration with **autoStartActiveObjects** = true, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will start the Classifier Behavior of the active Class right after the object is instantiated. The following figure shows the stereo system's Simulation Configuration where the value of **autoStartActiveObjects** is true. In this example, the Behavior of the active objects (Speaker, Headphone, and Player) will start automatically once the object starts.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Simulation Configuration.

If the value of **autoStartActiveObjects** is false, or if the Classes or blocks are not active Classes, you have to start each object using a **startObjectBehavior** Action. The first figure below shows the **System init** Activity, which is the Classifier Behavior of a stereo system block. This Behavior uses the **startObjectBehavior** Actions to start the Behaviors of objects, which are the internal parts of the stereo system object (big speakers, small speakers, and dvd player). You can simplify the **System init** activity, as shown in the second figure below, by making the blocks typing the big speakers, small speakers, and dvd player, to be active blocks then you can simulate the stereo system through a Simulation Configuration with autoStartActiveObjects = true.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### System init Activity to start the Behavior of runtime objects using startObjectBehavior Actions. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Simplified System init Activity to be used with autoStartActiveObjects whose value is true.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An active object is an object whose Classifier is an active Class, a Class element with isActive = true. When you run a Simulation Configuration with <strong>autoStartActiveObjects</strong> = true, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will start the Classifier Behavior of the active Class right after the object is instantiated. The following figure shows the stereo system's Simulation Configuration where the value of <strong>autoStartActiveObjects</strong> is true. In this example, the Behavior of the active objects (Speaker, Headphone, and Player) will start automatically once the object starts. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AutoStartActiveObjects.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic start of active objects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Simulation Configuration.</h6><p style="text-align: left;">If the value of <strong>autoStartActiveObjects</strong> is false, or if the Classes or blocks are not active Classes, you have to start each object using a <strong>startObjectBehavior</strong> Action. The first figure below shows the <strong>System init</strong> Activity, which is the Classifier Behavior of a stereo system block. This Behavior uses the <strong>startObjectBehavior</strong> Actions to start the Behaviors of objects, which are the internal parts of the stereo system object (big speakers, small speakers, and dvd player). You can simplify the <strong>System init</strong> activity, as shown in the second figure below, by making the blocks typing the big speakers, small speakers, and dvd player, to be active blocks then you can simulate the stereo system through a Simulation Configuration with autoStartActiveObjects = true. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:title="System init Activity to Start the Behavior of Runtime Objects Using startObjectBehavior Actions" ac:alt="System init Activity to Start the Behavior of Runtime Objects Using startObjectBehavior Actions"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AutoStartActiveObjects3.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic start of active objects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </p><h6 style="text-align: center;">System init Activity to start the Behavior of runtime objects using startObjectBehavior Actions.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="A Simplified System init Activity to be Used with autoStartActiveObjects Whose Value is True" ac:alt="A Simplified System init Activity to be Used with autoStartActiveObjects Whose Value is True"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AutoStartActiveObjects2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic start of active objects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;"> A Simplified System init Activity to be used with autoStartActiveObjects whose value is true.</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728742 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00029: Behavior

- page_id: `136728742`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728742/Behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:37.072+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation > Elements simulation
- labels: ['behavior-simulation', 'simulating-behaviors']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1336333573

1336333575

1336333572

**On this page**

1336333574

You can simulate a Behavior (Activity, State-Machine, or Interaction) either with or without its context Classifier by simply right-clicking the Behavior in the browser or on a diagram and select either **Simulation** > **Run** or **Simulation** > **Run** **with** **Context**. Here, the classifier is the context of the Behavior.

You can also set the Behavior as a target of Simulation Configuration (See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space='CST2024x'] for more details) as follows

- Running a Behavior with its context Classifier If you run the Behavior with its context Classifier, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize the object of the Classifier, which is the Behavior's context, to be simulated. And it will simulate the Behavior on the initialized object. If the Classifier has a Classifier Behavior, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will start the Classifier Behavior of the context and wait until the object is in the idle state (waiting for a signal) to start the Behavior.
- Running a Behavior without a context Classifier If you run the Behavior without its context, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create an object of the Behavior and simulate it directly. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will also initialize values for the properties and parameters of the simulating Behavior. You can modify the values through the Variables pane before starting the simulation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The simulation of the Behavior with its properties and parameters.

To simulate a Behavior from an Activity, a State Machine diagram, or an Interaction directly, do either of the following

- Open the diagram and click Start on the Simulation window toolbar
- Right-click the diagram and select Simulation > Run .

The simulation of the Behavior, which is the context of the diagram, will run.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729334 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00030: Behaviors on Entry, Exit, and Do Activities of a State

- page_id: `136729334`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729334/Behaviors+on+Entry+Exit+and+Do+Activities+of+a+State
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:09.551+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation > Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['activity-state-behaviors', 'behaviors-of-activity-states']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

117294362

117294364

117294363

States can have defined Behaviors for any Activities: Entry, Exit, or Do Activity. Cameo Simulation Toolkit creates a new simulation session to simulate those defined Behaviors. A defined Behavior can be an Activity, a State Machine, an Interaction, or an OpaqueBehavior. The simulation engine corresponding to a defined Behavior will be used to simulate a model. If the defined Behavior is OpaqueBehavior, the ScriptEngine will be used to simulate the code in the body of OpaqueBehavior.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Behavior simulation on the entry of a State in the StopWatch.mdzip sample project.

According to Precise Semantics of UML State Machines (PSSM) Compatible CallEvent arguments handling, the Caller is suspended until the running to the completion step is finished in case of the Sync Call. The running to the completion step ends after the Entry or Do Activity is done. The input parameter must match the argument of the Entry or Do Activity. If it has an output parameter, it will be redefined until the running to the completion step is over. Therefore, the last output parameter is returned to the Caller.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Caller is suspended until the running to the completion step is completed according to PSSM Compatible CallEvent arguments handling.

117294361

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opaque Behavior' space='MD2024x']

The simulation engine corresponding to a defined Behavior will be used to simulate a model. If the defined Behavior is OpaqueBehavior, the ScriptEngine will be used to simulate the code in the body of OpaqueBehavior. </p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="The Behavior Simulation on the Entry of a State (See Sample Project - StopWatch.mdzip)" ac:alt="The Behavior Simulation on the Entry of a State (See Sample Project - StopWatch.mdzip)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure 66.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Behaviors on Entry, Exit, and Do Activities of a State" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Behavior simulation on the entry of a State in the StopWatch.mdzip sample project.</h6><p>According to Precise Semantics of UML State Machines (PSSM) Compatible CallEvent arguments handling, the Caller is suspended until the running to the completion step is finished in case of the Sync Call. The running to the completion step ends after the Entry or Do Activity is done. The input parameter must match the argument of the Entry or Do Activity. If it has an output parameter, it will be redefined until the running to the completion step is over. Therefore, the last output parameter is returned to the Caller.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Behaviors on Entry, Exit, and Do Activity activities of a state.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Behaviors on Entry, Exit, and Do Activities of a State" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Caller is suspended until the running to the completion step is completed according to PSSM Compatible CallEvent arguments handling.</h6><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95f593ec-0ae2-4f16-b116-088314a5efd6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">117294361</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Opaque Behavior" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729806 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00031: Binding Connector

- page_id: `136729806`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729806/Binding+Connector
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:32.034+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['binding-connector']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A binding Connector is a Connector to which a stereotype «BindingConnector» is applied. According to the SysML specification, a binding Connector specifies that properties at both ends of a Connector have equal values. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value binding' space='CST2024x'] for more information about value binding with a binding Connector.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A binding Connector is a Connector to which a stereotype «BindingConnector» is applied. According to the SysML specification, a binding Connector specifies that properties at both ends of a Connector have equal values. See <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Value binding" /></ac:link> for more information about value binding with a binding Connector. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729658 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00032: Binding in a complex aggregate structure

- page_id: `136729658`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729658/Binding+in+a+complex+aggregate+structure
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:21.565+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Value binding
- labels: ['binding-in-aggregate-structure']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A binding connector connecting deep nested properties in a block must apply a stereotype «NestedConnectorEnd» at both ends and specify the propertyPath (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Nested Connector end' space='CST2024x'] for more information about the nested connector end). If there are multiple values specify a property in the propertyPath (the upper bound of the multiplicity of the property is greater than 1 or is infinite), Cameo Simulation Toolkit will construct a value list at each end of the connector and maintain the values in the list. You can use (IsOrdered = true) to order each property in the propertyPath to ensure that the order of values on the list remains the same.

The following figure illustrates an example of binding where the aggregate structure is complex. It shows an executable InstanceSpecification of the block **System**, which is **system:System**. It has three instances of subsystems as the values of the slot **subsystem:Subsystem[0..**]**. In the SysML Parametric diagram, the value property *value** of the slot **subsystem:Subsystem[0..**]* are bound to the value property **value** of the block **System**. So, the values of the value property **value** of object **System** will be [25, 50, 75] respectively.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Value Binding in a Complex Aggregation Structure.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A binding connector connecting deep nested properties in a block must apply a stereotype «NestedConnectorEnd» at both ends and specify the propertyPath (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Nested Connector end" /></ac:link> for more information about the nested connector end). If there are multiple values specify a property in the propertyPath (the upper bound of the multiplicity of the property is greater than 1 or is infinite), Cameo Simulation Toolkit will construct a value list at each end of the connector and maintain the values in the list. You can use (IsOrdered = true) to order each property in the propertyPath to ensure that the order of values on the list remains the same. </p><p>The following figure illustrates an example of binding where the aggregate structure is complex. It shows an executable InstanceSpecification of the block <strong>System</strong>, which is <strong>system:System</strong>. It has three instances of subsystems as the values of the slot <strong>subsystem:Subsystem[0..</strong>]<strong>. In the SysML Parametric diagram, the value property *value</strong> of the slot <strong>subsystem:Subsystem[0..</strong>]* are bound to the value property <strong>value</strong> of the block <strong>System</strong>. So, the values of the value property <strong>value</strong> of object <strong>System</strong> will be [25, 50, 75] respectively. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Value Binding in a Complex Aggregation Structure" ac:alt="Value Binding in a Complex Aggregation Structure"><ri:attachment ri:filename="complexAggre_binding.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Binding in a complex aggregate structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Value Binding in a Complex Aggregation Structure.</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729807 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00033: Block

- page_id: `136729807`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729807/Block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:32.219+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['block']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A SysML block is a UML Class stereotyped with «Block». Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates a SysML block the same way it simulates a UML Class.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A SysML block is a UML Class stereotyped with «Block». Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates a SysML block the same way it simulates a UML Class. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729813 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00034: BoundReference

- page_id: `136729813`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729813/BoundReference
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:32.830+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['bound-reference']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

According to the SysML specification, a bound reference must have a binding connector to a property or a nested property of an owning block. Therefore, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the value of the bound reference so that it works with the properties connected with the binding connector. In exceptional circumstances, for example, if a Classifier that types a bound reference is a subtype of a Classifier that types a property at the other end of a binding connector, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use the Classifier that types the bound reference to initialize the object. You can see more information about binding connector simulation in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Binding Connector' space='CST2024x'].

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Executing a Block Vehicle that contains Bound references.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>According to the SysML specification, a bound reference must have a binding connector to a property or a nested property of an owning block. Therefore, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the value of the bound reference so that it works with the properties connected with the binding connector. In exceptional circumstances, for example, if a Classifier that types a bound reference is a subtype of a Classifier that types a property at the other end of a binding connector, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use the Classifier that types the bound reference to initialize the object. You can see more information about binding connector simulation in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Binding Connector" /></ac:link>. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Executing a Block Vehicle that Contains Bound References" ac:alt="Executing a Block Vehicle that Contains Bound References"><ri:attachment ri:filename="execBlockwithBoundRef.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="BoundReference" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Executing a Block Vehicle that contains Bound references.</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729299 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00035: Breakpoints

- page_id: `136729299`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729299/Breakpoints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:05.701+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging
- labels: ['breakpoints']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

53874495

53874498

53874496

Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to add or remove breakpoints to or from model elements. A model simulation will pause when these model elements are activated during the simulation. You can open the **Breakpoints** pane to see and manage all of the existing breakpoints in an active project. The **Breakpoints** pane lists all breakpoints with their properties shown in separate columns.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Breakpoints pane.

The Breakpoints pane columns are listed in the table as follows

| Column | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Enabled | To display the enabled or disabled state of a breakpoint. If the value is true, the breakpoint is enabled. Otherwise, the breakpoint is disabled. A model simulation will be suspended at that particular breakpoint only when the breakpoint is enabled (true). |
| Element | To represent a model element to which each breakpoint is applied. The model simulation will be suspended when the symbol of the element is activated or deactivated (depending on the value in the Suspend column). |
| Condition | To represent a breakpoint condition, a boolean expression, that will be evaluated when a model simulation reaches the element to which a breakpoint is applied. The simulation will be suspended at that particular element or breakpoint when the result of the boolean expression is true. If the condition is not defined, the simulation will always be suspended when it reaches that particular breakpoint. |
| Suspend | To suspend a model simulation. There are three kinds of simulation suspensions: On Entry, On Exit, and Both.On Entry to suspend simulation when a breakpoint's element is activated.On Exit to suspend simulation when a breakpoint's element is deactivated.Both to suspend simulation once a breakpoint's element is either activated or deactivated. |

53900734

**Related pages**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728824 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00036: Built-in clock

- page_id: `136728824`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728824/Built-in+clock
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:39.871+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Simulation time and simulation clock
- labels: ['built-in-clock']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1349015512

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1349015514

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1349015513

The built-in clock generates simulation time continuously during a model simulation. You can use the clock ratio option in Cameo Simulation Toolkit to increase or decrease the amount of simulation time. The clock ratio specifies the ratio between actual time and simulation time in the same interval. You can set a ratio of the built-in clock for model simulation. The value you enter for the clock ratio tag definition of a Simulation Config allows you to scale a ratio up or down (see the following figure). For further information about stereotypes of the simulation configuration, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='CST2024x'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation Configuration with the specified clock ratio.

For example, if a clock ratio is 10, it means that 10 seconds current time is equivalent to one-second simulation time in the built-in clock. Therefore, the simulation clock is 10 times slower than the actual clock.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- The current version of Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to pause or resume the built-in clock.
- When you pause running simulation by either clicking the Suspend button or using breakpoints, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will stop the built-in clock operation and all running sessions temporarily. You can continue all of the activities by clicking the Resume button.

1349015511

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Internal simulation clock' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model-based clock' space='CST2024x']

## PAGE 00037: Built-in Math

- page_id: `136729717`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729717/Built-in+Math
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:25.600+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['built-in-math-solver']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

468817904

468817906

468817905

Cameo Simulation Toolkit comes loaded with a built-in Math Solver. As a default Parametric Evaluator, Math Solver can solve simple mathematical and logical expressions. You can use it to evaluate as follows

- Mathematical and logical expressions defined in the Constraints of Constraint Blocks for Parametric Simulation on a SysML Parametric diagram.
- Mathematical and logical expressions in Simulation Console.

See also:

468817903

## PAGE 00038: CallEvent

- page_id: `136729529`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729529/CallEvent
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:15.324+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation > Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['call-event']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

380827074

380827076

380827075

A CallEvent is a type of Event in UML elements supported by Cameo Simulation Toolkit. A CallEvent of an Operation is triggered when you call an Operation with****a specified****Method. If an Operation is called without a Method, the CallEvent**will**be automatically generated. You can use the CallEvent on AcceptEventActions and Transitions**as shown in the following figures.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A CallEvent on the AcceptEventActions of Actions.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A CallEvent on the Transitions of States.

380827073

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Accept Event Action' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MD2024x']

##### Calling a specific behavior

You can use the following APIs to call any Behavior by its name.

```text
arguments)
object callBehavior(String name, List> arguments, boolean isSynchronous)]]>
```

The following example shows how to call the "test" specified Behavior in ALH API

```text

```

You can also give initial parameter values in which the arguments lists must be created.

```text

```

If a found Behavior value is null or an argument's size is not equal to the in or inout parameter's size of the found Behavior, an IllegalArgumentException will be thrown.

##### Calling a specific operation

You can use the following APIs to call an operation

```text
arguments)
object callOperation(Object_ object, String name)
object callOperation(Object_ object, String name, List> arguments)
object callOperation(Object_ object, String name, List> arguments, boolean isSynchronous)
]]>
```

If the object is omitted then context (self) is used automatically.

The following example shows how to call a specified operation in ALH API by passing the operation's name

```text

```

You can also give initial parameter values in which the arguments lists must be created by executing the following code

```text

```

If a found Behavior value is null or an argument's size is not equal to the in or inout parameter's size of the found Behavior, an IllegalArgumentException will be thrown.

````html
object callOperation(Object_ object, String name, List<?> arguments)
object callOperation(Object_ object, String name, List<?> arguments, boolean isSynchronous)
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If the object is omitted then context (self) is used automatically.</p><p>The following example shows how to call a specified operation in ALH API by passing the operation's name</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="05a74076-308d-44aa-8e05-80b3ab61a463"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[value = ALH.callOperation("test"); ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>You can also give initial parameter values in which the arguments lists must be created by executing the following code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac9588b3-ae3d-4235-816f-dbb40dec694d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[arguments = ALH.createList();
arguments.add("value1");
arguments.add("value2");
value = ALH.callOperation("test", arguments);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If a found Behavior value is null or an argument's size is not equal to the in or inout parameter's size of the found Behavior, an IllegalArgumentException will be thrown.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728661 space=CST2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00040: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation

- page_id: `136728661`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728661/Cameo+Simulation+Toolkit+Documentation
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-01-26T14:01:38.340+01:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

859975751

859975771

859975761

INLINE

Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides the first in the industry extendable model execution framework based on OMG fUML and W3C SCXML standards. It extends UPDM plugin to validate system behavior by executing, animating, and debugging SysML Parametric models in the context of realistic mock-ups of the intended user interface.

##### Why Simulation?

The purpose of a simulation is to gain system understanding without manipulating the real system, either because it is not yet defined or available, or because it cannot be exercised directly due to cost, time, resources or risk constraints. Simulation is typically performed on a model of the system. 
 
With the Cameo Simulation Toolkit, users can test how the system reacts to user interaction or predefined testing data and execution scenarios.

##### Business Benefits

Users will find that one of the key business benefits of the Cameo Simulation Toolkit is cost efficiency. Employing Cameo Simulation Toolkit will assist enterprises in significantly reducing project costs, allowing users to identify design errors before the actual production of components.

To learn how to install and use Cameo Simulation Toolkit, see:

859975750

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729254 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00041: Carrying values using connectors

- page_id: `136729254`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729254/Carrying+values+using+connectors
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:02.020+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['carrying-values-via-connectors', 'connectors']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

43209831

43209833

43209832

A connector can carry a runtime value over from one connectable element to another if the following three conditions are present

- Datatypes on both elements are the same or compatible (equality of value can be defined), or one is the subtype of another.
- If the elements are nested features, the owner of the nested features must exist.
- If the elements are nested properties, in this example, b1, a connector must be drawn from a3 to the port and from the port to b1 because UML does not have a concrete definition about nested properties. However, SysML does have a definition about nested properties; therefore, the connector can be drawn between nested properties without the help of any ports.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using Port and Connector to connect nested properties.

When primitive datatypes, in this example, n and a1, are bound together, the runtime values related to the role of both connector ends must be equal (but not necessarily the same instance). If the feature value of a data type value on one end changes, the data type value on the opposite end will change as well. As a result, n must equal a1, r must equal a2, s must equal a3, and b must equal a4 as follows.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Connectors connecting primitive datatypes.

43209830

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Binding Connector' space='CST2024x']

However, SysML does have a definition about nested properties; therefore, the connector can be drawn between nested properties without the help of any ports. </li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties" ac:alt="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Carrying values using connectors" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Using Port and Connector to connect nested properties.</h6><p>When primitive datatypes, in this example, n and a1, are bound together, the runtime values related to the role of both connector ends must be equal (but not necessarily the same instance). If the feature value of a data type value on one end changes, the data type value on the opposite end will change as well. As a result, n must equal a1, r must equal a2, s must equal a3, and b must equal a4 as follows. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Connectors Connecting Primitive Datatypes" ac:alt="Connectors Connecting Primitive Datatypes"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Connectors_connecting_primitive_datatypes.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Carrying values using connectors" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Connectors connecting primitive datatypes.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a07061d-ae39-40f2-9a0b-dc390e1391f7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">43209830</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Connector" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Binding Connector" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729815 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00042: Change Structural Feature Event

- page_id: `136729815`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729815/Change+Structural+Feature+Event
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:33.102+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['change-structural-feature-event']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Change Structural Feature Event is an Event that occurs when a value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. The Change Structural Feature Event will be accepted by an Accept Change Structural Feature Event action. You can find more information about simulation of an Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action and a Change Structural Feature Event in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action' space='CST2024x'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A Change Structural Feature Event is an Event that occurs when a value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. The Change Structural Feature Event will be accepted by an Accept Change Structural Feature Event action. You can find more information about simulation of an Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action and a Change Structural Feature Event in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Accept change structural feature Event action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729509 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00043: Changing Fork behavior

- page_id: `136729509`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729509/Changing+Fork+behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:13.921+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to specify whether Forks in Activity Diagrams run in a parallel or sequential way during simulation. You can change the behavior of an individual Fork by adding a constraint with a hardcoded keyword (*sequential* or *parallel*). If a Fork is set to run in parallel, all outgoing edges from that Fork run simultaneously. If a Fork is set to run sequentially, only one outgoing edge from that Fork can run at a time.

If you change the Fork behavior by adding constraints as described in this chapter, this behavior will override the **Run Forks In Parallel** property of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='CST2024x'].

To change Fork behavior during simulation

- In an Activity Diagram, right-click a Fork and do one of the following: [ATTACHMENT filename='changing_fork_behavior.png']
  - If you want the fork to run sequentially, go to Simulation > Occurrence > Sequential .
  - If you want the fork to run in parallel, go to Simulation > Occurrence > Parallel .

After you change the behavior of a Fork as described above, the label indicating the specified behavior is added to the Fork symbol (either *sequential*, or *parallel*).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to specify whether Forks in Activity Diagrams run in a parallel or sequential way during simulation. You can change the behavior of an individual Fork by adding a constraint with a hardcoded keyword (<em>sequential</em> or <em>parallel</em>). If a Fork is set to run in parallel, all outgoing edges from that Fork run simultaneously. If a Fork is set to run sequentially, only one outgoing edge from that Fork can run at a time. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f7663df-f710-4098-8e71-f6081d9bb46f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you change the Fork behavior by adding constraints as described in this chapter, this behavior will override the <strong>Run Forks In Parallel</strong> property of a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="SimulationConfig stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Simulation Configuration]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To change Fork behavior during simulation</p><hr /><ul><li>In an Activity Diagram, right-click a Fork and do one of the following:<ul><li>If you want the fork to run sequentially, go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Occurrence</strong> &gt; <strong>Sequential</strong>.</li><li>If you want the fork to run in parallel, go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Occurrence</strong> &gt; <strong>Parallel</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ul><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="changing_fork_behavior.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Changing Fork behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><p><br />After you change the behavior of a Fork as described above, the label indicating the specified behavior is added to the Fork symbol (either <em>sequential</em>, or <em>parallel</em>).</p>
````

The following ALH API checks whether a particular object is in a specific state name.

Unless the object is specified, it will use the current active object, for example:

Alternatively, you can use ALH API through the fUML object syntax, with **[object.in](http://object.in)(stateName)***.* For example:

- ccobj.in (“Operating”);

##### Getting the current State of a runtime object

You can use the following API to retrieve a current State of a specified object. If the object has more than one active State, it recognizes which the runtime State is, and finds it.

ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.statemachines.mdbehaviorstatemachines.State.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target"><br />Checking a visited State</h3><p>You can check whether a specified State was visited by calling the following command.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="78465268-4a73-4879-a5dd-b908c37b2173"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[boolean wasInState(String stateName)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p><p>The following code fragment shows how to check whether a specified State of State Machine, e.g., <em>State1</em>, was visited through ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4b996387-85c6-4eb8-930c-8f16c5dc2c81"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.wasInState("State1");	// Returns true or false.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><h3>Checking if an Activity Diagram is being simulated</h3><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729257 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00045: Checking values against feature types

- page_id: `136729257`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729257/Checking+values+against+feature+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:02.685+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['value-check', 'checking-runtime-values']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

44165603

44165605

44165604

Cameo Simulation Toolkit will automatically run a compatibility check every time you assign a value to a feature during simulation. It checks the value against the feature's type. The value you assign must correspond with the type of the feature, meaning you can assign only values that are compatible with the feature's type. If you assign an incompatible value to a feature, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will not add it to the feature. The following table lists all compatible values for each datatype

| Primitive datatype (Qualified name) | Compatible value |
| --- | --- |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::BooleanPrimitiveValueTypes::Boolean (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::boolean | Strings "true" or "false" (non-case sensitive). |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::IntegerPrimitiveValueTypes::Integer (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::int | Strings that can be cast to numbers (If the string is a real number, the floating point will be eliminated). Natural number datatypesUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::UnlimitedNaturalPrimitiveValueTypes::UnlimitedNatural (SysML Profile.mdzip) Real numbers, including the following four datatypes (The floating point will be eliminated)UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::RealPrimitiveValueTypes::Real (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::doubleUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::float |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::RealPrimitiveValueTypes::Real (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::doubleUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::float | Strings that can be cast to numbers. Integer numbers including the following five datatypesUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::IntegerPrimitiveValueTypes::Integer (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::intUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::UnlimitedNaturalPrimitiveValueTypes::UnlimitedNatural (SysML Profile.mdzip) |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::StringPrimitiveValueTypes::String (SysML Profile.mdzip) | Every value is compatible. |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::UnlimitedNaturalPrimitiveValueTypes::UnlimitedNatural (SysML Profile.mdzip) | Strings that can be cast into positive numbers.Real and integer (positive) numbers including the following datatypesUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::IntegerPrimitiveValueTypes::Integer (SysML Pro- file.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Pro- file::datatypes::intUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::RealPrimitiveValueTypes::Real (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::doubleUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::float |
| Enumeration | If a value equals to one of the Enumeration Literals, the value is compatible. |

44165602

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Primitive' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728758 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00046: Class

- page_id: `136728758`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728758/Class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:37.736+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation > Elements simulation
- labels: ['class-simulation', 'simulating-classes']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1427265110

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1427265112

1428069550

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1427265111

You can run a Class element that is not a Behavior. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from the selected Class. The default value of its parts and properties will be used for initialization. If the selected Class has a defined Classifier Behavior which is Activity, State Machine or Interaction, it will also be simulated. For example, if you execute the [ATTACHMENT filename='Calculator.mdzip'] Class, the simulation will be performed on the Calculator State Machine as shown in the figure [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0007.png'] in the State Machine section.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### State Machine as the Classifier Behavior of the Calculator Class.

If the Class does not have a Classifier Behavior, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize the object from the selected Class and show its values and parts in the **Variables** pane. For a SysML model, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will update the values within the object with the constraint properties and the binding connectors. Therefore, you can manipulate the values of the object when working with the parametric model of the Class.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Running a SysML block that has no defined Classifier Behavior.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Animation of a SysML Parametric diagram.

You can simulate a system even though there is no Classifier Behavior in either the parent or its subsystems. When Cameo Simulation Toolkit runs the system, all Classifiers that have or do not have Classifier Behaviors will be simulated. The simulation will run the system and will stop only if you terminate it by clicking the**Stop**button.

#### Initialization of value properties using Arrays of default values

Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports use of Arrays in Class/Block as a data type. A primitive value type, e.g., String, Boolean, Integer, and Real, as a value property with a default value using an Array can be initialized. The value property is shown in tree control structure on the **Variables** pane as shown in the figure below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Primitive value types are initialized as value properties with default values using Arrays.

Furthermore, the value property name as a variable can be used as a member of an Array. For example, *x* = 5 and *v* = [2, 3, 4, x] make the initialized *v* = [2, 3, 4, 5].

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- The initialization through an instance replaces the default value in the Array of the Block.
- Any missing values in Array slots are replaced by default values from the Array of the Block.

#### Automatic instantiation of the implementation Class

Simulation Toolkit supports automatic instantiation of an implementation Class, if the super Class is abstract (Is Abstract = true).

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If there are multiple implementation Classes, Simulation Toolkit will randomly choose one.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Automatic instantiation of the implementation Class if the super Class is abstract.

1427265109

**Related pages**

- Animation of a State Machine Simulation
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Classifier Behavior property' space='CST2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729816 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00047: Classifier Behavior property

- page_id: `136729816`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729816/Classifier+Behavior+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:33.305+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['classifier-behavior-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A classifier Behavior property is a property to which the stereotype «ClassifierBehaviorProperty» is applied. The value of a classifier Behavior property is a Behavior simulation of the classifier Behavior of an object. Therefore, the value of the classifier Behavior property exists only after the Behavior of the object has been started (See [ATTACHMENT filename='execute_objectwithAdjunct.png'].). The block **Integrator** has a classifier Behavior property typed by the activity **Integrator**. You will see the Behavior simulation as the value of the classifier Behavior in the **Variables** pane.

#### NOTE: Note: Support multiple inherited Classifier Behaviors

false

Note: Support multiple inherited Classifier Behaviors

When a block inherits from multiple other blocks which have a classifier Behavior (CB) only one of them is run instead of all. In the figure below, block A inherits from block B and block C, however, when running the simulation, only block A Behavior is simulated. The Behaviors C and B will be not simulated.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Block A inheriting from multiple blocks, B and C.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Simulation console messages showing only Block A is run.

If the specialized block does not have a CB, all inherited should be run. In the figure below, System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 Behaviors are simulated as asynchronous sessions because the SubSystem block does not have a Classifier Behavior.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Block SubSystem without any inherited Classifier Behavior.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Simulation console messages showing Block System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 all running asynchronously.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A classifier Behavior property is a property to which the stereotype «ClassifierBehaviorProperty» is applied. The value of a classifier Behavior property is a Behavior simulation of the classifier Behavior of an object. Therefore, the value of the classifier Behavior property exists only after the Behavior of the object has been started (See <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="execute_objectwithAdjunct.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Adjunct property" /></ri:attachment><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Executing an Object with Adjunct and Classifier Behavior Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.). The block <strong>Integrator</strong> has a classifier Behavior property typed by the activity <strong>Integrator</strong>. You will see the Behavior simulation as the value of the classifier Behavior in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. </p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5bbc7b60-500d-439e-b736-ff623d26e3d0"><ac:parameter ac:name="icon">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note: Support multiple inherited Classifier Behaviors</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When a block inherits from multiple other blocks which have a classifier Behavior (CB) only one of them is run instead of all. In the figure below, block A inherits from block B and block C, however, when running the simulation, only block A Behavior is simulated. The Behaviors C and B will be not simulated.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-a.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Block A inheriting from multiple blocks, B and C.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-b.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Simulation console messages showing only Block A is run.</h6><p>If the specialized block does not have a CB, all inherited should be run. In the figure below, System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 Behaviors are simulated as asynchronous sessions because the SubSystem block does not have a Classifier Behavior.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-c.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Block SubSystem without any inherited Classifier Behavior.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-d.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Simulation console messages showing Block System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 all running asynchronously.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729549 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00048: Combined fragment

- page_id: `136729549`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729549/Combined+fragment
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:17.058+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation > Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['combined-fragment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1009470182

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1009470185

1009470181

**On this page**

33

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1009470184

A Combined fragment is a combination (expression) of Interaction fragments defined by an Interaction operator and corresponding Interaction operands. A Combined fragment may have Interaction constraints, also called **guards**.

Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports three major Interaction operators: **Alternative (alt)**,**Option (opt)**, and**Loop (loop)**. Combined fragments with various Interaction operators will also be supported (e.g., alt inside loop, opt inside loop, loop inside alt, etc.).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Alternative (alt), Option (opt), and Loop (loop) are supported in Interaction fragments.

##### Alternative (alt)

The Interaction operator **alt** signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior. Only one of the operands will be chosen. The chosen operand must have an explicit or implicit Guard expression evaluated as true at a particular point in the Interaction. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.

An operand guarded by **else** means a Guard that is the negation of the dis-junction of all other Guards. If none of the operands has a Guard evaluated true, none of the operands are executed, and the remainder of the enclosing Interaction fragment is executed.

##### Option (opt)

The Interaction operator **opt** signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior where either the (sole) operand happens or nothing happens. An option is semantically equivalent to an alternative combined fragment where there is one operand with non-empty content, and the second operand is empty. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.

##### Loop (loop)

The Interaction operator **loop** signifies that the combined fragment represents a loop. The loop operand will be repeated a number of times. Only an Interaction constraint (not iteration bounds), a Boolean expression shown in square brackets that guards an operand in a combined fragment, is supported.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Any Guard that is not Boolean expression evaluation will show a **Question** dialog box to set the evaluation result true or false. See also [using Guards on Transitions](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Using+Guards+on+Transitions).

382168754

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Guards on Transitions' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operators' space='CST2024x']

A Combined fragment may have Interaction constraints, also called <strong>guards</strong>.</p><p>Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports three major Interaction operators: <strong>Alternative (alt)</strong>,<strong> Option (opt)</strong>, and<strong> Loop (loop)</strong>. Combined fragments with various Interaction operators will also be supported (e.g., alt inside loop, opt inside loop, loop inside alt, etc.).</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Combined Fragment.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Combined fragment" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Alternative (alt), Option (opt), and Loop (loop) are supported in Interaction fragments.</h6><h3 class="Textbody">Alternative (alt)</h3><p class="Textbody">The Interaction operator <strong>alt</strong> signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior. Only one of the operands will be chosen. The chosen operand must have an explicit or implicit Guard expression evaluated as true at a particular point in the Interaction. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.</p><p class="Textbody">An operand guarded by <strong>else</strong> means a Guard that is the negation of the dis-junction of all other Guards. If none of the operands has a Guard evaluated true, none of the operands are executed, and the remainder of the enclosing Interaction fragment is executed.</p><h3 class="Textbody">Option (opt)</h3><p class="Textbody">The Interaction operator <strong>opt</strong> signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior where either the (sole) operand happens or nothing happens. An option is semantically equivalent to an alternative combined fragment where there is one operand with non-empty content, and the second operand is empty. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.</p><h3 class="Textbody">Loop (loop)</h3><p class="Textbody">The Interaction operator <strong>loop</strong> signifies that the combined fragment represents a loop. The loop operand will be repeated a number of times. Only an Interaction constraint (not iteration bounds), a Boolean expression shown in square brackets that guards an operand in a combined fragment, is supported.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7efb0106-a9af-4c09-82c0-ad1ae096e047"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Any Guard that is not Boolean expression evaluation will show a <strong>Question</strong> dialog box to set the evaluation result true or false. See also <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Using+Guards+on+Transitions">using Guards on Transitions</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="094456de-17cd-4df2-a13d-e1061251eaf4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">382168754</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Using Guards on Transitions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Operators" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729711 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00049: Communicating with evaluators through simulation console

- page_id: `136729711`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729711/Communicating+with+evaluators+through+simulation+console
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:25.182+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['communicating-with-parametric-evaluator']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

468495788

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

468495790

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

468495789

You can communicate with a parametric evaluator directly through the command prompt, which is located in the lower part of the **Simulation****Console** pane. You can click the arrow of the language selection drop-down list to the right of the command prompt and select a language you want. Once selected, the language will appear in the command prompt in the **Console** pane. For example, if you select **Matlab**, the language in the command prompt will change from "**»**" to "**matlab**»" (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Selecting a Language for the Command Prompt.

You can enter an expression or a command, which is written in the syntax of the language you selected, in the command prompt and press the **Enter** key on your keyboard to simulate it (see the following figure).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Executing Expression in the Command Prompt.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can also use the command prompt in the **Simulation****Console** pane to communicate with the script engine by selecting a scripting language from the language selection drop-down list. For example, selecting **JavaScript** will cause the language to change to "js»".

468495787
