<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729357 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00050: Completion Events and Transitions

- page_id: `136729357`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729357/Completion+Events+and+Transitions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:09.990+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation > Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['completion-events', 'transitions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

122387409

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

122387411

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

122387410

Completion Events are standard UML Events which are fired during the execution of a State Machine diagram. For composite or submachine States, a completion Event will be generated when all internal Activities, e.g., **entry** and **doActivity** Behaviors, have completed execution under either of the following circumstances

- If the State is a composite State, all its orthogonal Regions have reached a FinalState.
- if the State is a submachine State, the submachine StateMachine execution has reached a FinalState.

#### TIP: TIP

TIP

You can enable/disable the **Completion Events and Transitions** option in the **SCXML Engine** group in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='CST2024x']** dialog for completion Events and Transitions support.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Completion Events and Transitions option in the SCXML Engine group in the Project Options dialog.

**Case 1**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Completion Events and Transitions in a State Machine diagram.

For example, in the above State Machine diagram, a Transition from the "on" State to the "off" State does not happen until a completion Event is generated. The completion Event is generated after the DoActivity Behavior completes and only then the State transits from "on" to "off".

**Case 2**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Completion Events and Transitions in a State Machine diagram.

In the above State Machine diagram, a completion Event is generated after the entry and sendOff Behaviors have completed.

**Case 3**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Completion Events and Transitions in a State Machine diagram.

In the above State Machine diagram, a completion Event will be generated after the entry and working Behaviors have completed.

#### INFO: Information

Information

Completion Events have dispatch priority over all other Events. They are put into the beginning of the Event queue.

122387408

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729762 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00051: Connecting to a running MATLAB session

- page_id: `136729762`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729762/Connecting+to+a+running+MATLAB+session
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:28.639+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Integration with external Evaluators > Integration with MATLAB
- labels: ['connecting-to-matlab-session', 'matlab-session']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

67934221

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

67934236

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

67934226

Simulation Toolkit can make connection to a running MATLAB session, thus all variables in the workspace can be used.

To connect Simulation Toolkit to a running MATLAB session through sharing

- Execute the matlab.engine.shareEngine command through the MATLAB Command window.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connecting to a running MATLAB session' space='CST2024x'] before the connection is made.
- If the sharing command shown above is not executed, the other MATLAB session loaded by Simulation Toolkit will be used instead of the shared one.

After the session connection, Simulation Toolkit can access variables in the MATLAB shared workspace that are not in the SysML model. You can define some variables in MATLAB, e.g., *m1* and *m2* and solve parametric in Simulation Toolkit. These two groups of variables are then merged and can be accessed in MATLAB as shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Variables in the shared MATLAB workspace can be used in either Simulation Toolkit or MATLAB.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e5c09506-5247-4fff-a7d1-4a63aa3bc831"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">67934221</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc95e30f-7a25-4997-96c9-2d8266d8f1d6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">67934236</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc35c9fb-7374-4a80-b011-c4cf9b4fe8b4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">67934226</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Simulation Toolkit can make connection to a running MATLAB session, thus all variables in the workspace can be used.</p><p><br /></p><p>To connect Simulation Toolkit to a running MATLAB session through sharing</p><hr /><ul><li>Execute the <strong>matlab.engine.shareEngine</strong> command through the MATLAB Command window.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5593dd7f-5918-44cd-abcc-be3c85858b13"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="IntegratingMagicDraworCameoSystemsModelerwithMATLAB%C2%AE(onMicrosoftWindowsorMacOSX)"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[MATLAB must be integrated successfully]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Connecting to a running MATLAB session" /></ac:link> before the connection is made.</li><li>If the sharing command shown above is not executed, the other MATLAB session loaded by Simulation Toolkit will be used instead of the shared one.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">After the session connection, Simulation Toolkit can access variables in the MATLAB shared workspace that are not in the SysML model. You can define some variables in MATLAB, e.g., <em>m1</em> and <em>m2</em> and solve parametric in Simulation Toolkit. These two groups of variables are then merged and can be accessed in MATLAB as shown in the following figure.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Variables in the Shared MATLAB Workspace.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Connecting to a running MATLAB session" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Variables in the shared MATLAB workspace can be used in either Simulation Toolkit or MATLAB.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729174 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00052: Console log's filter options

- page_id: `136729174`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729174/Console+log+s+filter+options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:56.458+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Simulation console
- labels: ['console-log-filter', 'log-filter-in-console']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

33294084

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

33294086

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

33294085

When running a model, Cameo Simulation Toolkit always shows a log list in the **Console** pane that records each and every element that is being simulated such as the start and end elements. If you want to narrow down the log lists to display only specific messages, e.g., errors or warning messages, you can use the **Options** button in the **Console** pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Console's Options button.

The **Options** button on the Console pane contains four options to filter log reports as follows

| Option | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Debug | Debug, by default, shows all messages. |
| Info | This option only shows information with a priority equivalent to INFO or higher (WARN and ERROR). |
| Warn | This is the default filter option. It only shows information with a priority equivalent to WARN or higher (ERROR). |
| Error | This option only displays information with a priority equivalent to ERROR. |

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- By default, the user print output, the start time, the end time, and the duration of the main Behavior simulation will always be printed.
- Duration Analysis information will be printed in the Debug filter option.

33294083

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Console pane' space='CST2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729161 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00053: Console pane

- page_id: `136729161`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729161/Console+pane
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:55.462+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Simulation console
- labels: ['console-tab']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

32300809

32300811

32300810

Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides the **Simulation** **Console** pane in the **Simulation** window. The **Console** pane displays simulation information during a model simulation including the date and time the simulation engine starts and the date and time the simulation runs and stops.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Runtime information of Cameo Simulation Toolkit.

The **Console** pane may contain a hyperlink to a model element in a MagicDraw project. During a model simulation, scripts evaluation failures may happen and thus expression evaluation errors occur. If Cameo Simulation Toolkit cannot evaluate some scripts in an element, it will create a hyperlink in the **Console** pane to that element in the Containment tree. When you click the link, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will highlight the element in the Containment tree.

The following figure shows a hyperlink resulting from errors in evaluating scripts in the **Console** pane. The link points to the corresponding element in the Containment tree.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A hyperlink to the Element whose scripts cannot be simulated.

The table below shows the function of each button in the **Console** pane

| Button | Name | Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Clear Console | To remove all simulation information displayed in the Console pane. |
|  | Show Runtime Information | To display the runtime information of the Cameo Simulation Toolkit in the Console pane.The runtime information consists of the Cameo Simulation Toolkit version, registered simulation engines, and available scripting engines. |
|  | Options | To filter outputs in the Console pane. There are four filter options: Debug, Info, Warn, and Error (See for more details.). |

The following figure shows the simulation Console 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation console.

32300807

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title="Console log's filter options" space='CST2024x']

| --- | --- |
| E | A real value that is closer than any other to e, the base of natural logarithms. |
| PI | A real value that is closer than any other to pi, the ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter. |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729821 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00055: Constraint Block

- page_id: `136729821`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729821/Constraint+Block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:33.639+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['constraint-block']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A constraint Block is a subtype of a Block. It is a class stereotyped with «ConstraintBlock». It has a constraint with an expression to constrain the values of its constraint parameters. If an object initialized from a constraint Block and a value bound to a constraint parameter of that object is changed, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will evaluate the expression of the constraint. You can see more information about how Cameo Simulation Toolkit evaluates an expression in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating expressions' space='CST2024x'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A constraint Block is a subtype of a Block. It is a class stereotyped with «ConstraintBlock». It has a constraint with an expression to constrain the values of its constraint parameters. If an object initialized from a constraint Block and a value bound to a constraint parameter of that object is changed, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will evaluate the expression of the constraint. You can see more information about how Cameo Simulation Toolkit evaluates an expression in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link>. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729692 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00056: Constraints on parts

- page_id: `136729692`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729692/Constraints+on+parts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:23.676+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['constraints-on-parts']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In a particular system or context where blocks are used, it is possible to add constraints on parts and constraint value ranges. To better explain how it works, see the following example of a car model.

A car model has *front:* *Wheel* and*rear: Wheel*. The diameter of the Wheel block is specified as *diameter: Real value property*. It is possible to add different constraints to different parts; e.g., the front wheel’s diameter is *{diameter < 19}* and rear wheel’s diameter is *{diameter < 20}*.

The following figure demonstrates a Car (block) along with the diameter of Wheel (constraints) applied to the rear, front, and spare wheel (part properties).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A sample model of constraints on part properties.

If the constraints of the block are applied to the part properties through another block with constraints inside, you will get the following results.

1. The value ranges will be evaluated through all of the applied constraints. The constraints may reside in the part properties, main block, or constraint blocks. For example, front wheel {diameter >= 15, diameter < 19} .
2. If a constraint fails during evaluation, a failure message will appear on the owned constraint; e.g., part properties, main block, or constraint blocks In the Simulation Console panel.
3. In the Variables pane, the constraints applied to the part properties appear together with the constraints of its own part properties in {...}. Cameo Simulation Toolkit highlights failed constraints with a red background and opens the failure message in the tooltip of the applied constraint.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### This figure depicts the constraints failure message in the Console panes andthe details of failed constraints (in red background) that are applied to part properties front : Wheel in the Variables pane.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In a particular system or context where blocks are used, it is possible to add constraints on parts and constraint value ranges. To better explain how it works, see the following example of a car model.</p><p class="Standard">A car model has <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b3f3ffc1-4b94-4632-bbcb-c2ef63feb228"><em>front:</em> <em>Wheel</em></ac:inline-comment-marker> and<em> rear: Wheel</em>. The diameter of the Wheel block is specified as <em>diameter: Real value property</em>. It is possible to add different constraints to different parts; <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5ee9814a-9b94-4782-9652-50c8cb08a72b">e.g., the front whe</ac:inline-comment-marker>el’s diameter is <em>{diameter &lt; 19}</em> and rear wheel’s <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="66357b79-e0ed-4a59-837e-67e30bf20b09">diameter</ac:inline-comment-marker> is <em>{diameter &lt; 20}</em>.</p><p class="Standard">The following figure demonstrates a Car (block) along with the diameter of Wheel (constraints) applied to the rear, front, and spare wheel (part properties).</p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="An example of constraints on the part properties" ac:alt="An example of constraints on the part properties"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraints_onParts1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Constraints on parts" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">A sample model of constraints on part properties.</h6><p class="Standard"> </p><p class="Standard">If the constraints of the block are applied to the part properties through another block with constraints inside, you will get the following results.</p><ol><li>The value ranges will be evaluated through all of the applied constraints. The constraints may reside in the part properties, main block, or constraint blocks. For example, front wheel <em>{diameter &gt;= 15, diameter &lt; 19}</em>.</li><li>If a constraint fails during evaluation, a failure message will appear on the owned constraint; e.g., part properties, main block, or constraint blocks In the Simulation Console panel.</li><li>In the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, the constraints applied to the part properties appear together with the constraints of its own part properties in {...}. Cameo Simulation Toolkit highlights failed constraints with a red background and opens the failure message in the tooltip of the applied constraint.</li></ol><p class="Standard"> </p><p class="Standard"> <ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="The constraints appear in the Simulation Console and Variables panes during simulation." ac:alt="The constraints appear in the Simulation Console and Variables panes during simulation."><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraints_onParts2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Constraints on parts" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">This figure depicts <span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">the constraints failure message in the Console panes and </span>the details of failed constraints (in red background) that are applied to part <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="f4d177b3-bb2c-4d5c-aecb-c20ac65e5789">properties front : Wheel in the Variables pane.</ac:inline-comment-marker></h6><p class="Standard"> </p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729181 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00057: Context, runtime object, and runtime value

- page_id: `136729181`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729181/Context+runtime+object+and+runtime+value
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:57.317+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['context', 'runtime-object', 'runtime-value', 'simulation-context']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Structural feature

35169272

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Structural feature

35169274

35169271

**On this page**

2

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Structural feature

35169273

When you are executing a model simulation, Cameo Simulation Toolkit creates the context, runtime objects, and runtime values to store the simulated values of the model.

#### **Context**

A simulation session is always associated with its context of simulation. The context of a simulation session is a Class or one of its subtypes. When a context element is simulated, a runtime object (of the context's type) will be created to store the runtime values. In the following figure, the context of the selected simulation session is the Calculator class.

#### **Runtime object**

A runtime object is the simulated value of a Class. In other words, it is a runtime instance of a Class and of the context as well. In the following figure, the runtime object of the simulation session context is the "Calculator@6e46ab00" instance. Since the runtime instance is the "Calculator" Class type, it can contain structural features (which correspond to the Class attributes), such as "display" and "operand1".

#### **Runtime value**

A runtime value refers to the value of the structural features mentioned in the Runtime object section, e.g., "200" and "120". However, if the type of a structural feature is a Classifier, its runtime value can also refer to another runtime object of a structural feature type.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Simulation console showing the context "Class, the runtime object of the simulation session context "Calculator@6e46ab00", and the runtime values of "120" and "200".

#### Glossary

#### EXPAND: Class

Class

Class is a Classifier that describes a set of objects that share the same features, constraints, and semantics (meaning). A class is the most widely used Classifier. It is shown as a solid-outline rectangle containing the Class name and with optional compartments separated by horizontal lines containing features or other members of the Classifier.

#### EXPAND: Classifier

Classifier

Classifier is a category of UML elements that have some common features, e.g., attributes and methods. It describes a set of instances that have common behavioral and structural features (operations and attributes respectively).

#### EXPAND: Element

Element

Element is the abstract root UML metaclass, it has no superclass in the hierarchy of UML elements. It is the superclass for all metaclasses in the UML infrastructure library.

#### EXPAND: Instance

Instance

Instance is a system's entity; it is a concrete manifestation (implementation) of an abstraction. Abstraction could be represented by one or more Classifiers or no Classifiers at all. When an instance manifests a Classifier, it is called instance of that Classifier. For example, Object is an instance of a Class, while the link is an instance of an association. An instance could have a name or be anonymous when the name is not important. The instance name allows you to distinguish it from the other instances within the same naming context (scope).

#### EXPAND: Object

Object

Object is an instance of a Class. It is an individual (thing) with a State and relationships to other objects. The State of an object identifies the values for that object of properties of the Classifier of the object.

#### EXPAND: Structural feature

Structural feature

Structural feature is a typed feature of a Classifier that specifies the structure of instances of the classifier. It specifies that instances of the featuring classifier have a slot whose value or values are of a specified type.

#### Sample model

[ATTACHMENT filename='Calculator.mdzip']250

35825649

**Related pages**

To learn more about working with context, runtime objects, and runtime values, you can visit the following pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating values and objects in the variables pane' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating runtime objects from Classifiers' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting runtime objects to InstanceSpecifications' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Recording Verification status of runtime values' space='CST2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72d05045-adbe-49ab-af30-52fb4609c235"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169272</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f525946f-98ef-43a3-8467-e1b0c0a53d91"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169274</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e75edad2-6584-4a5c-90e5-8eaef80c5b5f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169271</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c01a964c-5980-4d8c-8782-32395823ed02"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4fa895f0-2b9d-46f4-bcf9-2849374f82c4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169273</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>When you are executing a model simulation, Cameo Simulation Toolkit creates the context, runtime objects, and runtime values to store the simulated values of the model. </p><h2><strong>Context</strong> </h2><p>A simulation session is always associated with its context of simulation. The context of a simulation session is a Class or one of its subtypes. When a context element is simulated, a runtime object (of the context's type) will be created to store the runtime values. In the following figure, the context of the selected simulation session is the Calculator class. </p><h2><strong>Runtime object</strong> </h2><p>A runtime object is the simulated value of a Class. In other words, it is a runtime instance of a Class and of the context as well. In the following figure, the runtime object of the simulation session context is the &quot;<span>Calculator@6e46ab00</span>&quot; instance. Since the runtime instance is the &quot;Calculator&quot; Class type, it can contain structural features (which correspond to the Class attributes), such as &quot;display&quot; and &quot;operand1&quot;. </p><h2><strong>Runtime value</strong> </h2><p>A runtime value refers to the value of the structural features mentioned in the Runtime object section, e.g., &quot;200&quot; and &quot;120&quot;. However, if the type of a structural feature is a Classifier, its runtime value can also refer to another runtime object of a structural feature type. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Simulation console" ac:alt="Simulation console"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Variable_panel.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Context, runtime object, and runtime value" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Simulation console showing the context &quot;Class, the runtime object of the simulation session context &quot;Calculator@<span style="color: rgb(0,51,102);">6e46ab00</span>&quot;, and the runtime values of &quot;120&quot; and &quot;200&quot;.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h2>Glossary</h2><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68addeff-6845-48c9-adf9-660dd5124a75"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Class</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Class is a Classifier that describes a set of objects that share the same features, constraints, and semantics (meaning). A class is the most widely used Classifier. It is shown as a solid-outline rectangle containing the Class name and with optional compartments separated by horizontal lines containing features or other members of the Classifier.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aa5e5f7e-7c99-4ce4-be31-1555c1218a51"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Classifier</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Classifier is a category of UML elements that have some common features, e.g., attributes and methods. It describes a set of instances that have common behavioral and structural features (operations and attributes respectively).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60e5d5fa-c507-48d7-889c-5a8d77526cc6"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Element</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Element is the abstract root UML metaclass, it has no superclass in the hierarchy of UML elements. It is the superclass for all metaclasses in the UML infrastructure library. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="269574b7-688a-4b59-a254-030d72a81014"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Instance</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Instance is a system's entity; it is a concrete manifestation (implementation) of an abstraction. Abstraction could be represented by one or more Classifiers or no Classifiers at all. When an instance manifests a Classifier, it is called instance of that Classifier. For example, Object is an instance of a Class, while the link is an instance of an association. An instance could have a name or be anonymous when the name is not important. The instance name allows you to distinguish it from the other instances within the same naming context (scope).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="054be2d9-e123-43f4-8f12-5669c19e2340"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Object</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Object is an instance of a Class. It is an individual (thing) with a State and relationships to other objects. The State of an object identifies the values for that object of properties of the Classifier of the object.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0656aaec-9163-439e-b209-356ae3a177fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Structural feature</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Structural feature is a typed feature of a Classifier that specifies the structure of instances of the classifier. It specifies that instances of the featuring classifier have a slot whose value or values are of a specified type.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h2>Sample model</h2><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="view-file" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b66ade8a-1197-48fa-9140-1f2352da234b"><ac:parameter ac:name="name"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Calculator.mdzip"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Context, runtime object, and runtime value" /></ri:attachment></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">250</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b3eaaba6-2e83-48de-aaa9-3ca5ca1722e4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35825649</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p>To learn more about working with context, runtime objects, and runtime values, you can visit the following pages</p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating values and objects in the variables pane" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Exporting runtime objects to InstanceSpecifications" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Recording Verification status of runtime values" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729063 space=CST2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00058: Control panel for Web UI

- page_id: `136729063`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729063/Control+panel+for+Web+UI
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2024-01-04T13:36:53.755+01:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Web Server for Cameo Simulation Toolkit
- labels: ['control-panel-for-web-ui', 'web-ui-control-panel']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The simulation control panel for Web UI allows you to control a simulation directly from the Web UI using the **Start**, **Pause**/**Resume**, **Step Into**, **Step Over**, and **Terminate** buttons when no simulation window is used. These four buttons have the same functionality as those of the buttons on the simulation toolbar. You can move the toolbar on the browser as desired. The following figure shows the web UI Mockup of the Calculator sample model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Web UI control panel with the restart function.

The **Add Control Panel** option in the Specification window of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''] allows you to enable these buttons on the Web UI.

To enable the control panel buttons on the Web UI

1. Double-click the <<SimulationConfig>> on the diagram pane to open its Specification window.
2. Select the Add Control Panel check box under the Web Server option. The value will change to true and the addControlPanel stereotype will be added in the <<SimulationConfig>> .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Enabling the Add Control Panel option in the Specification window of <<SimulationConfig>>.

You can start and stop the Web Server using the **Start/Stop Web Server** button on the simulation **Console** pane.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Start/Stop Web Server button on the simulation Console pane.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the *Calculator.mdzip* sample model that comes with MagicDraw.

To open the sample

- Find it in the modeling tool <modeling_tool_install_dir>\samples\simulation\Calculator.mdzip.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The simulation control panel for Web UI allows you to control a simulation directly from the Web UI using the <strong>Start</strong>, <strong>Pause</strong>/<strong>Resume</strong>, <strong>Step Into</strong>, <strong>Step Over</strong>, and <strong>Terminate</strong> buttons when no simulation window is used. These four buttons have the same functionality as those of the buttons on the simulation toolbar. You can move the toolbar on the browser as desired. The following figure shows the web UI Mockup of the Calculator sample model</p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4daf2789-beca-497a-ae9a-e93325ffbedc"><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4daf2789-beca-497a-ae9a-e93325ffbedc"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="WebUIControl.png" /></ac:image><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;"> The Web UI control panel with the restart function.</h6><p class="Standard">The <strong>Add Control Panel</strong> option in the Specification window of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SimulationConfig stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Simulation Configuration]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> allows you to enable these buttons on the Web UI.</p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard">To enable the control panel buttons on the Web UI</p><hr /><ol><li class="Standard">Double-click the<em> &lt;&lt;SimulationConfig&gt;&gt;</em> on the diagram pane to open its Specification window. </li><li class="Standard">Select the<strong> Add Control Panel</strong> check box under the <strong>Web Server</strong> option. The value will change to <strong>true </strong>and the <strong>addControlPanel</strong> stereotype will be added in the<em> &lt;&lt;SimulationConfig&gt;&gt;</em>.</li></ol><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddControlPanel.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard">Enabling the Add Control Panel option in the Specification window of &lt;&lt;<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fb6fc57e-3b83-49cf-8840-59cc67bb84f1">SimulationConfig&gt;&gt;.</ac:inline-comment-marker></h6><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fb6fc57e-3b83-49cf-8840-59cc67bb84f1">You can start and stop the Web Server using the <strong>Start/Stop Web Server</strong> button on the simulation <strong>Console</strong> pane.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fb6fc57e-3b83-49cf-8840-59cc67bb84f1"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="startstopwebserver.PNG" /></ac:image><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><h6><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fb6fc57e-3b83-49cf-8840-59cc67bb84f1">The Start/Stop Web Server button on the simulation Console pane.</ac:inline-comment-marker></h6><p><br /></p><h2>Sample model</h2><p>The model used in the figures on this page is the <em>Calculator.mdzip</em> sample model that comes with MagicDraw.</p><p><br /></p><p>To open the sample</p><hr /><ul><li>Find it in the modeling tool <em>&lt;modeling_tool_install_dir&gt;\samples\simulation\Calculator.mdzip.</em></li></ul><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730077 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00059: Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity

- page_id: `136730077`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730077/Creating+increaseTime+Operation+and+increaseTime+Activity
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:45.590+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model > Defining the stopwatch operations by using Activities
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An **increaseTime** operation is necessary for the StopWatch operation. It is used to increase the value of time by 1 whenever the operation is called. Therefore, we need to create an operation and Activity for the **StopWatch** Class to define this operation the same as we did for a resetTime operation and resetTime Activity.

To create an operation and an Activity for the StopWatch

1. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Operation .
2. Name the new operation "increaseTime" .
3. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Activity .
4. Name the new Activity "increaseTime" .
5. Right-click the increaseTime Activity in the containment browser and select New Diagram > Activity Diagram to add an Activity diagram to the increaseTime Activity. The new Activity diagram will be created under the increaseTime Activity. We will use the default name of this diagram, which is "increaseTime" .

To increase the time values, the increaseTime Activity will do the following 
(ii) get the object of the StopWatch using a ReadSelfAction 
 (ii) obtain the current time value using the ReadStructuralFeatureAction 
 (iii) increment the time value by one using an OpaqueBehaviorAction 
 (iv) set the increased time value to the object of the StopWatch using AddStructuralFeatureValueAction 
 (v) set the specification of the IncreaseTime Activity to the IncreaseTime Operation 
 
**(i) Getting****the****object****of****the****StopWatch****using****a****ReadSelfAction**

The Object of the StopWatch, which contains the time value, must be obtained by using a ReadSelfAction. To create the ReadSelfAction, you can follow the same steps used to create the ReadSelfAction in the **resetTime** Activity. In this example, the Object, which is the value of the output pin of the ReadSelfAction, needs to be supplied to multiple Actions (ReadStructuralFeatureAction and AddStructuralFeatureValueAction that will be created later). Therefore, a Fork must be added to this Activity. 
 
To add a Fork to an Activity

1. Click Fork Vertical on the Activity diagram toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavf6bb2ee026c1f5da69ac93853fd47cae.png']
2. Click the increaseTime Activity diagram to create a Fork.
3. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the fork and the self output pin. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavd71668fa7e06ac9f3a537f68d7ef8f4a.png']

**(ii)****Obtaining****the****current****time****value u****sing****a****ReadStructuralFeatureAction**

Once you have obtained the object of the Stopwatch, you will need to get the time value.

To get the current time value using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction

1. Click Action > Any Action on the Activity diagram toolbar. The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open.
2. Select ReadStructuralFeatureAction and click OK .
3. Click the increaseTime Activity diagram. A new ReadStructuralFeatureAction will be created on the diagram.
4. Right-click the ReadStructuralFeatureAction symbol on the diagram and select Specification to open its Specification window. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action.png']
5. Select the Structural Feature row and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select Property dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-property-a.png']
6. Select the Attribute time:Integer[1] of the StopWatch Class and click the OK button.
7. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Fork and the self input pin of ReadStructuralFeatureAction. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavda542b6684ebbe2004005e0d7f47ee80.png']

**(iii)****Incrementing****the****time v****alue****by o****ne****using****an****Opaque Behavior Action**

We need an OpaqueBehaviorAction to increase the time value. An OpaqueBehaviorAction receives an integer value as its input parameter and returns the integer value, which is the value of the input parameter that has been incremented by one. 
 
To create an Opaque Behavior for the **increaseTime**Activity

1. Right-click the increaseTime Activity in the containment browser and select New Element > Opaque Behavior . A new Opaque Behavior will be created under the increaseTime Activity.
2. Name it "increase" .
3. Right-click the increase Opaque Behavior in the containment browser and select Specification to open its Specification window.
4. Select the Parameters node in the Specification window and click the Create button . A new parameter will be created for the increase Opaque Behavior, and the Parameter Specification window will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-opaque-behavior.png']
5. Name the parameter "ti" , select Integer as its type, and select in as its direction. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-parameter.png']
6. Click the Back button to return to the Specification window of the increase Opaque Behavior.
7. Repeat the same steps used to create the parameter ti to create another parameter, which will be a return parameter of the increase Opaque Behavior.
8. Name the return parameter "to" , select Integer as its type, and select return as its direction. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-parameter-2.png']
9. Click the Back button to return to the Specification window of the increase Opaque Behavior.

Once the parameters "ti" and "to" have been created, we will write a simple JavaScript that will be executed during the simulation. 
 
To create a simple JavaScript to be executed in the simulation

1. Open the increase Opaque Behavior's Specification window and select the increase Opaque Behavior node on the left-hand side of the dialog.
2. Type a JavaScript into the **Body** row, e.g., *"to=ti+1"*. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] NoteYou can use any scripting language that is supported by JSR-223. To specify the scripting language that you are going to use, type it directly in the **Language** row. JavaScript will be used by default if no scripting language is specified.
3. Click Close to close the Specification window.
4. Create an OpaqueBehaviorAction on the increaseTime Activity by dragging the created increase Opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the increaseTime Activity diagram. A new OpaqueBehaviorAction will be created with the pins that correspond to the defined parameters ti and to .
5. Name the created OpaqueBehaviorAction "inc".
6. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the output pin t of the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the input pin ti of the inc:increase OpaqueBehaviorAction . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddava7acc324d5817b8f773c8d3f58b0a8d1.png']

**(iv) Setting****the i****ncreased****time****value****to the****object****of****StopWatch****using****AddStructuralFeatureValueAction**

Once the time value has been increased using the **inc:increase** OpaqueBehaviorAction, you need to set it back to the Object of **StopWatch**. To do this, you can follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the **resetTime** Activity. 
 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To set the increased time value to the object of StopWatch

1. Follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the resetTime Activity diagram.
2. Get the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to set the value to the time:Integer of StopWatch. Make sure that the Is Replace All of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction is set to true .
3. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect from the output pin to of inc:increase OpaqueBehaviorAction to the input pin t of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction (see the steps required for resetting the time value of the StopWatch object to zero with AddStructuralFeatureValueAction in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity' space='CST2024x'] ).
4. Add an Initial Node and an Activity Final stage to the increaseTime Activity and click Control Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect between the Actions. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavff9d9f7f43a5f3ceeb2fce67dc48f4af.png']

**(v)****Setting****the****specification****of****the i****ncrease time****Activity****to the****IncreaseTime****Operation**

To set the specification of the increaseTime Activity to the increaseTime operation

1. Open the Specification window of the increaseTime Activity by right-clicking the increaseTime Activity in the containment browser and select Specification .
2. Select the Specification row and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select Element dialog will open.
3. Select the increaseTime operation of the StopWatch Class as the specification of the Activity.
4. Click OK to close the Select Element dialog.
5. Click Close to close the Specification window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An <strong>increaseTime</strong> operation is necessary for the StopWatch operation. It is used to increase the value of time by 1 whenever the operation is called. Therefore, we need to create an operation and Activity for the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class to define this operation the same as we did for a resetTime operation and resetTime Activity.</p><p>To create an operation and an Activity for the StopWatch</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Operation</strong>.</li><li>Name the new operation <em>&quot;increaseTime&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">New Element</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Activity</strong>.</li><li>Name the new Activity <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;increaseTime&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">New Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Activity</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong> to add an Activity diagram to the increaseTime Activity. The new Activity diagram will be created under the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity. We will use the default name of this diagram, which is <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;increaseTime&quot;</strong>.</li></ol><p> </p><p>To increase the time values, the increaseTime Activity will do the following <br class="atl-forced-newline" />(ii) get the object of the StopWatch using a ReadSelfAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (ii) obtain the current time value using the ReadStructuralFeatureAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (iii) increment the time value by one using an OpaqueBehaviorAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (iv) set the increased time value to the object of the StopWatch using AddStructuralFeatureValueAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (v) set the specification of the IncreaseTime Activity to the IncreaseTime Operation <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(i) Getting </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>object </strong><strong>of </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>StopWatch </strong><strong>using </strong><strong>a </strong><strong>ReadSelfAction</strong></p><p>The Object of the StopWatch, which contains the time value, must be obtained by using a ReadSelfAction. To create the ReadSelfAction, you can follow the same steps used to create the ReadSelfAction in the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity. In this example, the Object, which is the value of the output pin of the ReadSelfAction, needs to be supplied to multiple Actions (ReadStructuralFeatureAction and AddStructuralFeatureValueAction that will be created later). Therefore, a Fork must be added to this Activity. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To add a Fork to an Activity</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Fork </strong><strong>Vertical</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Fork Vertical Button on the Activity Diagram Toolbar" ac:alt="The Fork Vertical Button on the Activity Diagram Toolbar"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavf6bb2ee026c1f5da69ac93853fd47cae.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the increaseTime Activity diagram to create a Fork.</li><li>Click <strong>Object</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the fork and the <strong>self</strong> output pin.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Connecting the Output Pin and Fork" ac:alt="Connecting the Output Pin and Fork"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavd71668fa7e06ac9f3a537f68d7ef8f4a.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p><br /><strong>(ii) </strong><strong>Obtaining </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>current </strong><strong>time </strong><strong>value u</strong><strong>sing </strong><strong>a </strong><strong>ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong></p><p>Once you have obtained the object of the Stopwatch, you will need to get the time value.</p><p>To get the current time value using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any </strong><strong>Action</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action </strong><strong>MetaClass</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity diagram. A new ReadStructuralFeatureAction will be created on the diagram.</li><li>Right-click the ReadStructuralFeatureAction symbol on the diagram and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> to open its Specification window. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Structural Feature Attribute of the ReadStructuralFeatureAction" ac:alt="The Structural Feature Attribute of the ReadStructuralFeatureAction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-add-structural-feature-action.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Structural</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Feature</strong> row and click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-dot-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Property</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Selecting a Structural Feature in the Select Property Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting a Structural Feature in the Select Property Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-property-a.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the Attribute <strong>time:Integer[1]</strong> of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class and click the <strong>OK</strong> button.</li><li>Click <strong>Object</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Fork and the <strong>self</strong> input pin of ReadStructuralFeatureAction.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Connecting the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the Fork" ac:alt="Connecting the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the Fork"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavda542b6684ebbe2004005e0d7f47ee80.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(iii) </strong><strong>Incrementing </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>time v</strong><strong>alue </strong><strong>by o</strong><strong>ne </strong><strong>using </strong><strong>an </strong><strong>Opaque Behavior Action</strong></p><p>We need an OpaqueBehaviorAction to increase the time value. An OpaqueBehaviorAction receives an integer value as its input parameter and returns the integer value, which is the value of the input parameter that has been incremented by one. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create an Opaque Behavior for the <strong>increaseTime </strong>Activity</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Opaque </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Behavior</strong>. A new Opaque Behavior will be created under the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity.</li><li>Name it <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;increase&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increase</strong> Opaque Behavior in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Parameters</strong> node in the Specification window and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create</strong> button . A new parameter will be created for the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increase</strong> Opaque Behavior, and the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Parameter </strong>Specification window will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating Parameters for the Opaque Behavior" ac:alt="Creating Parameters for the Opaque Behavior"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Name the parameter <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;ti&quot;</em>, select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong> as its type, and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">in</strong> as its direction.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specification of parameter" ac:alt="Specification of parameter"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-parameter.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li> Click the <strong>Back</strong> button to return to the Specification window of the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior.</li><li>Repeat the same steps used to create the parameter <strong>ti</strong> to create another parameter, which will be a return parameter of the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior.</li><li>Name the return parameter <em>&quot;to&quot;</em>, select <strong>Integer</strong> as its type, and select <strong>return</strong> as its direction.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-parameter-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Back</strong> button to return to the Specification window of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increase</strong> Opaque Behavior. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once the parameters &quot;ti&quot; and &quot;to&quot; have been created, we will write a simple JavaScript that will be executed during the simulation. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a simple JavaScript to be executed in the simulation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior's Specification window and select the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior node on the left-hand side of the dialog.</li><li><p>Type a JavaScript into the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Body</strong> row, e.g., <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;to=ti+1&quot;</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Typing a JavaScript for the Increase Opaque Behavior" ac:alt="Typing a JavaScript for the Increase Opaque Behavior"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfffd00c-b473-40d1-9048-d244bd880c3a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can use any scripting language that is supported by JSR-223. To specify the scripting language that you are going to use, type it directly in the <strong>Language</strong> row. JavaScript will be used by default if no scripting language is specified.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong> to close the Specification window. </li><li>Create an OpaqueBehaviorAction on the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity by dragging the created <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity diagram. A new OpaqueBehaviorAction will be created with the pins that correspond to the defined parameters <strong>ti</strong> and <strong>to</strong>.</li><li>Name the created OpaqueBehaviorAction &quot;inc&quot;.</li><li>Click <strong>Object</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the output pin <strong>t</strong> of the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the input pin <strong>ti</strong> of the <strong>inc:increase</strong> OpaqueBehaviorAction .<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Adding the Increase Opaque Behavior to the IncreaseTime Activity" ac:alt="Adding the Increase Opaque Behavior to the IncreaseTime Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddava7acc324d5817b8f773c8d3f58b0a8d1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(iv) Setting </strong><strong>the i</strong><strong>ncreased </strong><strong>time </strong><strong>value </strong><strong>to the </strong><strong>object </strong><strong>of </strong><strong>StopWatch </strong><strong>using </strong><strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong></p><p>Once the time value has been increased using the <strong>inc:increase</strong> OpaqueBehaviorAction, you need to set it back to the Object of <strong>StopWatch</strong>. To do this, you can follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Adding an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to Set the Increased Time Value" ac:alt="Adding an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to Set the Increased Time Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav66cd1d3f270c7771a4959ddbeac8863f.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /></p><p>To set the increased time value to the object of StopWatch</p><hr /><ol><li>Follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram.</li><li>Get the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to set the value to the time:Integer of StopWatch. Make sure that the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Is</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Replace</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">All</strong> of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction is set to <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">true</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Object</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect from the output pin <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">to</strong> of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">inc:increase</strong> OpaqueBehaviorAction to the input pin <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">t</strong> of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction (see the steps required for resetting the time value of the StopWatch object to zero with AddStructuralFeatureValueAction in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ac:link>).</li><li>Add an Initial Node and an Activity Final stage to the increaseTime Activity and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Control</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Flow </strong>on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect between the Actions. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="A Complete Model of the IncreaseTime Activity" ac:alt="A Complete Model of the IncreaseTime Activity" ac:height="430" ac:width="475"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavff9d9f7f43a5f3ceeb2fce67dc48f4af.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(v)</strong><strong>Setting </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>specification </strong><strong>of </strong><strong>the i</strong><strong>ncrease time </strong><strong>Activity </strong><strong>to the </strong><strong>IncreaseTime </strong><strong>Operation</strong><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To set the specification of the increaseTime Activity to the increaseTime operation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity by right-clicking the <strong>increaseTime </strong>Activity in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong>.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> row and click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-dot-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Element</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> operation of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class as the specification of the Activity.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong> to close the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Element</strong> dialog.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> to close the Specification window.</li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729396 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00060: Creating a model for Activity simulation

- page_id: `136729396`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729396/Creating+a+model+for+Activity+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:10.845+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['model-for-activity-simulation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

142598154

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

142598157

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

142598156

You can simulate a UML Activity or a Classifier whose Classifier Behavior is defined by an Activity. This section demonstrates how to create a simple but executable Activity model through the following steps

1. Create a Class containing two properties typed by Integers.
2. Create an Activity to print the summation value of the two properties.
3. Assign the Activity as the Classifier Behavior of the created Class.
4. Create an opaque Behavior to print the summation value of two input parameters of type Integer .
5. Write a script to print the summation of the given integer values that are referred to by the two input parameters.
6. Complete the Activity diagram of the Class.
7. Create a ReadSelfAction to read a runtime object that will be supplied to the input pins of both the readX and readY Actions.
8. Create an InstanceSpecification and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the two created properties.

To create a Class containing two attributes typed by Integers

1. To create a new *UML* project, click **File** > **New Project** on the main menu. The **New Project**dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='cst-new-proj.png']
2. Select **Simulation****Project** from the **Simulation** group and specify the project's name, e.g., "SimpleActivitySimulation".
3. Specify the location where you want to save your project file, and then click **OK**.
4. Right-click the **Model** model in the containment browser and select **Create Element** > **Class**. A new Class element, which is the context of the Activity, will be created in the containment browser. Name the created Class, e.g., "SumPrinter".
5. Add two properties: **x** and **y** of type **Integer**. 
Right-click the **SumPrinter** Class and select **Create Element** > **Property**. Type 'x' to name the property (see the first figure below). Right-click **x** and select **Specification** to open its Specification window. Select **Integer** as the property type (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Creating a new Property x for the SumPrinter Class. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Specification of Property x. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The SumPrinter Class with the Properties X and Y of Integer Type.
6. Repeat Step 5 to create Property **y**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Once the Properties **x** and **y** have been created, define the Behavior of the created Class: Specify the Classifier Behavior of the **SumPrinter**Class with a UML Activity element.

To create an Activity to print the summation value of the two properties

1. Right-click the SumPrinter Class in the containment browser and select New Diagram > Activity Diagram to create a new Activity under it.
2. Name the diagram "PrintSum".

Now that the Activity has been created, you need to assign it as the Classifier Behavior of SumPrinter.

To assign the Activity as the Classifier Behavior of the created Class

1. Right-click the **SumPrinter** Class in the containment browser and select **Specification** to open its Specification window. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select All from the Properties drop-down menu to make sure that all of the properties are listed in the dialog.
3. Click Classifier Behavior and select the PrintSum Activity from the drop-down list on the right-hand side.

To create an opaque Behavior to print the summation value of the two input parameters of type **Integer**:

1. Right-click the Model model in the Containment tree and select Create Element > Opaque Behavior . A new opaque Behavior will be created under the Model model.
2. Name it "PrintSumOfIntegers". The following figure shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment browser. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Add two input parameters of type Integer : a and b .
4. Right-click the **PrintSumOfIntegers** opaque Behavior and select **Create****Element**> **Parameter**. Name the created parameter 'a' in the name field and select **Integer**as the type of Parameter **a** (see the following figure that shows the Specification of Parameter **a**). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Repeat Steps 3-4 to create Parameter **b** as shown in the following figure that shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior containing Parameters **a** and **b** in the Containment browser. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To write a script to print the summation of the given integer values

- Open the Specification window of the **PrintSumOfIntegers** opaque Behavior and write a script in the **Body** field (You can use any scripting language that is supported by MagicDraw's Macro Engine, e.g., BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, or Python). In this example, JavaScript will be used to print the summation of the given integer values that are referred to by the parameters **a** and **b**; therefore, the script will be: "print(a+b)". [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If you want to use a scripting language other than JavaScript, you can specify it in the **Language**attribute of the Specification window.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specifying a Scripting Language in the Specification dialog.

The next step is to complete the **PrintSum** Activity diagram of the **SumPrinter** Class and add a **ReadStructuralFeatureAction** so that the values of properties **x** and **y**, which are owned by the **SumPrinter** Class, are readable. The values of **a** and **b** will later be passed on to the **PrintSumOfIntegers** opaque Behavior as the values of input parameters **a** and **b** respectively.

To complete the Activity diagram of the Class

1. Drag the PrintSumOfIntegers opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the PrintSum Activity diagram. A new Action of PrintSumOfIntegers will be created.
2. Name the Action "print". [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Add the Initial and Activity Final nodes to the Activity diagram and connect them to the print Action using a control flow. The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Initial and Final Activity nodes. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click **Action** and select **Any****Action** from the **Activity Diagram** toolbar on the **PrintSum** Activity diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  1. Select **Read Structural Feature****Action** in the **Select Action MetaClass** dialog. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Click the **PrintSum** Activity diagram to create the Action and name it "readX" as shown in the following figure [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Open the Specification window of the Action **readX**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Click the **Structural Feature** and the**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** button to open the **Select Property** dialog to select **Structural Feature**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Select the Property **x** of the **SumPrinter** Class and click **OK**. The **Select Property** dialog will close.
9. Create an object flow from the pin **result** of the **readX** Action to pin **a** of the **print** Action. The following figure shows the Flow between readX and print Actions in the Activity diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. Repeat Steps 4 to 10 to create another Action named **readY**, which is the **ReadStructuralFeatureAction**, with the following arrangements **•** The name of the Action is "readY". **•** The structural feature is the attribute 'y' of the **SumPrinter** Class. **•** The name of the output pin of **readY** is 'b'. **•** The output pin **b** of **readY** connects to pin **b** of the **print** Action. The following figure shows the Activity diagram with readX and readY Actions [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create a ReadSelfAction to read a runtime object that will be supplied to the input pins of **readX** and **readY** Actions

1. Click **Action** > **Any****Action** on the Activity Diagram toolbar. The **Select****Action****MetaClass**dialog will open (see the following figure). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select **Read Self****Action**.
3. Click the **PrintSum** Activity diagram to create an Action and name it, for example, **readSelf.**The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Action readSelf. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Create a **Fork****Horizontal** and use **Object****Flow** to connect it to the pins of the Actions **readX**, **readY**, and **readSelf** on the diagrams. The following figure shows a complete PrintSum Activity diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The final step is to create an **InstanceSpecification** whose Classifier is the **SumPrinter** and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the properties **x** and **y**. These values will be used during the simulation. 
 
To create an **InstanceSpecification** whose Classifier is the **SumPrinter** and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the Properties **x** and **y**

1. Right-click the Model model and select Create Element > InstanceSpecification .
2. Name the created InstanceSpecification, e.g., **instance**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the created **Instance** and open the Specification window of **instance**.
4. Click the Classifier field and the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The **Select Elements** dialog will open. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Select the **SumPrinter** Class to edit the Classifier and click **OK**.
6. Click **Slots** on the left-hand side pane of the Specification window and select **x:Integer**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Click the **Create****Value** button to create a new value of the slot. The **Value** box will open for you to assign the value to Property x slot. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Type a number, e.g., 2 as the value of the property **x** slot.
9. Repeat Steps 6 to 8 to assign "8" as the value of the property **y** slot as shown in the following figure and click **Close**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The model is now ready for you to simulate.

142598153

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Classifier Behavior property' space='CST2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="74e8ecaf-b93c-4053-b8a5-c918988c8fb4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">142598154</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc3bf126-b37d-4975-a411-1ca92ef7bc77"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">142598157</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="533855bd-5cd7-473c-bee2-cbcf5b1a16a3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">142598156</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can simulate a UML Activity or a Classifier whose Classifier Behavior is defined by an Activity. This section demonstrates how to create a simple but executable Activity model through the following steps</p><ol><li>Create a Class containing two properties typed by Integers.</li><li>Create an Activity to print the summation value of the two properties.</li><li>Assign the Activity as the Classifier Behavior of the created Class.</li><li>Create an opaque Behavior to print the summation value of two input parameters of type <strong>Integer</strong>.</li><li>Write a script to print the summation of the given integer values that are referred to by the two input parameters.</li><li>Complete the Activity diagram of the Class.</li><li>Create a <strong>ReadSelfAction</strong> to read a runtime object that will be supplied to the input pins of both the <strong>readX</strong> and <strong>readY</strong> Actions. </li><li>Create an <strong>InstanceSpecification</strong> and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the two created properties.<span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> </span></li></ol><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><br /></span></p><p>To create a Class containing two attributes typed by Integers</p><hr /><ol><li><p>To create a new <em>UML</em> project, click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>New Project </strong>dialog will open.</p><ac:image ac:title="The New Project Dialog" ac:alt="The New Project Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-new-proj.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p>Select <strong>Simulation</strong><strong>Project</strong> from the <strong>Simulation</strong> group and specify the project's name, e.g., &quot;SimpleActivitySimulation&quot;.</p></li><li><p>Specify the location where you want to save your project file, and then click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Right-click the <strong>Model</strong> model in the containment browser and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Class</strong>. A new Class element, which is the context of the Activity, will be created in the containment browser. Name the created Class, e.g., &quot;SumPrinter&quot;.</p></li><li><p>Add two properties: <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong> of type <strong>Integer</strong>. <br />Right-click the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Property</strong>. Type 'x' to name the property (see the first figure below). Right-click <strong>x</strong> and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its Specification window. Select <strong>Integer</strong> as the property type (see the second figure below).</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="New project dialog" ac:alt="New project dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="new-project-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Creating a new Property x for the SumPrinter Class.</h6><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating a New Property 'x' for the SumPrinter Class" ac:alt="Creating a New Property 'x' for the SumPrinter Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0042.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Specification of Property
````
