x.</h6><h6><ac:image ac:title="Specification for property" ac:alt="Specification for property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-property.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6> The SumPrinter Class with the Properties X and Y of Integer Type.</h6></li><li><p>Repeat Step 5 to create Property <strong>y</strong>.<br /><ac:image ac:title="SumPrinter Class with Properties X and Y of Integer Type" ac:alt="SumPrinter Class with Properties X and Y of Integer Type"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0042.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><p>Once the Properties <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong> have been created, define the Behavior of the created Class: Specify the Classifier Behavior of the <strong>SumPrinter </strong>Class with a UML Activity element.</p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create an Activity to print the summation value of the two properties</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong>New </strong><strong>Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong>Activity </strong><strong>Diagram</strong> to create a new Activity under it.</li><li>Name the diagram &quot;PrintSum&quot;.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>Now that the Activity has been created, you need to assign it as the Classifier Behavior of SumPrinter. </p><p><br /></p><p>To assign the Activity as the Classifier Behavior of the created Class </p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its Specification window. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Specify classifier behavior property" ac:alt="Specify classifier behavior property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-class-sum-printer.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Select <strong>All</strong> from the <strong>Properties</strong> drop-down menu to make sure that all of the properties are listed in the dialog.</li><li>Click <strong>Classifier </strong><strong>Behavior</strong> and select the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity from the drop-down list on the right-hand side.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create an opaque Behavior to print the summation value of the two input parameters of type <strong>Integer</strong>: </p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>Model</strong> model in the Containment tree and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Opaque </strong><strong>Behavior</strong>. A new opaque Behavior will be created under the <strong>Model</strong> model.</li><li><p>Name it &quot;PrintSumOfIntegers&quot;. The following figure shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment browser.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment Browser" ac:alt="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SetNameOfPrintSumOfIntegers.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </h6></li><li>Add two input parameters of type <strong>Integer</strong>: <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong>.</li><li><p>Right-click the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior and select <strong>Create </strong><strong>Element </strong>&gt; <strong>Parameter</strong>. Name the created parameter 'a' in the name field and select <strong>Integer </strong>as the type of Parameter <strong>a</strong> (see the following figure that shows the Specification of Parameter <strong>a</strong>). </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Specification Dialog of Parameter a" ac:alt="Specification Dialog of Parameter a"><ri:attachment ri:filename="zecification-of-parameter-a.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Repeat Steps 3-4 to create Parameter <strong>b</strong> as shown in the following figure that shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior containing Parameters <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> in the Containment browser.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior Containing Parameters a and bin the Containment Browser" ac:alt="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior Containing Parameters a and bin the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="OpaqueBehaviorWithAB.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p>To write a script to print the summation of the given integer values</p><hr /><ul><li><p>Open the Specification window of the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior and write a script in the <strong>Body</strong> field (You can use any scripting language that is supported by MagicDraw's Macro Engine, e.g., BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, or Python). In this example, JavaScript will be used to print the summation of the given integer values that are referred to by the parameters <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong>; therefore, the script will be: &quot;print(a+b)&quot;. </p><h6><br /><ac:image ac:title="JavaScript forPrinting the Summation ofInteger Values" ac:alt="JavaScript forPrinting the Summation ofInteger Values"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ul><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc7b5dd2-f737-4141-89a2-84c4c8aa629a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you want to use a scripting language other than JavaScript, you can specify it in the <strong>Language </strong>attribute of the Specification window.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Specifying a Scripting Language" ac:alt="Specifying a Scripting Language"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6>Specifying a Scripting Language in the Specification dialog.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The next step is to complete the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class and add a <strong>ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong> so that the values of properties <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong>, which are owned by the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class, are readable. The values of <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> will later be passed on to the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior as the values of input parameters <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> respectively. </p><p><br /></p><p>To complete the Activity diagram of the Class </p><hr /><ol><li>Drag the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram. A new Action of <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> will be created.</li><li><p>Name the Action &quot;print&quot;. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Dragging the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior totheActivityDiagram toCreate a PrintAction" ac:alt="Dragging the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior totheActivityDiagram toCreate a PrintAction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DND_PrintSumOfIntegers_andNamedWithPrint.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Add the Initial and Activity Final nodes to the Activity diagram and connect them to the print Action using a control flow. The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Initial and Final Activity nodes.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with Initial and Final Activity Nodes" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with Initial and Final Activity Nodes"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddInitial_ActivityFinal_to_print.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Click <strong>Action</strong> and select <strong>Any </strong><strong>Action</strong> from the <strong>Activity Diagram</strong> toolbar on the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="SelectingAnyAction from theActivityDiagram Toolbar" ac:alt="SelectingAnyAction from theActivityDiagram Toolbar"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddAnyActionToolbarButton.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><ol><li><p>Select <strong>Read Structural Feature </strong><strong>Action</strong> in the <strong>Select Action MetaClass</strong> dialog. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting ReadStructuralFeatureAction in the SelectAction Metaclass Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting ReadStructuralFeatureAction in the SelectAction Metaclass Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="readStructFeatAction.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li></ol></li><li><p>Click the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram to create the Action and name it &quot;readX&quot; as shown in the following figure</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with Action readX" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with Action readX"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CreateReadXAction.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Open the Specification window of the Action <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">readX</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Specification Dialog of ReadStructuralFeatureAction readX" ac:alt="The Specification Dialog of ReadStructuralFeatureAction readX"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-read-structural-feature.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Structural Feature</strong> and the<strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2015-12-02 15_31_56-Specification of State __.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> button to open the <strong>Select Property</strong> dialog to select <strong>Structural Feature</strong>.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting the Property x in the Select Property Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting the Property x in the Select Property Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-read-structural-feature-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Select the Property <strong>x</strong> of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class and click <strong>OK</strong>. The <strong>Select Property</strong> dialog will close.</p></li><li><p>Create an object flow from the pin <strong>result</strong> of the <strong>readX</strong> Action to pin <strong>a</strong> of the <strong>print</strong> Action. The following figure shows the Flow between readX and print Actions in the Activity diagram.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram Showing the Flow between readX and print Actions" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram Showing the Flow between readX and print Actions"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ConnectReadXToPrint.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Repeat Steps 4 to 10 to create another Action named <strong>readY</strong>, which is the <strong>ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong>, with the following arrangements</p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The name of the Action is &quot;readY&quot;. </p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The structural feature is the attribute 'y' of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class. </p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The name of the output pin of <strong>readY</strong> is 'b'. </p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The output pin <strong>b</strong> of <strong>readY</strong> connects to pin <strong>b</strong> of the <strong>print</strong> Action.</p><p> The following figure shows the Activity diagram with readX and readY Actions</p><p><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with readX and readY Actions" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with readX and readY Actions"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddReadY.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create a ReadSelfAction to read a runtime object that will be supplied to the input pins of <strong>readX</strong> and <strong>readY</strong> Actions</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any </strong><strong>Action</strong> on the Activity Diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action </strong><strong>MetaClass </strong>dialog will open (see the following figure). </p><p><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Read Self Action from the Select Action Metaclass Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting Read Self Action from the Select Action Metaclass Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="readselfaction.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Select <strong>Read Self</strong><strong>Action</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Click the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram to create an Action and name it, for example, <strong>readSelf. </strong>The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Action readSelf.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with Action readSelf" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with Action readSelf"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddReadSelfAction.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Create a <strong>Fork </strong><strong>Horizontal</strong> and use <strong>Object </strong><strong>Flow</strong> to connect it to the pins of the Actions <strong>readX</strong>, <strong>readY</strong>, and <strong>readSelf</strong> on the diagrams. The following figure shows a complete PrintSum Activity diagram.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="A Complete PrintSum Activity Diagram" ac:alt="A Complete PrintSum Activity Diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CompletePrintSumActivity.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p>The final step is to create an <strong>InstanceSpecification</strong> whose Classifier is the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the properties <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong>. These values will be used during the simulation.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create an <strong>InstanceSpecification</strong> whose Classifier is the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the Properties <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong> </p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>Model</strong> model and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>InstanceSpecification</strong>.</li><li><p>Name the created InstanceSpecification, e.g., <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">instance</strong>.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="The Created InstanceSpecification in the Containment Browser" ac:alt="The Created InstanceSpecification in the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NameInstanceSpecificationWithInstance.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Right-click the created <strong>Instance</strong> and open the Specification window of <strong>instance</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Click the Classifier field and the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2015-12-02 15_31_56-Specification of State __.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. The <strong>Select Elements</strong> dialog will open.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Select Elements Dialog of the Selected Classifier" ac:alt="The Select Elements Dialog of the Selected Classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-elements-dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Select the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class to edit the Classifier and click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Click <strong>Slots</strong> on the left-hand side pane of the Specification window and select <strong>x:Integer</strong>.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating a Slot Value of x" ac:alt="Creating a Slot Value of x"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-instance-slots.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Create </strong><strong>Value</strong> button to create a new value of the slot. The <strong>Value</strong> box will open for you to assign the value to Property x slot.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Assigning a Value to Property x Slot" ac:alt="Assigning a Value to Property x Slot"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-instance-slots-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Type a number, e.g., 2 as the value of the property <strong>x</strong> slot.</p></li><li><p>Repeat Steps 6 to 8 to assign &quot;8&quot; as the value of the property <strong>y</strong> slot as shown in the following figure and click <strong>Close</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Created InstanceSpecification with Slot Values in the Containment Tree" ac:alt="The Created InstanceSpecification with Slot Values in the Containment Tree"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InstanceSpecificationOfSumPrinter.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The model is now ready for you to simulate. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3c03e720-db13-4a6d-96c6-2f45192eabaa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">142598153</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Class" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729551 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00061: Creating a model for interaction simulation

- page_id: `136729551`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729551/Creating+a+model+for+interaction+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:17.365+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation
- labels: ['model-for-interaction-simulation', 'interaction-simulation-model']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

384506323

384506325

384506324

You can simulate an Interaction element, a (UML or SysML) Sequence diagram, or a Classifier whose Classifier Behavior is defined by an Interaction. This section demonstrates how to create a simple executable Interaction model through the following steps

- Create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes.
- Create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in (i) , for printing the summation of two Integer input parameters.
- Write a script to print the summation of the two input parameters.
- Create an operation, owned by the properties specified in (i) , and specify the opaque Behavior in (ii) as its method.
- Create an Interaction as the Classifier Behavior of the Class specified in (i) .
- Create call message to call operation specified in (iv) by another property.

classTo create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes

1. To create a new UML project, click **File** > **New Project** on the main menu. The **New Project**dialog will open. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select UML Project from the General-Purpose Modeling group and specify the project's name, e.g., 'SimpleSequenceExecution'.
3. Specify the location where you want to save your project file and click OK .
4. Right-click the **Model** node in the Containment tree and select **Create** **Element** > **Class**. A new Class element will be created. Name the created Class, e.g., 'System'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Create two more Classes and name them, e.g., 'a' and 'b.
6. Right-click the System model in the Containment tree and select Create Element > Property to create property for the Class. Name the property, e.g., 'a1'. [ATTACHMENT filename='cst-containment-tree-b.png']
7. Right-click **a1** and select the Specification window. Select **A** as the property type. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Repeat step 6 to create property **b1** of type **B** (see the following figure). [IMAGE alt='' src='']

opaqueTo create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for interaction simulation' space='CST2024x']), to print the summation of two Integer input parameters

1. Right-click the Class **B** and select **Create Element** > **Opaque** **Behavior**. Name the opaque Behavior, e.g., 'Add'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the opaque Behavior **Add** and select **Create Element** > **Parameter**to add a parameter element to the opaque Behavior. Name the parameter, e.g., 'par1'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the parameter **par1** and select the Specification window. Select **Integer** as the parameter type. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Repeat Step 2 to create another parameter and name it, e.g., 'par2' of type **Integer**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

scriptTo write a script to print the summation of the two input parameters

- Open the Specification window of the opaque Behavior **Add** and write a script in the **Body** field (you can use any scripting language that is supported by MagicDraw Macro Engine, e.g., BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, or Python). In this example, JavaScript is used: print(par1+par2); to print the summation of the two Integer parameters **par1** and **par2**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Specification window of an Opaque Behavior.

operationTo create an operation owned by the properties specified in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for interaction simulation' space='CST2024x'], and to specify the opaque Behavior as in ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for interaction simulation' space='CST2024x']) as its method

1. Right-click **B** and select **Create** **Element** > **Operation**to add an operation element to the Class. Name the operation, e.g., 'Add'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the operation **Add** and select **Create Element** > **Parameter**to add a parameter to the operation element. Name the parameter, e.g., 'par1'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the parameter **par1** and select **Specification**. Select **Integer** as the parameter type and **in** as the parameter direction. The Specification window of parameter par1 will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Repeat Step 2 and 3 to create another parameter. Name it, e.g., **par2** of type **Integer**, having **in** as its direction. The parameters associated with the Operation will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Right-click the operation Add and select Specification . The Specification window will open.
6. Select the opaque Behavior **Add** as the **Method** of the operation. The Specification window of the Opaque Behavior will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The parameters associated with an Operation in the Containment tree will be as follows. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create an Interaction as the Classifier Behavior of the Class specified in ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for interaction simulation' space='CST2024x'])

1. Right-click **System** and select **New** **Diagram** > **Sequence** **Diagram**. Select all properties in the **Display Lifelines** dialog and click **OK**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the Class System in the containment browser and select Specification to open its Specification window.
3. Make sure that the Interaction **System** is the Classifier Behavior of the Class **System**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create a call message to call the operation specified in ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for interaction simulation' space='CST2024x']) by another property

1. Double-click the Interaction **System** to open the Sequence diagram containing two lifelines: **a1** and **b1**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select **Call** **Message** from the **Diagram Modeling** **Elements** toolbar and create a call message from **a1** to **b1**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']|
3. Double-click the call message created in Step 2 to open the Specification window and select the operation **Add** as the **Signature****(operation)** of the call message. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Select **Argument** on the left-hand side of the dialog to specify a value of the element. Type, e.g., **4** and **5** as the values of parameters **par1** and **par2** respectively. The Specification window of a call message showing its Arguments tags will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src=''] A Sequence diagram showing a call message between two lifelines will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']

384506322

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported elements in interaction simulation' space='CST2024x']

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8c14047-83ba-4568-be08-678320259b52"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">384506323</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fde4b27e-0b7d-4ea7-8059-518a6ccb5542"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">384506325</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b9cda626-9283-44de-8366-9124e747e921"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">384506324</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can simulate an Interaction element, a (UML or SysML) Sequence diagram, or a Classifier whose Classifier Behavior is defined by an Interaction. This section demonstrates how to create a simple executable Interaction model through the following steps</p><ul><li>Create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes.</li><li>Create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);">(i)</span>, for printing the summation of two Integer input parameters.</li><li>Write a script to print the summation of the two input parameters.</li><li>Create an operation, owned by the properties specified in <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);">(i)</span>, and specify the opaque Behavior in (ii) as its method.</li><li>Create an Interaction as the Classifier Behavior of the Class specified in <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);">(i)</span>.</li><li>Create call message to call operation specified in (iv) by another property.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c059179-953a-43ed-989a-250a796cd57d"><ac:parameter ac:name="">class</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes </p><hr /><ol><li><p>To create a new UML project, click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>New Project </strong>dialog will open.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The New Project Dialog" ac:alt="The New Project Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ew-uml-project.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Select <strong>UML Project</strong> from the <strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> group and specify the project's name, e.g., 'SimpleSequenceExecution'.</li><li>Specify the location where you want to save your project file and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>Right-click the <strong>Model</strong> node in the Containment tree and select <strong>Create</strong> <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Class</strong>. A new Class element will be created. Name the created Class, e.g., 'System'. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating a New Class Named System" ac:alt="Creating a New Class Named System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Create two more Classes and name them, e.g., 'a' and 'b.</li><li>Right-click the <strong>System</strong> model in the Containment tree and select <strong>Create</strong> <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Property </strong>to create property for the Class. Name the property, e.g., 'a1'.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating a New Property a1 for the System Class" ac:alt="Creating a New Property a1 for the System Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree-b.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p>Right-click <strong>a1</strong> and select the Specification window. Select <strong>A</strong> as the property type.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Class A as Property Type" ac:alt="Selecting Class A as Property Type"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-property-a.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Repeat step 6 to create property <strong>b1</strong> of type <strong>B</strong> (see the following figure).</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="System Class with Properties a1of Type A and b1 of Type B" ac:alt="System Class with Properties a1of Type A and b1 of Type B"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree-c.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11a424ab-8ea0-46d1-84ef-d797ca270964"><ac:parameter ac:name="">opaque</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in (<ac:link ac:anchor="class"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ac:link>), to print the summation of two Integer input parameters</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click the Class <strong>B</strong> and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Opaque</strong> <strong>Behavior</strong>. Name the opaque Behavior, e.g., 'Add'.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Opaque BehaviorAdd Owned by Class Bunder the Data Model" ac:alt="Opaque BehaviorAdd Owned by Class Bunder the Data Model"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree-d.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Right-click the opaque Behavior <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Add</strong> and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Parameter </strong>to add a parameter element to the opaque Behavior. Name the parameter, e.g., 'par1'. </p><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation7.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Right-click the parameter <strong>par1</strong> and select the Specification window. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729997 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 00062: Creating a new execution engine

- page_id: `136729997`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729997/Creating+a+new+execution+engine
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:42.904+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Developer Guide > Java APIs > Engine
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When creating an execution engine, you are required to implement three abstract methods in the Subclass of ExecutionEngine (see JavaDoc for more details). These abstract methods are as follows

```text

```

API developers can insert any desired code into the above abstract methods and get the events when the execution engine is initialized, executed, and terminated. 
 You can execute the following code to create a MyExecutionEngine class

```text

```

The created class MyExecutionEngine must be returned from createEngine() of its descriptor as defined in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating an execution engine descriptor' space='CST2024x'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When creating an execution engine, you are required to implement three abstract methods in the Subclass of ExecutionEngine (see JavaDoc for more details). These abstract methods are as follows<br /> <span style="color: rgb(153,51,102);"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6dab922e-1f3f-47d9-a939-2d7f2e34ae4c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	public abstract class ExecutionEngine {
		...
		public abstract void init(Element element);
		public abstract void execute(Element element);
		public abstract void onClose();
		...
	} ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> API developers can insert any desired code into the above abstract methods and get the events when the execution engine is initialized, executed, and terminated. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> You can execute the following code to create a MyExecutionEngine class</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c5c36737-bb57-4b71-b927-fe7663c85a59"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	public class MyExecutionEngine extends ExecutionEngine {

		public MyExecutionEngine(ExecutionEngineDescriptor engineDescriptor) {
			super(engineDescriptor);
		} 
	
		@Override
		public void execute(Element element) {
		...
		} 
		
		@Override
		public void init(Element element) {
			Project project = Project.getProject(element);
			// add engine listener if needed
			EngineListener listener = new MyEngineListener(project);
			addEngineListener(listener);
		} 

		@Override
		public void onClose() {
		...
		}
	} ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The created class MyExecutionEngine must be returned from createEngine() of its descriptor as defined in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating an execution engine descriptor" /></ac:link>.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730011 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00063: Creating a new UML project

- page_id: `136730011`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730011/Creating+a+new+UML+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:43.875+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

First of all, we will need to create a new MagicDraw project. 
 
 To create a new MagicDraw project

1. Click File > _GoBack New Project on the main menu. The New Project dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='new-project-a.png']
2. Select UML Project from the General-Purpose Modeling group and enter the project name and location. The project name will be " StopWatch" in this example.
3. Click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>First of all, we will need to create a new MagicDraw project. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To create a new MagicDraw project</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt;<ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a73b1aa7-9b06-4c1d-a83a-c64841b95121"><ac:parameter ac:name="">_GoBack</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro> <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>New Project</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The New Project Dialog" ac:alt="The New Project Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="new-project-a.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a new UML project" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">UML</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Project</strong> from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">General-Purpose</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Modeling</strong> group and enter the project name and location. The project name will be &quot;<em style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch&quot;</em> in this example.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>. </li></ol>
````


If an instance of the com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Class has been passed on to the parameter "a", you can create an object through the specified Class directly as follows

Note that if a parameter is equal to null, execution will be thrown.

You can use the following API to create a Java ArrayList.

```text
createList()
ArrayList createList(Object object)]]>
```

A Java List will be required under certain circumstances, e.g., if you want to create Argument values that will be passed to some calling Behaviors or operations. In scripting languages, the built-in functions importPackage and importClass can be used to import the Java packages and Classes.

The following code fragment shows how to create a Java ArrayList in the scripting language using a "new" operator

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to create the Java ArrayList in the scripting language with ALH

```text

```

Alternatively, you can use an initial value as the constructor's parameter.

```text

```

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- Only one initial value of the list is allowed.
- Variable-length argument lists are not supported.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730010 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00066: Creating an executable stopwatch model

- page_id: `136730010`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730010/Creating+an+executable+stopwatch+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:43.799+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

798415423

798415426

798415424

This section guides you through the steps required to create a stopwatch model in Cameo Simulation Toolkit. You will learn how to create a new UML project as the first step, followed by creating the stopwatch structure through defining the stopwatch Classifier and its structural features and adding a Time attribute. In the next step, you will define the stopwatch Classifier Behavior by using the State Machine Behavior (shown in this example). You will also be guided through creating an initial stage, the ready, running, paused, stopped and final States, transition, Package of Signal elements, and Signal Events for transitions for the stopwatch State Machine diagram. The last two sections explain new operations required for defining the elapsed time and adding Activities to the ready and running States, resetting the elapsed time to zero, and increasing the elapsed time by one.

798415422

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

You will learn how to create a new UML project as the first step, followed by creating the stopwatch structure through defining the stopwatch Classifier and its structural features and adding a Time attribute. In the next step, you will define the stopwatch Classifier Behavior by using the State Machine Behavior (shown in this example). You will also be guided through creating an initial stage, the ready, running, paused, stopped and final States, transition, Package of Signal elements, and Signal Events for transitions for the stopwatch State Machine diagram. The last two sections explain new operations required for defining the elapsed time and adding Activities to the ready and running States, resetting the elapsed time to zero, and increasing the elapsed time by one.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f0ce5a6e-1dca-4fdb-8735-76ba3ac9f1c8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">798415422</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="8d22cdbe-c271-4829-815e-6566983feaaf" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00067: Creating an execution engine descriptor

- page_id: `136729998`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729998/Creating+an+execution+engine+descriptor
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:42.979+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Developer Guide > Java APIs > Engine
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following example shows how to create an Execution Engine Descriptor

```text
getValidationSuite();
	}]]>
```

The new execution engine descriptor must implement the ExecutionEngineDescriptor or extend the AbstractExecutionEngineDescriptor abstract Class. You can find details about these Classes in JavaDoc.

```text

```

The created execution engine descriptor must then be registered to the simulation manager (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Registering an execution engine to the Simulation Manager' space='CST2024x']).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The following example shows how to create an Execution Engine Descriptor<br /> <span style="color: rgb(127,0,85);"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1717ad49-21fb-46b4-9eef-5f836bc57e86"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	public interface ExecutionEngineDescriptor {
		String getEngineName();
		ImageIcon getEngineIcon();
		boolean canExecute(Element element);
		ExecutionEngine createEngine();
		boolean canAnimate(PresentationElement element);
		boolean isAutoDiagramOpened();
		boolean canDebug();
		boolean canUserTriggerEvents();
		boolean isDiagramPerSession();
		boolean isHasIdle();
		boolean isFindEngine(Element element);
		List<? Extends ValidationSuite> getValidationSuite();
	}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> </p><p>The new execution engine descriptor must implement the ExecutionEngineDescriptor or extend the AbstractExecutionEngineDescriptor abstract Class. You can find details about these Classes in JavaDoc.<br /> <span style="color: rgb(127,0,85);"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="300849dc-2a03-4030-8de9-bc925d475c22"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	public class MyExecutionEngineDescriptor extends AbstractExecutionEngineDescriptor {
	@Override
	
	public boolean canExecute(Element element) {
		return element instanceof Interaction;
	} 
	
	@Override
	public ExecutionEngine createEngine() {
		return new MyExecutionEngine(this);
	} 

	@Override
	public String getEngineName() {
		return "My Execution Engine";
}
} ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The created execution engine descriptor must then be registered to the simulation manager (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Registering an execution engine to the Simulation Manager" /></ac:link>).</p><p> </p>
````

## PAGE 00068: Creating an execution engine listener

- page_id: `136729999`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729999/Creating+an+execution+engine+listener
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:43.051+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Developer Guide > Java APIs > Engine
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following example shows how to create an engine listener

```text
values) { 
		System.out.printIn("--Activate Element-- : element name = " ((NamedElement)element).getName());
	} 

	@Override
	public void elementDeactivated(Element element, Collection> values) { 
		System.out.printIn("--Deactivate Element-- : element name = " + ((NamedElement)element).getName());
	} 

	@Override
	public void eventTriggered(String eventID) {
		NamedElement element = (NamedElement)project.getElementByID(eventID);
		System.out.printIn("--Event Trigger-- : event name = " + ((NamedElement)element).getName());
	} 

	@Override
	public void executionTerminated() {
		System.out.printIn("--Engine Terminated--");
	}
} ]]>
```

Once you have created an EngineListener, you can register it to the specified ExecutionEngine if you want to receive events occurring in each execution engine. All engine listeners of a specific engine will be activated under the conditions as follows

- An element is activated.
- An element is deactivated.
- An event is triggered.
- An engine is terminated.

```text
values); 
		void elementDeactivated(Element element, Collection> values); 
		void eventTriggered(String eventID); 
		void executionTerminated();	
	}]]>
```

See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a new execution engine' space='CST2024x'] for more information about adding execution engine listeners to the *MyExecutionEngine* class.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can add more than one execution engine listener to an execution engine.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The following example shows how to create an engine listener<br /> <span style="color: rgb(127,0,85);"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a808a382-6c31-4dd6-aeb3-054feb1defd9"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	public class MyEngineListener implements EngineListener {
	
	private Project project; 
		public MyEngineListener(Project project) {
		this.project = project;
	} 

	@Override
	public void elementActivated(Element element, Collection<?> values) { 
		System.out.printIn("--Activate Element-- : element name = " ((NamedElement)element).getName());
	} 

	@Override
	public void elementDeactivated(Element element, Collection<?> values) { 
		System.out.printIn("--Deactivate Element-- : element name = " + ((NamedElement)element).getName());
	} 

	@Override
	public void eventTriggered(String eventID) {
		NamedElement element = (NamedElement)project.getElementByID(eventID);
		System.out.printIn("--Event Trigger-- : event name = " + ((NamedElement)element).getName());
	} 

	@Override
	public void executionTerminated() {
		System.out.printIn("--Engine Terminated--");
	}
} ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once you have created an EngineListener, you can register it to the specified ExecutionEngine if you want to receive events occurring in each execution engine. All engine listeners of a specific engine will be activated under the conditions as follows</p><ul><li>An element is activated.</li><li>An element is deactivated.</li><li>An event is triggered.</li><li>An engine is terminated.</li></ul><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68658132-16ea-476d-8dc7-54965276de92"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	public interface EngineListener { 
		void elementActivated(Element element, Collection<?> values); 
		void elementDeactivated(Element element, Collection<?> values); 
		void eventTriggered(String eventID); 
		void executionTerminated();	
	}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> See <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a new execution engine" /></ac:link> for more information about adding execution engine listeners to the <em>MyExecutionEngine</em> class.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a3f3f95e-b48a-4621-8a08-e79f342933b5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>You can add more than one execution engine listener to an execution engine.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729995 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00069: Creating and registering a new SimulationExecutionListener

- page_id: `136729995`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729995/Creating+and+registering+a+new+SimulationExecutionListener
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:42.760+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Developer Guide > Java APIs > Execution
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A *SimulationExecutionListener* Class is a listener for Events that will be activated during execution of a model. All available Events are listed below.

- An execution is started.
- An element is activated.
- An element is deactivated.
- A signal event is triggered.
- An operation is called.
- A behavior is called.
- A runtime object is created.
- An execution is terminated.

```text
values); 
		void elementDeactivated(Element element, Collection> values); 
		void eventTriggered(SignalInstance signal); 
		void operationCalled(Operation operation, ParameterValueList pvl, Object_ caller, Object_ target, boolean isSynchronous); 
		void behaviorCalled(Behavior behavior, ParameterValueList pvl, Object_ caller, Object_ target, boolean isSynchronous); 
		void objectCreated(Object_ sender, Object_ object); 
		void valueChange(StructuredValue context, FeatureValue feature, Object oldValue, Object newValue); 
		void executionTerminated(SimulationExecution execution); 
		void configLoaded(Element config);
		void busyStatusChange(StructuredValue context, Object oldValue, Object newValue);
	}]]>
```

Once you have created the execution listener, you can register it to a list of global execution listeners.

To register a new execution listener to a global list, type the following code

```text

```

All registered listeners will be cleared and removed automatically when the execution is terminated.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

*ExecutionAdapter* and *ExecutionListener* are not valid in Cameo Simulation Toolkit 19.0 and in later versions.

All open APIs below are used by the Simulation Dashboard plugin and can be added for particular purposes.

| Open API | Function |
| --- | --- |
| beforeObjectDestroyed(Object_ object) | Works with an object-destroying Listener when added into SimulationExecutionListener. |
| objectStateActivated(StructuredValue context, State newState) | Works with a State activation Listener when added into SimulationExecutionListener. |
| SimulationExecutionListener.contextInitialized(SimulationExecution execution) | Gets an Event when objects have been initialized but not started the execution yet. |
| SimulationHelper.findFeatureValue(Object_ runtimeContext, List<Property> propertyPath, Property target) : List<FeatureValue> | Gets featureValue objects that match the given nested property path. |
| SimulationHelper.getAvailableSignals(StructuredValue target, ExecutionEngine engine) | Retrieves available Signals from the specified structured value. |
| SimulationHelper.getConfigElement(SimulationExecution execution) | Gets a Config element. |
| SimulationHelper.isFeatureValueInvalid(FeatureValue featureValue, int index) | Checks the validity of FeatureValue. |
| SimulationManager.getContext(SimulationSession session) : Object_ | Gets the execution context. |
| SimulationManager.registerSimulationExecutionListener(SimulationExecutionListener listener, SimulationExecution execution) | Registers SimulationExecutionListener dynamically during the execution. |
| SimulationManager.unregisterSimulationExecutionListener(SimulationExecutionListener listener)SimulationManager.unregisterSimulationExecutionListener(SimulationExecutionListener listener, SimulationExecution execution) | Unregisters SimulationExecutionListener. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A <em>SimulationExecutionListener</em> Class is a listener for Events that will be activated during execution of a model. All available Events are listed below.</p><ul><li>An execution is started.</li><li>An element is activated.</li><li>An element is deactivated.</li><li>A signal event is triggered.</li><li>An operation is called.</li><li>A behavior is called.</li><li>A runtime object is created.</li><li>An execution is terminated.</li></ul><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95dc93b7-cb68-49a4-8598-d369a3d6b94b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	public class SimulationExecutionListener { 
		void executionStarted(SimulationExecution execution); 
		void elementActivated(Element element, Collection<?> values); 
		void elementDeactivated(Element element, Collection<?> values); 
		void eventTriggered(SignalInstance signal); 
		void operationCalled(Operation operation, ParameterValueList pvl, Object_ caller, Object_ target, boolean isSynchronous); 
		void behaviorCalled(Behavior behavior, ParameterValueList pvl, Object_ caller, Object_ target, boolean isSynchronous); 
		void objectCreated(Object_ sender, Object_ object); 
		void valueChange(StructuredValue context, FeatureValue feature, Object oldValue, Object newValue); 
		void executionTerminated(SimulationExecution execution); 
		void configLoaded(Element config);
		void busyStatusChange(StructuredValue context, Object oldValue, Object newValue);
	}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Once you have created the execution listener, you can register it to a list of global execution listeners.</p><p><br /></p><p>To register a new execution listener to a global list, type the following code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1a92ddc0-3397-4180-ac17-d712734a7034"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[SimulationManager.registerSimulationExecutionListener(listener); //listener is an instance of SimulationExecutionListener.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>All registered listeners will be cleared and removed automatically when the execution is terminated.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95a17ebf-d3f0-4e27-974d-b47db534d6a8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><em>ExecutionAdapter</em> and <em>ExecutionListener</em> are not valid in Cameo Simulation Toolkit 19.0 and in later versions.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p><p>All open APIs below are used by the Simulation Dashboard plugin and can be added for particular purposes.</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 100.0%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 78.9792%;" /><col style="width: 21.0353%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Open API</th><th>Function</th></tr><tr><td><p><strong>beforeObjectDestroyed(Object_ object)<br /></strong></p></td><td><p>Works with an object-<span class="ILfuVd"><span class="e24Kjd">destroying</span></span> Listener when added into SimulationExecutionListener.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>objectStateActivated(StructuredValue context, State newState)</strong></td><td colspan="1">Works with a State activation Listener when added into SimulationExecutionListener.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>SimulationExecutionListener.contextInitialized(SimulationExecution execution)</strong></td><td colspan="1">Gets an Event when objects have been initialized but not started the execution yet.</td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>SimulationHelper.findFeatureValue(Object_ runtimeContext, List&lt;Property&gt; propertyPath, Property target) : List&lt;FeatureValue&gt;</strong></p></td><td><p>Gets featureValue objects that match the given nested property path.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>SimulationHelper.getAvailableSignals(StructuredValue target, ExecutionEngine engine)</strong></td><td colspan="1">Retrieves available Signals from the specified structured value.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>SimulationHelper.getConfigElement(SimulationExecution execution)</strong></td><td colspan="1">Gets a Config element.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>SimulationHelper.isFeatureValueInvalid(FeatureValue featureValue, int index)</strong></td><td colspan="1">Checks the validity of FeatureValue.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>SimulationManager.getContext(SimulationSession session) : Object_</strong></td><td colspan="1">Gets the execution context.</td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>SimulationManager.registerSimulationExecutionListener(SimulationExecutionListener listener, SimulationExecution execution)</strong></p></td><td><p>Registers SimulationExecutionListener dynamically during the execution.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>SimulationManager.unregisterSimulationExecutionListener(SimulationExecutionListener listener)</strong></p><p><strong>SimulationManager.unregisterSimulationExecutionListener(SimulationExecutionListener listener, SimulationExecution execution)</strong></p></td><td><p>Unregisters SimulationExecutionListener.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 00070: Creating and sending signals

- page_id: `136729898`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729898/Creating+and+sending+signals
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:39.555+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs > Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

767236619

767236637

767236617

**On this page**

4

767236636

##### Creating a signal instance

You can create Signal Instances by using the following API. The signatures of this API are as follows

```text

```

The following example demonstrates how to create a Signal Instance

```text
if 's' is an instance of a Signal.]]>
```

The parameter of this API can be either a String or a Signal. If it is a String, the system will find a Signal whose name or qualified name contains the String.

##### Sending a signal instance to a specific target object

You can send an existing Signal Instance (or create a new one and then send it) to a target object with the following APIs

```text

```

The conditions that apply when creating an Instance are as follows

- If a signal name contains "::", it will find the signal from a qualified name. The signal will be found if its qualified name is ended with signalName.
- If an object is an instance of an Object_, send the signal to that Object_ directly.
- If an object is an instance of a String, there are two possible cases as follows
  - It will find the target object(s) from all waiting objects whose part/property names match the target's String parameter.
  - It will find the target object(s) through connected ports, given that the port is the name of the current object.

The following example shows how to send a specific signal to a specific target object in ALH API

```text

```

All parameters must not be null. Otherwise, the ScriptEngine errors will be thrown.

##### Getting the last signal instance from a runtime object

You can use the following API to retrieve the last signal instance from a runtime object. It is equivalent to $signal$.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get the last signal instance in the event pool of a specified object using ALH API

```text

```

It returns the Instance of the following

```text

```

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d136e7d5-6270-4b4d-8b7b-243e702b3b9c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">767236619</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9d911190-a7e4-4198-abc0-64aa3166c9ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">767236637</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e659526-4b94-4115-ab30-c04a8a4bbca7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">767236617</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e1523f38-099b-4bb6-a124-f061bb219daf"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="67988eb2-26d6-408f-8b42-e4ac5e2ff5ee"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">767236636</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3>Creating a signal instance</h3><p>You can create Signal Instances by using the following API. The signatures of this API are as follows<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(127,0,85);"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="17357beb-c062-40d7-9e3e-7fdbfec8d9b2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[SignalInstance createSignal(Signal signal)
SignalInstance createSignal(String keyword)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>The following example demonstrates how to create a Signal Instance</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2eacd5cf-6926-4f2e-a9e5-a7eb764b3c45"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.createSignal("start");
ALH.createObject("statemachine::signals::start");
ALH.createSignal(s); ---> if 's' is an instance of a Signal.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The parameter of this API can be either a String or a Signal. If it is a String, the system will find a Signal whose name or qualified name contains the String.</p><h3><br />Sending a signal instance to a specific target object</h3><p>You can send an existing Signal Instance (or create a new one and then send it) to a target object with the following APIs<br /><span style="color: rgb(127,0,85);"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="021ca246-41fd-4270-b574-c39e63e3dfc6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[void sendSignal(String signalName, Object_ object)
void sendSignal(String signalName, String targetName)
void sendSignal(SignalInstance signal, Object_ object)
void sendSignal(SignalInstance signal, String targetName)
void sendSignal(String signalName, Object_ target, String portName)
void sendSignal(SignalInstance signal, Object_ target, String portName)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>The conditions that apply when creating an Instance are as follows</p><ul><li>If a signal name contains &quot;::&quot;, it will find the signal from a qualified name. The signal will be found if its qualified name is ended with signalName.</li><li>If an object is an instance of an Object_, send the signal to that Object_ directly.</li><li>If an object is an instance of a String, there are two possible cases as follows<br /><ul><li>It will find the target object(s) from all waiting objects whose part/property names match the target's String parameter.</li><li>It will find the target object(s) through connected ports, given that the port is the name of the current object.</li></ul></li></ul><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The following example shows how to send a specific signal to a specific target object in ALH API</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71eca44d-e3ec-401d-a9dc-a9e951a496d8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.sendSignal("play", o); → "o" references to a target object.
ALH.sendSignal("system::play", o); → Find a signal using a qualified name.
ALH.sendSignal("play", "Speaker"); → Send to all waiting objects that have "Speaker"
as their type name.
ALH.sendSignal("play", "Player", "out2"); → "Player" is an object, and "out2" is a port name.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>All parameters must not be null. Otherwise, the ScriptEngine errors will be thrown.</p><h3><br />Getting the last signal instance from a runtime object</h3><p>You can use the following API to retrieve the last signal instance from a runtime object. It is equivalent to $signal$.<br /><span style="color: rgb(127,0,85);"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b04b24f-d1de-4636-b8d0-b3b57e70c679"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[SignalInstance getLastSignal(Object_ o)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>The following code fragment shows how to get the last signal instance in the event pool of a specified object using ALH API</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8651252f-9751-4de8-80ef-34c555587eaf"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getLastSignal(o);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>It returns the Instance of the following</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d6a78a41-5cd5-4dfd-a45b-b6fb9763e2c1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[fUML.Semantics.CommonBehaviors.Communications.SignalInstance.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730045 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00071: Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity

- page_id: `136730045`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730045/Creating+resetTime+Operation+and+resetTime+Activity
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:44.769+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model > Defining the stopwatch operations by using Activities
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a **resetTime** operation

1. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Operation .
2. Name the new operation "resetTime" . The resetTime operation will be created. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav33bc1cb1ce7b331271657a674d705aba.png']

Next, we will use an Activity to define the **resetTime** operation. The Activity will contain the Actions and the flows that will show the steps to set the **time** value to zero.

To create a **resetTime**Activity

1. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Activity .
2. Name the created Activity "resetTime" .
3. Add an Activity diagram to the resetTime Activity by right-clicking the resetTime Activity in the containment browser and select New Diagram > Activity Diagram . The new Activity diagram will be created under the resetTime Activity. We will use the default name of this diagram, which is "resetTime" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav4a4eacea1d68f44689eb60d76e0cb569.png']

On the **resetTime** Activity diagram, you will need an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to set the value of **time:Integer[1]** to zero. The structural feature of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction must be set as the time attribute of the StopWatch Class. 
 
To reset the time value of the StopWatch Object to zero using an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction

1. Click Action > Any Action... on the Activity diagram toolbar (see the following figure). The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6e7022874460de01a81f4414ef90cadc.png']
2. Select AddStructuralFeatureValueAction and click OK . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Click the **resetTime** Activity diagram to place the position of the created **AddStructuralFeatureValueAction**.
4. Right-click the symbol of the **AddStructuralFeatureValueAction** on the **resetTime** Activity diagram and select **Specification** to open its Specification window. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action.png']
5. Scroll down to the Structural Feature row, select it, and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select Property dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-property-a.png']
6. The Attribute time:Integer[1] of the StopWatch Class will be selected as the structural feature of this Action in this example.
7. Click OK to close the Select Property dialog and return to the Specification window.
8. Click the Is Replace All row and select the check box. The AddStructuralFeatureValueAction will remove any existing value and assign a new value to the structural feature. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-b.png']

We need to specify input pins for both the Object and the value of the **AddStructuralFeatureValueAction** metaClass. The object of the Classifier that contains the structural feature and its value will be supplied through these input pins respectively. 
 
To create input pins

1. Click the Pins node on the left-hand side of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction Specification dialog. The pins that are related to the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction will appear.
2. Click the Value row and select Input Pin . [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-c.png']
3. Name the input pin "t" and specify its type as Integer . [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-input-pin.png']
4. Click the Close button. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav34ec79dfb0b9452bdf513c2af654c933.png']
5. Select AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and click the Display Pins icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. The Select Pins dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-input-pin.png']
6. Select all pins and click OK .

To allow the object of the StopWatch to supply the value to self input using a ReadSelfAction

1. Click Action > Any Action... on the Activity diagram toolbar. The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open.
2. Select ReadSelfAction and click OK .
3. Click the resetTime Activity diagram. A ReadSelfAction will be created on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavea520615a98996e6601cca689431039c.png']
4. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar and click the self output pin of the ReadSelfAction and the self input pin of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. An object flow will be created to connect these two pins together. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavc15454b336b7513863415f2b71cfdf49.png']

Next, we will create a ValueSpecificationAction to supply a value to the input pin **t** of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. 
 
To create a LiteralInteger of zero value using a ValueSpecificationAction

1. Click Action > Any Action... on the Activity diagram toolbar. The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open.
2. Select ValueSpecificationAction and click OK to close the Select Action MetaClass dialog.
3. Click the resetTime Activity diagram to create a ValueSpecificationAction.
4. Right-click the ValueSpecificationAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and select Specification to open its Specification window.
5. Select the Value row and click the Show Shortcut Menu button, and select Value Specification > Literal Integer to create a Literal Integer. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-value-specification-action.png']
6. A new Literal Integer with a default value of 0 will be created as the value of the ValueSpecificationAction.
7. Right-click the pin result of the ValueSpecificationAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and select Specification to open its Specification window.
8. Rename the output pin ( result ) to " t " and specify its type as Integer . [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-output-pin.png'] [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav71821695a25971d7ea9242546e13e75b.png']
9. Add an Initial stage and an Activity Final stage to the resetTime Activity diagram.
10. Click Control Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Initial Node to the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction and the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to the Final stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavccef70c22bee118881298b220e6db83b.png']

You have now created a complete resetTime Activity diagram. The next thing you will need to do is to set the**Specification** of the **resetTime** Activity to the **resetTime** operation. 
 
To set the **Specification**of the **resetTime**Activity to the **resetTime**operation

1. Right-click the resetTime Activity in the containment browser and select Specification to open its Specification window.
2. Select the Specification row and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button (see the following figure). The Select Element dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-activity-properties.png']
3. Select the resetTime operation of the StopWatch Class as the specification of the Activity and click OK to close the Select Element dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-element-dialog.png']
4. Click Close to close the Specification window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a <strong>resetTime</strong> operation</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Operation</strong>.</li><li>Name the new operation <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;resetTime&quot;</em>. The resetTime operation will be created.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating the ResetTime Operation" ac:alt="Creating the ResetTime Operation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav33bc1cb1ce7b331271657a674d705aba.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Next, we will use an Activity to define the <strong>resetTime</strong> operation.
````
