The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> will remove any existing value and assign a new value to the structural feature.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Selecting the Is Replace All Attribute Check Box" ac:alt="Selecting the Is Replace All Attribute Check Box"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-b.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /></li></ol><p>We need to specify input pins for both the Object and the value of the <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> metaClass. The object of the Classifier that contains the structural feature and its value will be supplied through these input pins respectively. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create input pins</p><hr /><ol><li>Click the <strong>Pins</strong> node on the left-hand side of the <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong><strong>Specification </strong>dialog. The pins that are related to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> will appear.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> row and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Input</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Pin</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Assigning a Value to the Input Pin" ac:alt="Assigning a Value to the Input Pin"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-c.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Name the input pin <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;t&quot;</em> and specify its type as <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specifying the Name of the Pin and Its Type for the Value" ac:alt="Specifying the Name of the Pin and Its Type for the Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-input-pin.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> button. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Display Pins Button on the Smart Manipulator Toolbar" ac:alt="The Display Pins Button on the Smart Manipulator Toolbar"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav34ec79dfb0b9452bdf513c2af654c933.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Select <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resetTime</strong> Activity diagram and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Display </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Pins</strong> icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select Pins</strong> dialog will open. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Select Pins Dialog" ac:alt="The Select Pins Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-input-pin.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select all pins and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><span style="text-decoration: underline;"><br /></span></p><p>To allow the object of the StopWatch to supply the value to self input using a ReadSelfAction</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any</strong><strong>Action...</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select</strong><strong>Action</strong><strong>MetaClass</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ReadSelfAction</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resetTime</strong> Activity diagram. A ReadSelfAction will be created on the diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating a ReadSelf Action" ac:alt="Creating a ReadSelf Action" ac:height="310" ac:width="386"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavea520615a98996e6601cca689431039c.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Object</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">self</strong> output pin of the ReadSelfAction and the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">self</strong> input pin of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. An object flow will be created to connect these two pins together.<br /><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Connecting the Input and Output Pins with an Object Flow" ac:alt="Connecting the Input and Output Pins with an Object Flow" ac:height="311" ac:width="386"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavc15454b336b7513863415f2b71cfdf49.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Next, we will create a ValueSpecificationAction to supply a value to the input pin <strong>t</strong> of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a LiteralInteger of zero value using a ValueSpecificationAction</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any</strong><strong>Action...</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action </strong><strong>MetaClass</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong>ValueSpecificationAction</strong> and click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action MetaClass</strong> dialog.</li><li>Click the resetTime Activity diagram to create a ValueSpecificationAction.</li><li>Right-click the ValueSpecificationAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification </strong>to open its Specification window.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> row and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Show</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Shortcut</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Menu</strong> button, and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Literal Integer</strong> to create a Literal Integer. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating a Literal Integer Value for the Value of Action" ac:alt="Creating a Literal Integer Value for the Value of Action"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-value-specification-action.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>A new Literal Integer with a default value of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">0</strong> will be created as the value of the ValueSpecificationAction.</li><li>Right-click the pin <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">result</strong> of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ValueSpecificationAction</strong> on the resetTime Activity diagram and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Rename the output pin (<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">result</strong>) to &quot;<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">t</strong>&quot; and specify its type as <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Output Pin with Its Type Assigned to the Result" ac:alt="The Output Pin with Its Type Assigned to the Result"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-output-pin.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Connecting ValueSpecificationAction and addStructuralFeatureValueAction with an Object Flow" ac:alt="Connecting ValueSpecificationAction and addStructuralFeatureValueAction with an Object Flow"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav71821695a25971d7ea9242546e13e75b.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Add an Initial stage and an Activity Final stage to the resetTime Activity diagram.</li><li>Click <strong>Control</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Initial Node to the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction and the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to the Final stage.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavccef70c22bee118881298b220e6db83b.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> You have now created a complete resetTime Activity diagram. The next thing you will need to do is to set the<strong> Specification</strong> of the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity to the <strong>resetTime</strong> operation. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To set the <strong>Specification </strong>of the <strong>resetTime </strong>Activity to the <strong>resetTime </strong>operation</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity in the containment browser and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> row and click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-dot-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button (see the following figure). The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Element</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Specification Attribute of the ResetTime Activity" ac:alt="The Specification Attribute of the ResetTime Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-activity-properties.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the <strong>resetTime</strong> operation of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class as the specification of the Activity and click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>Select Element</strong> dialog.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Select element dialog" ac:alt="Select element dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-element-dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong> to close the Specification window. <br /><br /></li></ol><p> </p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729234 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00072: Creating runtime objects from Classifiers

- page_id: `136729234`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729234/Creating+runtime+objects+from+Classifiers
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:00.573+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['runtime-objects-from-classifiers']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

39858299

39858302

39858301

With Cameo Simulation Toolkit, you can use a Classifier with nested parts as the simulation context without the need to create Instance Specifications for those nested parts. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create runtime objects for those parts automatically. If the type of a property is Data Type, the default value of the runtime value of that property will also be created, depending on the default value of the property's type. In addition, if the type of the part contains a specified Classifier Behavior, and the type itself is set as active, the Behavior will run (the autoStartActiveObjects option in the SimulationConfig must be set as true).

The first figure shows a Class diagram located in the *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample, demonstrating property light, button and timer as parts of the System Class.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Class System and its parts.

You may replace the SimulationTarget of the SimulationConfig named FlashLight with the System Class. You can see the result of running this SimulationConfig. The result shows that Runtime Objects are automatically created for the parts of the System Class, and the Behavior of each part also automatically starts.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Sessions and Variables Panes showing runtime objects with a Classifier.

39858298

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Classifier Behavior property' space='CST2024x']

Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create runtime objects for those parts automatically. If the type of a property is Data Type, the default value of the runtime value of that property will also be created, depending on the default value of the property's type. In addition, if the type of the part contains a specified Classifier Behavior, and the type itself is set as active, the Behavior will run (the autoStartActiveObjects option in the SimulationConfig must be set as true). </p><p>The first figure shows a Class diagram located in the <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample, demonstrating property light, button and timer as parts of the System Class. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Class System and Its Parts" ac:alt="Class System and Its Parts"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ClassSystemAndItsParts.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Class System and its parts.</h6><p>You may replace the SimulationTarget of the SimulationConfig named FlashLight with the System Class. You can see the result of running this SimulationConfig. The result shows that Runtime Objects are automatically created for the parts of the System Class, and the Behavior of each part also automatically starts. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight" ac:alt="Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ModifiedExecutionConfigFlashLight.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Sessions and Variables Panes Showing Runtime Objects with a Classifier" ac:alt="Sessions and Variables Panes Showing Runtime Objects with a Classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-console-e.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br />Sessions and Variables Panes showing runtime objects with a Classifier.</h6><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4cc2a2b-03e7-4285-8e51-bb5ecc0e948d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39858298</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729239 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00073: Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications

- page_id: `136729239`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729239/Creating+runtime+objects+from+Instance+Specifications
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:00.970+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['runtime-objects-from-instance-specifications']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

39929354

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

39929356

39929353

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

39929355

At the beginning of a model simulation, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create a runtime object to store runtime values. If the element to be simulated is an InstanceSpecification or an SimulationConfig whose SimulationTarget is InstanceSpecification, the runtime values will be generated from the slot values. These runtime values will later be assigned to the runtime object's structural features that are equivalent to the defined feature of the slots.

If the slot of the InstanceSpecification is empty, and the defined feature of the slots has a defined default value, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will generate a runtime value from the default value and assign it to the structural feature of the runtime object.

The following figure shows the example of a runtime object that is created for executing the pipe InstanceSpecification. The length slot of the InstanceSpecification contains only one value, which is 1.0. The runtime value, which is produced for the length structural feature of the runtime object, will equal this slot value (1.0), while the runtime values of radius and thickness will equal the default values of the radius and thickness property of the Pipe class (0.05 and 0.002 respectively).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Variables pane showing the runtime object.

#### Representation of a Runtime object

The **Variables** pane in Cameo Simulation Toolkit displays **Name** and **Value** information of a runtime object that you are executing. If a runtime object is created by an Instance Specification, the name of the Instance Specification will appear in the **Value** column as shown in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A representation of the runtime object in the Variables pane.

You can also see a representation of the element's name in tooltip as in the **Value** column if you point over the element being activated on the diagram pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A representation of element being activated on the tooltip.

You can open a **Message** dialog that contains the runtime object being activated by clicking the element and the tooltip icon [IMAGE alt='' src='']on the tooltip menu.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A representation of the Element dialog.

#### Locating Instance Specification in the Containment tree

If you used an Instance Specification to create a runtime object and you want to find that particular Instance Specification in the Containment tree among the other items, Cameo Simulation Toolkit can locate and highlight it for you. This is particularly useful if you have a long list of items in the Containment tree, and it takes some time to locate a specific item. All you need to do is select the **Go****To** command from the context menu of that runtime object in the **Variables** pane.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If a runtime object is not created from an instance specification, the **Go****To** command will be disabled on the context menu.

To locate and highlight an Instance Specification in the Containment tree through the **Variables** pane

1. Right-click a runtime object in the **Variables** pane and select **Go****To**. The name of the Instance Specification that you used so as to create the runtime object will appear on the submenu. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Click the Instance Specification's name. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will locate and highlight it in the Containment tree. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

40952040

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance Specification' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment Tree' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='CST2024x']

All you need to do is select the <strong>Go </strong><strong>To</strong> command from the context menu of that runtime object in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40ec13c3-5de6-45ec-8c2d-94b274d938aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p> If a runtime object is not created from an instance specification, the <strong>Go </strong><strong>To</strong> command will be disabled on the context menu. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To locate and highlight an Instance Specification in the Containment tree through the <strong>Variables</strong> pane<span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> </span></p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click a runtime object in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane and select <strong>Go </strong><strong>To</strong>. The name of the Instance Specification that you used so as to create the runtime object will appear on the submenu. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Menu Item for Selecting Instance Specification in the Containment Tree" ac:alt="Menu Item for Selecting Instance Specification in the Containment Tree"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SelectInstance_inContainment.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Click the Instance Specification's name. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will locate and highlight it in the Containment tree.</p><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="SelectInstance_result_inContainment.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c85f987b-4d01-4ad7-a893-d793055fa673"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">40952040</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Instance Specification" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Containment Tree" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730014 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00074: Creating the stopwatch structure

- page_id: `136730014`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730014/Creating+the+stopwatch+structure
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:44.036+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Next, we will define the stopwatch Classifier and its structural features by using a Class diagram. We will start by creating a new package to store the stopwatch model. 
 
 To create a new package to store the stopwatch model

1. Right-click Model in the containment browser and select New Element > Package . A new package will be created under the Model node. Name the created package "system" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavcbf13a2eed10e9f0ad36880954bb8398.png']
2. Create a new Class diagram in the system package by clicking the Class Diagram button [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav58e81b0fa02881f9f435fa92dbdccb79.png'] on the Diagrams toolbar. The Create Diagram dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='new-diagram-menu.png']
3. Name the Class diagram "System" and select the system package as the diagram owner.
4. Click **OK**. NoteYou can also use the context menu of the system package to create a new Class diagram by right-clicking the system package and select **New Diagram** > **Class Diagram**.
5. Use the diagram toolbar of Class Diagram to create a new Class Element and name the created Class "StopWatch" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav9d2a257d15dee27f02355f217932ae9e.png']

We will then add a Time Attribute to the **StopWatch** Class. Since this Attribute represents the elapsed time in seconds, it will be typed by an **Integer** data type. 
 
To add a Time Attribute to the **StopWatch**Class

1. Click the manipulated button (the small orange button) in the StopWatch Class. A new Attribute will be created in the StopWatch Class.
2. Name the Attribute "time" and type it with an Integer by directly typing it into the Attribute compartment of the StopWatch Class. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav96fbc3722aee48918b184b38ee181f39.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong> dialog will open. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Create Diagram Dialog" ac:alt="The Create Diagram Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="new-diagram-menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Name the Class diagram <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;System&quot;</em> and select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">system</strong> package as the diagram owner.</li><li><p>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.<br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72fef5ad-47e0-4575-b419-3ab435f114cd"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also use the context menu of the system package to create a new Class diagram by right-clicking the system package and select <strong>New Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong>Class Diagram</strong>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Use the diagram toolbar of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong> to create a new <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class</strong> Element and name the created Class <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;StopWatch&quot;</em>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating the StopWatch Class" ac:alt="Creating the StopWatch Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav9d2a257d15dee27f02355f217932ae9e.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> We will then add a Time Attribute to the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class. Since this Attribute represents the elapsed time in seconds, it will be typed by an <strong>Integer</strong> data type. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To add a Time Attribute to the <strong>StopWatch </strong>Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Click the manipulated button (the small orange button) in the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class. A new Attribute will be created in the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class.</li><li>Name the Attribute <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;time&quot;</em> and type it with an <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong> by directly typing it into the Attribute compartment of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Adding a Time Attribute to the StopWatch Class" ac:alt="Adding a Time Attribute to the StopWatch Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav96fbc3722aee48918b184b38ee181f39.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p> </p><p> </p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730156 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00075: Creating User Interface mockups for the stopwatch model

- page_id: `136730156`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730156/Creating+User+Interface+mockups+for+the+stopwatch+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:48.192+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to use the MagicDraw's User Interface (UI) Modeler to create user interface mockups that will be displayed during model execution. This session shows you how to create a user interface for the stopwatch model. The user interface will be in the form of a panel with buttons to send signals to the runtime object of the stopwatch during model execution. A label will be used to show the runtime time value. 
 
 To create user a interface mockup

1. Right-click the Model node and select New Element > Package to create a new package for the UI Elements.
2. Name the created package " ui ". [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-containment.png']
3. Click the button on the Custom Diagram toolbar to create a new User Interface Modeling Diagram . The Create Diagram dialog will open.
4. Name the diagram " StopWatchUI " and select the ui package as the owner of the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav99741dc64147b53d16c89670321df3a3.png']
5. Click OK .
6. Create a new frame for the UI mockup by clicking Frame on the User Interface Modeling Diagram toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='package-ui.png']
7. Create a label to show the runtime time value by clicking Label on the diagram toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='package-ui-2.png']
8. Create five buttons to send the signals to the runtime object of the stopwatch model by clicking Button on the diagram toolbar.
9. Name the buttons: Start , stop , split , unsplit , and reset . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav15cc35ab7ecdbd6e363cc911d64736af.png']
10. Create a UI Prototype by dragging the StopWatch Class from the containment browser to the created UI frame on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-b.png']
11. Create a Runtime Value Prototype by dragging the time property of the StopWatch Class from the containment browser to the created label on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-c.png']
12. Create a Signal Instance Prototype by dragging the Signal from the containment browser to each of the buttons. The buttons will send the dragged signals to the runtime object when you click them during the execution. Therefore, each signal in the stopwatch model will be dragged to each button that has a corresponding name. For example, the start signal will be dragged to the start button, and the stop signal will be dragged to the stop button. [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-d.png']
13. Open the Specification window of the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration to add the ready user interface mockup for the stopwatch to the SimulationConfig for the StopWatch.
14. Edit the UI field by clicking the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select «UI» dialog will open.
15. Select the created Frame in the ui package and click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='select-ui-dialog.png']

The modified SilentStopwatch is now ready to run the stopwatch with the UI mockup. Next, you need to run the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration. 
 
 To run the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1. Right-click the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration and select Simulation > Run . A simulation session will be created to execute the StopWatch class.
2. Click the Run Simulation button on the Simulation window toolbar. The execution of the StopWatch class will start with the designed UI mockup. The time value displayed in the label is now zero (0). [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-e.png']

You can click the **start** button to start the stopwatch. You can use the buttons on the UI mockup to send the signals to the runtime object of the **StopWatch** class. The time value displayed on the label of the UI mockup will increment by one every second. 
 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Click the **stop** button to stop the execution and click it again to go to the final stage, which is the termination of the execution.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729231 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00076: Creating values and objects in the variables pane

- page_id: `136729231`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729231/Creating+values+and+objects+in+the+variables+pane
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:00.099+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['creating-values-in-variables-tab', 'creating-objects-in-variables-tab']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

39503046

39503048

39503047

You can create a new value or object through the shortcut menu **Add** **Value** of a property typed by a primitive type, a Class, or a block in the **Variables** pane. The shortcut menu will be available only when the number of existing values or objects of the selected property is less than the upper multiplicity of the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using the Add Value shortcut menu to add a new value.

If a property is typed by a primitive type (and SysML primitive value types), Cameo Simulation Toolkit will add a new value, which is a default value of the primitive type, as the value of that property.

If a property is typed by a class or a Block, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show a list of Classes or Blocks containing the Class or the Block that types the property and all of its subtypes, which are not abstract Classes or abstract Blocks. When you select a Class or a Block from the list, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from that Class or Block and set the object as the value of the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Selecting a Class or a Block to initialize an object.

39503045

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='CST2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68c7614c-09f6-4005-9db0-d30ae4b81765"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503046</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="da58126e-db0f-44f5-a366-222e946fc99a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503048</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2db62b72-10f5-4347-921c-c75b68ef0f0f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503047</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can create a new value or object through the shortcut menu <strong>Add</strong> <strong>Value</strong> of a property typed by a primitive type, a Class, or a block in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. The shortcut menu will be available only when the number of existing values or objects of the selected property is less than the upper multiplicity of the property. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Context Menu to Add a New Value" ac:alt="Context Menu to Add a New Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="addValue_menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating values and objects in the variables pane" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Using the Add Value shortcut menu to add a new value.</h6><p>If a property is typed by a primitive type (and SysML primitive value types), Cameo Simulation Toolkit will add a new value, which is a default value of the primitive type, as the value of that property. </p><p>If a property is typed by a class or a Block, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show a list of Classes or Blocks containing the Class or the Block that types the property and all of its subtypes, which are not abstract Classes or abstract Blocks. When you select a Class or a Block from the list, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from that Class or Block and set the object as the value of the property. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Selecting a Class or a Block to Initialize an Object" ac:alt="Selecting a Class or a Block to Initialize an Object"><ri:attachment ri:filename="classblocks_to_initObj.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating values and objects in the variables pane" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Selecting a Class or a Block to initialize an object.</h6><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c60a695-f941-474f-a75b-d7b4dbf912db"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503045</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728992 space=CST2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00077: CSV export

- page_id: `136728992`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728992/CSV+export
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2023-09-22T11:45:15.635+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['csv-export', 'exporting-simulation-results-to-csv']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1429392940

1429392943

2098005390

**On this page**

33

1429392941

As of Version 18.4, Cameo Simulation Toolkit can export simulation results to a CSV file.

A new configuration, called **CSV Export**, has been added to export simulation results to a CSV file. CSV files take the following properties:

- File Name The name of the file that the exported results are written into. The path is the same as the project directory (by default). Alternatively, a full path may be specified as D:\ExportedCSVResults.csv
- Record Time If true (by default), time will be recorded in the first column of the exported CSV file.
- Represents Specifies the Classifier represented by the Configuration.
- Value Specifies the properties of the Classifier (including nested properties) whose values will be recorded and written to the CSV file.
- Write At The End Records values only once at the end of execution, right before termination, rather than listening and recording value changes during the execution. When Number of runs is more than 1 in the main configuration, the values are recorded once in each iteration.

To export simulation results to a CSV file

1. Open a sample simulation project from the samples folder of Cameo Simulation Toolkit. Here the HingeMonteCarloAnalysis sample is used.
2. Drag the CSV Export Configuration from the toolbox to the Simulation Configuration diagram.
3. Double-click the **CSV Export** Configuration and fill in values for **File Name**, **Represents**, and **Value** from its Specification window. The **File Name** property is the name of the file in the project's working directory. The **Record Time** property is for recording time in the first column of the exported CSV file. The **Represents** property specifies the Classifier represented by the Configuration, and the **Values** property refers to the values selected which will be written to the exported CSV file. Optionally, you can also set the **Write At The End** property to record values only once at the end of execution before termination. The following figure shows the parameters of CSV Export configuration. [ATTACHMENT filename='CSV Export Clearance Specification Window.png']
4. Set the created **CSV** export as a tagged value of the **executionListeners** tag definition of the Simulation Configuration. This step is important for the results to be written to file. See the CSV export configuration below: [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Run the simulation and then stop it. The results will be written to the File Name specified in Step 3. See the parameters of CSV Export configuration below: [ATTACHMENT filename='CSV Export Result.png']

##### Support of Part properties

Not only can CSV export the values of properties with primitive types, e.g., Real, Integer, etc., but it can also export the types of the properties that are not primitive. Selected Part names in the **value** tag will be used as headers, and type names will be used as values of exported CSVs as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Selected Part names used as headers and type names used as values of exported CSVs.

1429392939

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing data from a CSV file in a line chart' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting plots data to a CSV file' space='CST2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729107 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00078: Customizing animation

- page_id: `136729107`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729107/Customizing+animation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:51.449+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Animation
- labels: ['customizing-animation', 'animation-customization']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

1441516382

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

1441516385

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

1441516384

There are four kinds of annotated elements in a model simulation: (i) Active, (ii) Visited, (iii) Breakpoint, and (iv) Last Visited elements. By default, active elements will be annotated with red, visited elements with green, last visited elements with orange, and breakpoints with yellow. Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize the colors of these annotated elements through the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='CST2024x']** dialog in the **Animation** group.

The **Auto** **Open** **Diagrams** option in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='CST2024x']** dialog and Simulation window allows Cameo Simulation Toolkit to open a tab where you can see a diagram that contains a currently active Element in a non-silent simulation (animation is enabled). If you want to enable this option, you need to select **true** as its value. This is useful when you want to track the simulation current status. Select **false** as the value of **Auto****Open** **Diagrams** when you would like to focus on just one diagram and avoid automatic switching or opening of diagrams.

If the value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is **false** and you run a silent-mode simulation of an activity diagram that has a decision node without any decision input, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight all elements including the decision node and open a **Question** dialog to ask for a decision when the simulation reaches the decision node. However, if the value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is false, and you are running a non-silent simulation, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight only the decision node.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The default value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is **false**.

To select the **Auto****Open****Diagrams** option in the **Simulation** window

- Click the Auto Open Diagrams toggle button in the Simulation window as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### INFO: Information

Information

When animation with selected **Auto Open Diagrams** is used, it must open animated diagrams in the same tab and must not open new diagram windows as new tabs. If a diagram is already open in a tab other than the active tab, the other tab will close, and the diagram animation will be shown in the active tab.

1441516381

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='CST2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize the colors of these annotated elements through the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Project options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Project Options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> dialog in the <strong>Animation</strong> group.</p><p>The <strong>Auto</strong> <strong>Open</strong> <strong>Diagrams</strong> option in the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Project options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Project Options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> dialog and Simulation window allows Cameo Simulation Toolkit to open a tab where you can see a diagram that contains a currently active Element in a non-silent simulation (animation is enabled). If you want to enable this option, you need to select <strong>true</strong> as its value. This is useful when you want to track the simulation current status. Select <strong>false</strong> as the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open</strong> <strong>Diagrams</strong> when you would like to focus on just one diagram and avoid automatic switching or opening of diagrams. </p><p>If the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is <strong>false</strong> and you run a silent-mode simulation of an activity diagram that has a decision node without any decision input, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight all elements including the decision node and open a <strong>Question</strong> dialog to ask for a decision when the simulation reaches the decision node. However, if the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is false, and you are running a non-silent simulation, Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight only the decision node. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dfbd5162-7a95-4d00-b8d5-f2e9cd7dbb1a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The default value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is <strong>false</strong>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To select the <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> option in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window </p><hr /><ul><li>Click the <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> toggle button in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window as follows</li></ul><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Auto Open Diagrams button" ac:alt="Auto Open Diagrams button"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110041_2016-06-24+15_23_53-Photos.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Customizing animation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ec1cbc1c-e000-41dd-8703-db447586369a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When animation with selected <strong>Auto Open Diagrams</strong> is used, it must open animated diagrams in the same tab and must not open new diagram windows as new tabs. If a diagram is already open in a tab other than the active tab, the other tab will close, and the diagram animation will be shown in the active tab.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8032842-3ff9-4f03-bb47-db1401db5205"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1441516381</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Project options" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729152 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00079: Debug process simulation

- page_id: `136729152`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729152/Debug+process+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:54.730+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging
- labels: ['debug-buttons']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

30123672

30123675

30123674

Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to control a model simulation using the debug buttons such as **Suspend**, **Resume**, **Step** **into**, and **Step** **over**. The table below explains the functions of all of the debug buttons.

| Button | Name | Shortcut key | Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Start Simulation | F8 | To start simulating an object of the initialized session shown in the Sessions pane. |
|  | Restart | F8 | To restart the last simulated element. |
|  | Suspend | F8 | To pause a running simulation session in the Sessions. |
|  | Resume | F8 | To resume a simulation session. |
|  | Step into | F5 | To simulate and animate a currently active element in a selected simulation session in the Sessions pane. |
|  | Step over | F6 | To simulate a currently active element in a selected simulation session and run animation in the background. |
|  | Terminate | None | To stop a session in the Sessions pane. If the selected session contains sub-sessions, all of the sub-sessions will also be terminated. |

You can examine and edit variables in the **Variables** pane (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='CST2024x']), pause the a model simulation at predefined breakpoints (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Breakpoints' space='CST2024x']), or simulate one element at a time using the **Step** **into** or **Step** **over** button.

The **Debugger** pane includes a player-like control panel for a step-by-step simulation (see the table above), threads or Behaviors with an expandable stack trace (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding simulation sessions' space='CST2024x']), input/output console for custom commands or expressions evaluation (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation console' space='CST2024x']), **Variables** pane/runtime structure (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Runtime Value Monitoring' space='CST2024x']), and **Breakpoints** pane.

30123671

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Breakpoints' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding simulation sessions' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation console' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Runtime Value Monitoring' space='CST2024x']

and <strong>Breakpoints</strong> pane.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5c0a0fdc-7786-4f8f-afc1-8a72c9afa205"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">30123671</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Variables pane" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Breakpoints" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding simulation sessions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Simulation console" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Runtime Value Monitoring" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729366 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00080: Deferred Events

- page_id: `136729366`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729366/Deferred+Events
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:10.159+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation > Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['event-deffering', 'deffering-events']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Important

122554799

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Important

122554801

122554798

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Important

122554800

UML State Machines provide a special mechanism for deferring Events in States. In every State, you can include a clause [Event list]/defer. If an Event in the current State’s deferred Event list occurs, the Event will be saved (deferred) for future processing until a State is entered that does not list the Event in its deferred Event list. Upon entry to such a State, the UML State Machine will automatically recall any saved Event(s) that are no longer deferred and will then either consume or discard these Events.

- A deferred Event is activated as soon as it enters a State where it is not deferred.
- A deferred Event can only be added to a State Machine diagram at this time.

#### Deferrable Trigger

A list of Triggers that are candidates to be retained by the State Machine if they trigger no transitions out of the State (not consumed). A deferred Trigger is retained until the State Machine reaches a State configuration where it is no longer deferred.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Specification window for Event State moving - showing the editable Deferrable Trigger tag.

As in the State Machine diagram in the following figure, the Elevator**'open'** Event is deferred as long as the Elevator is moving. Only when the State transitions to **'stopped'** State again, the **'open'** Event will be executed because it is no longer deferred at this point.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 A deferred clause added to Event moving which causes the Event to delay until it is no longer deferred.

#### WARNING: Important

Important

This version of Cameo Simulation Toolkit does not support **Deferred Events** for Activity diagrams. They can now only be added to a State in a State Machine diagram.

122943877

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MD2024x']

In every State, you can include a clause [Event list]/defer. If an Event in the current State’s deferred Event list occurs, the Event will be saved (deferred) for future processing until a State is entered that does not list the Event in its deferred Event list. Upon entry to such a State, the UML State Machine will automatically recall any saved Event(s) that are no longer deferred and will then either consume or discard these Events.</p><ul><li>A deferred Event is activated as soon as it enters a State where it is not deferred. </li><li>A deferred Event can only be added to a State Machine diagram at this time. </li></ul><h2>Deferrable Trigger</h2><p>A list of Triggers that are candidates to be retained by the State Machine if they trigger no transitions out of the State (not consumed). A deferred Trigger is retained until the State Machine reaches a State configuration where it is no longer deferred.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Deferable Trigger" ac:alt="Deferable Trigger"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-state-moving.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Deferred Events" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Specification window for Event State moving - showing the editable Deferrable Trigger tag.</h6><p>As in the State Machine diagram in the following figure, the Elevator<strong> 'open'</strong> Event is deferred as long as the Elevator is moving. They can now only be added to a State in a State Machine diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="691d9796-6ca0-48fc-82b8-6d01a42304ff"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122943877</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="State Machine" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730021 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00081: Defining the stopwatch Classifier Behavior

- page_id: `136730021`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730021/Defining+the+stopwatch+Classifier+Behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:44.202+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Class Element, which can be executed with Cameo Simulation Toolkit, must have its Classifier Behavior defined. The Classifier Behavior can be any kind of Behavior such as Activity, State Machine, and Interaction. This tutorial uses the State Machine Behavior to define the Behavior of the stopwatch.

To define the Classifier Behavior for the **StopWatch** Class, you have to create the Behavior on the StopWatch, which will be assigned later as the Classifier Behavior of the **StopWatch** automatically. 
 
 To define the Classifier Behavior for the **StopWatch** Class

1. Right-click the StopWatch symbol on the Class diagram and select New Diagram > State-Machine Diagram... . A new State Machine Element will be created under the StopWatch Class with a new State Machine diagram.
2. Name the State Machine diagram "StopWatch".
3. Make sure that the StopWatch State machine is the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class. To see the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class, right-click the StopWatch symbol on the Class diagram and select Specification from the context menu. The Specification window will open.
4. Select All from the Properties drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-class-stopwatch.png']
5. Scroll down to see the Classifier Behavior row, and you will see that the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class is defined by the StopWatch State Machine. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event.png'] [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-class-stopwatch-c.png']

Next, we are going to create the StopWatch initial and final stages, and the four States (**Ready**, **Running**, **Paused**, and **Stopped**) in the initial stage as well.

To add an **Initial**stage to the **StopWatch**State Machine

- Click Initial on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the State Machine diagram to place the Initial stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavfc807e5dad35a6dc8eb8a2e2334bb90d.png']

To add the ready, running, paused, and stopped States

1. Click State on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the diagram to place a State. Name the created State "ready" .
2. Repeat Step 1 to create the " running" , " paused" , and " stopped" States. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavdb2618531755fa8d4b674c2de40b0379.png']

To add a final stage to the State machine diagram

- Click Final State on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the diagram to place the final stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav7c8723378243ad324a3743c62dca401d.png']

Now that the initial and final stages have been created, you will need to add the transitions between them and among the four States in the initial stage. 
 
To add a transition from the **Initial**stage to the **ready**State

1. Click a stage (the Initial stage in this example) that will be the source of the transition to be created. The smart manipulator toolbar will open.
2. Click the Transition icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddava072afa3d5c17146c05715796f526339.png']
3. Click a State (the ready State in this example) that will be the target of the transition. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav60294b81f5996dcd6bfa1060c5cb598a.png']
4. Repeat Steps 1 to 3 to create more transitions between the following States: from the ready to running State from the running to paused State from the running to stopped State from the paused to running State from the paused to stopped State from the stopped to ready State from the stopped State to the Final stage [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav0b3243bea9e2b36c84b6875f3524ffdf.png']
5. Create a looping transition to the running State by clicking the Transition to self icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav43be35b8d4a2413da8149a015837abdb.png']
6. Now your StopWatch State machine will look similar to the one shown in the following figure [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavb2013b68b56b9e5b343057299cb23010.png']

You will see that some of the created transitions have the SignalEvent triggers. These triggers need signal Elements and a package that is required to store those signal Elements. You can add a package and create the signal Elements by using the browser context menu. 
 
To add a new package for the signal Elements

1. Right-click the Model node in the containment browser and select New Element > Package .
2. Name the created package "signals" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav02f2e9636d8edccd1414fa29212758ed.png']

To create signal Elements

1. Right-click the signals package and select New Element > Signal .
2. Name the created signal "reset" . The reset signal will be created and stored in the signals package.
3. Repeat Steps 1 to 2 to create the split , start , stop , and unsplit signal Elements. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavbb4a178543c51f8a6410935f98cbc0ab.png']

To create signal Events for the transitions on the StopWatch State Machine diagram

- Drag a signal Element from the containment browser to a transition on the State Machine diagram. The signal Event will later be specified as the trigger of the transition. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav98b0652dae8cdc9b9239a0b08b2abf68.png']

To create a signal Event for each transition

1. Drag the start signal to the transition between the ready and running States.
2. Drag the split signal to the transition between the running and paused States.
3. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the running and stopped States.
4. Drag the unsplit signal to the transition between the paused and running States.
5. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the paused and stopped States.
6. Drag the reset signal to the transition between the stopped and ready States.
7. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the stopped and Final States. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6e722749fb60fdc6e89a10de0981a858.png']

Now each and every transition between the States has its own signal Event. Next, you will also need to create a signal Event for the **transition****to****self** of the running State. The signal Event that will be created as the trigger for the **transition****to****self** is called **TimeEvent**. 
 
To create a signal Event for the **transition****to****self**of the running State

1. Right-click the Transition to self of the running State and select Specification to open its Specification window.
2. Click the Categorized View button to open the properties of the transition in the categorized view.
3. Click the Event Type row in the Trigger category and select TimeEvent from the drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event.png']
4. Click the When row in the Trigger category and type "1s" to specify that the trigger will send the time Event every second. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event-b.png']
5. Click Close . You will see all of the created stages, States, transitions, and signal Events are shown on the StopWatch State Machine diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav1cfe524dfbea755379071dbc78160048.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
