Behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </li></ul><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a signal Event for each transition</p><hr /><ol><li>Drag the <strong>start</strong> signal to the transition between the <strong>ready</strong> and <strong>running</strong> States.</li><li>Drag the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">split</strong> signal to the transition between the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">running</strong> and <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paused</strong> States.</li><li>Drag the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal to the transition between the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">running</strong> and <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> States.</li><li>Drag the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">unsplit</strong> signal to the transition between the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paused</strong> and <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">running</strong> States.</li><li>Drag the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal to the transition between the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paused</strong> and <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> States.</li><li>Drag the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">reset</strong> signal to the transition between the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> and <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ready</strong> States.</li><li>Drag the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal to the transition between the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> and <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Final</strong> States. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="StopWatch State Machine with Signal Events" ac:alt="StopWatch State Machine with Signal Events"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav6e722749fb60fdc6e89a10de0981a858.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Defining the stopwatch Classifier Behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Now each and every transition between the States has its own signal Event. Next, you will also need to create a signal Event for the <strong>transition</strong><strong>to</strong><strong>self</strong> of the running State. The signal Event that will be created as the trigger for the <strong>transition</strong><strong>to</strong><strong>self</strong> is called <strong>TimeEvent</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a signal Event for the <strong>transition </strong><strong>to </strong><strong>self </strong>of the running State</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>Transition</strong><strong>to</strong><strong>self</strong> of the running State and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Categorized View</strong> button to open the properties of the transition in the categorized view.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Event</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Type</strong> row in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Trigger</strong> category and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">TimeEvent</strong> from the drop-down menu.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specifying Time Event as the Signal Event for the Transition to Self of the Running State" ac:alt="Specifying Time Event as the Signal Event for the Transition to Self of the Running State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-transition-event.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Defining the stopwatch Classifier Behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">When</strong> row in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Trigger</strong> category and type <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;1s&quot;</em> to specify that the trigger will send the time Event every second.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specifying the Value for the When Attribute in the Trigger Category" ac:alt="Specifying the Value for the When Attribute in the Trigger Category"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-transition-event-b.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Defining the stopwatch Classifier Behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong>. You will see all of the created stages, States, transitions, and signal Events are shown on the StopWatch State Machine diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The StopWatch State Machine with All of the Defined Stages,States, Transitions, and Signal Events" ac:alt="The StopWatch State Machine with All of the Defined Stages,States, Transitions, and Signal Events" ac:height="330" ac:width="344"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav1cfe524dfbea755379071dbc78160048.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Defining the stopwatch Classifier Behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730044 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00082: Defining the stopwatch operations by using Activities

- page_id: `136730044`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730044/Defining+the+stopwatch+operations+by+using+Activities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:44.664+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

984249231

984249234

984249232

This section defines the values of the elapsed time among the States in the StopWatch State Machine. When the stopwatch enters the **ready** State, the elapsed time defined by the Attribute **time:Integer[1]** should be reset to zero. In addition, the elapsed time should increment by 1 every second while the StopWatch is at the **running** State. Therefore, we need to add two new operations: (i) ResetTime and (ii) increaseTime to the StopWatch Class to define the elapsed time at the ready and running States. The **resetTime** operation will reset the elapsed time to zero, and the **increaseTime** operation will increment the elapsed time by one.

984249230

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity' space='CST2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="39f5646d-0bc3-47dd-901b-a69f559a0b60"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249231</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3c14916f-a570-4a4c-b221-f076761ccb8c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249234</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="459be496-a0fc-4b5d-bc77-6dae7f097baf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249232</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This section defines the values of the elapsed time among the States in the StopWatch State Machine. When the stopwatch enters the <strong>ready</strong> State, the elapsed time defined by the Attribute <strong>time:Integer[1]</strong> should be reset to zero. In addition, the elapsed time should increment by 1 every second while the StopWatch is at the <strong>running</strong> State. Therefore, we need to add two new operations: (i) ResetTime and (ii) increaseTime to the StopWatch Class to define the elapsed time at the ready and running States. The <strong>resetTime</strong> operation will reset the elapsed time to zero, and the <strong>increaseTime</strong> operation will increment the elapsed time by one.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a4acebe4-25bd-4647-af0f-63230fba9da9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249230</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729327 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00083: Defining Triggers on Transitions

- page_id: `136729327`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729327/Defining+Triggers+on+Transitions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:08.965+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation > Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['triggers-on-transitions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Warning

1061801889

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Warning

1061801891

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Warning

1061801890

If you want a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Context, runtime object, and runtime value' space='CST2024x'] to change its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space='MD2024x'] on a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MD2024x'], you need to define a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram elements' space='MD2024x'] on the Transition by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assigning Event Type' space='MD2024x']. A runtime object will change its State when it receives a Trigger on the Transition. A Trigger can be a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MD2024x'], a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MD2024x'], or a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MD2024x'].

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The StopWatch's State Machine diagram with defined Triggers on the Transitions.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

Optionally, you can also use a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Send Signal Action' space='MD2024x'] of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity' space='CST2024x'] as a Signal Event of Transition. As shown in the following figure, the *stopped* State can wait for the *off* Send Signal Action of the *do*Activity to proceed to the final State.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The off Send Signal Action of the do Activity as a Signal Event of Transition.

#### WARNING: Warning

Warning

- According to UML Version 2.5.1, the initial Transition must not have Triggers or Guards. If the Initial Transition violates the rule, Simulation will print a warning message in the Console pane .
- Simulation also provides the validation rule to prevent the model error by displaying a warning in the Validation Results panel . You can either select the Check Model Before Execution option through validation and verification before running the simulation or select Pre-execution constraints from the [Validation Suite](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Validation+dialog#Validationdialog-descrDescriptionofValidationdialogoptions) drop-down list in the **Validation** dialog and click Validate .

1061801888

**Related page**

- Signal properties mapping to behavior parameters

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3b503811-4d15-4904-afc5-4f24115da8ea"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801889</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bcfc06ba-69d7-48fc-9868-1fc1181d7ad2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801891</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b9caa312-1679-4b43-8b97-72cb2e414058"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801890</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you want a <ac:link ac:anchor="Runtimeobject"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Context, runtime object, and runtime value" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[runtime object]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to change its <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="State" /></ac:link> on a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Transition" /></ac:link>, you need to define a <ac:link ac:anchor="Trigger"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Trigger]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> on the Transition by <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Assigning Event Type" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[assigning an Event Type to a Transition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. A runtime object will change its State when it receives a Trigger on the Transition. A Trigger can be a <ac:link ac:anchor="SignalEvent"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Signal Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, a <ac:link ac:anchor="TimeEvent"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Time Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, or a <ac:link ac:anchor="ChangeEvent"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Change Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="StopWatch State Machine.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Defining Triggers on Transitions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>The StopWatch's State Machine diagram with defined Triggers on the Transitions.</h6><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ef68b471-2890-49cd-b761-4690446070d2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Optionally, you can also use a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Send Signal Action" /></ac:link> of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Activity" /></ac:link> as a Signal Event of Transition. As shown in the following figure, the <em>stopped</em> State can wait for the <em>off</em> Send Signal Action of the <em>do </em>Activity to proceed to the final State.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Send Signal Action.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Defining Triggers on Transitions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>The off Send Signal Action of the do Activity as a Signal Event of Transition.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="85d9561c-851f-4963-9b5b-d06c07870df8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Warning</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>According to UML Version 2.5.1, the initial Transition must not have Triggers or Guards. If the Initial Transition violates the rule, Simulation will print a warning message in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Console+pane">Console pane</a>.</li><li>Simulation also provides the validation rule to prevent the model error by displaying a warning in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Validation+Results+panel">Validation Results panel</a>. You can either select the <strong>Check Model Before Execution</strong> option through <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Validation+and+verification">validation and verification</a> before running the simulation or select <strong>Pre-execution constraints</strong> from the <strong><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Validation+dialog#Validationdialog-descrDescriptionofValidationdialogoptions">Validation Suite</a></strong> drop-down list in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Validation+dialog"><strong>Validation</strong> dialog</a> and click <strong>Validate</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3d29cb9b-7b89-4f4f-b721-fa0d66c1d605"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801888</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Signal+properties+mapping+to+behavior+parameters">Signal properties mapping to behavior parameters</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728766 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00085: Diagram

- page_id: `136728766`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728766/Diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:37.995+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation > Elements simulation
- labels: ['diagram-simulation', 'simulating-diagrams']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use the shortcut menu or the diagram toolbar to simulate a diagram. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will simulate the element, which is the context of the selected diagram, the same way it simulates a Behavior or a Class depending on the element which is the context of diagram. You can also specify the diagram as a target of a Simulation Configuration (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space='CST2024x'] for more details).

To simulate a diagram do one of the following

- Right-click a diagram and select either Simulation > Run or Simulation > Run with Context ( if the context of the diagram is a Behavior). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
- Click the **Run** button in the diagram toolbar and select either **Run** or **Run with Context** (if the context of the diagram is a Behavior). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To terminate diagram simulation do one of the following

- Click the **Terminate** button in the diagram toolbar. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
- Click the **Terminate** button in the **Simulation** pane. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

You can also specify the diagram as a target of a Simulation Configuration (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link> for more details).</p><p><br /></p><p>To simulate a diagram do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a diagram and select either <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; ````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=158598554 space=CST2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00086: Diagram Animation on the web user interface

- page_id: `158598554`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/158598554/Diagram+Animation+on+the+web+user+interface
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-26T10:22:36.413+01:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Web Server for Cameo Simulation Toolkit
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Behavioral diagrams (Activity diagrams, State Machine diagrams, and Sequence Diagrams) and Composite Structure diagrams (Internal Block diagrams) can be embedded into HTML-generated user interfaces and animated during the web server simulation.

Composite Structure diagrams only display activations and not run-time values.

##### Creating a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram

The sample model *Joystick.mdzip* is used to demonstrate how to create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram.

To create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram

1. Open the <modeling_tool_install_dir>\samples\simulation\Joystick.mdzip sample file.
2. Create a User Interface Modeling Diagram by adding a frame and panel to the diagram. To learn more about the components of a UI modeling diagram, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UI modeling diagram simulation' space=''] .
3. Drag the State Machine Diagram, long drop it on the frame, and select the **Create Diagram Knob Symbol** command. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click the diagram symbol in the frame to open the smart manipulator toolbar and click[IMAGE alt='' src='']to view the diagram in the frame. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Drag the Classifier from the Containment tree and drop it on the frame. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Drag the Signals from the Containment tree and drop them on the frame to create Buttons. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
After completing the steps above, the diagram should look like the one below. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Drag and drop the frame on the Simulation Configuration. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Set the **Start Web Server** property to t*rue*in the Specification window of Sim Config.
9. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] in the diagram toolbar to run the web server simulation for the generated UI Mockup.
10. Click the URL that appears in the******Console**pane to open the **Embedded Browser** or the **Web Browser**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

After creating a UI Mockup the Behavioral Diagram can be simulated both in the modeling tool and the web browser.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### State Machine Diagram simulation on the Embedded browser.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### State Machine Diagram simulation on the Web browser.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Behavioral diagrams (Activity diagrams, State Machine diagrams, and Sequence Diagrams) and Composite Structure diagrams (Internal Block diagrams) can be embedded into HTML-generated user interfaces and animated during the web server simulation.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ee50158-2f92-465f-aa5f-53340f89533f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Composite Structure diagrams only display activations and not run-time values.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Creating a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">The sample model <em>Joystick.mdzip</em> is used to demonstrate how to create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">To create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <em>&lt;modeling_tool_install_dir&gt;\samples\simulation\Joystick.mdzip</em><span> </span>sample file.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Create a User Interface Modeling Diagram by adding a frame and panel to the diagram. /></span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop State Machine Diagram.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></span></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Click the diagram symbol in the frame to open the smart manipulator toolbar and click<ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729309 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00087: Disabling updates in Simulation panes

- page_id: `136729309`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729309/Disabling+updates+in+Simulation+panes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:06.895+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging
- labels: ['turning-off-updates-in-simulation-pane', 'disabling-updates-in-simulation-pane']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can turn off automatic updates of panes in the **Simulation** window by clicking [IMAGE alt='' src='']. Turning off auto-updates of panes will cause all of the panes (Sessions, Console, Variables, and Breakpoints) in the Simulation window to be disabled and the simulation speed to increase. This can be seen in the comparison of the Simulation window before turning off the auto-update option and after turning off the auto-update option in the following figures respectively.

The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is enabled

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is enabled.

The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is disabled

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is disabled.

After the auto-update of panes option is disabled, you can work on the model, make changes, or run it, but the actions that you are performing will not be displayed in any of the panes in the Simulation window. If you enable the option, the Actions that you have performed will appear in the panes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can turn off automatic updates of panes in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window by clicking <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Disable_autoupdate_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Disabling updates in Simulation panes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span>. Turning off auto-updates of panes will cause all of the panes (Sessions, Console, Variables, and Breakpoints) in the Simulation window to be disabled and the simulation speed to increase. This can be seen in the comparison of the Simulation window before turning off the auto-update option and after turning off the auto-update option in the following figures respectively.</p><p>The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is enabled</p><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="LockUpdate_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Disabling updates in Simulation panes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is enabled. </h6><p>The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is disabled</p><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DisabledUpdates.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Disabling updates in Simulation panes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is disabled.</h6><p>After the auto-update of panes option is disabled, you can work on the model, make changes, or run it, but the actions that you are performing will not be displayed in any of the panes in the Simulation window. If you enable the option, the Actions that you have performed will appear in the panes.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=142934457 space=CST2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00088: Display Values in Diagrams

- page_id: `142934457`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/142934457/Display+Values+in+Diagrams
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2023-11-09T10:37:04.068+01:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Developer Guide > Java APIs
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When no native model execution is running in the Cameo Simulation Toolkit, some external tool might be executing the model. There is a capability to dynamically display any custom value string on parts and/or ports in structure diagrams, without modifying the model in Cameo Simulation Toolkit itself. Currently, only parts and ports that are typed with a primitive data type are supported.

For that purpose, start by extending *com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation.dashboard.api.ExternalValueProvider*class . The returned custom values will be displayed on the diagrams representing the *context*for which the *ExternalValueProvider* instance was constructed*.* The model is not modified in any way when these values are displayed. The color in which the values are displayed is configurable via project option "Runtime Value Text Color on Shapes". In the image bellow, two such values are provided and displayed in a diagram of the Transmission.mdzip sample project: "30.0 mi/h" for carSpeed and "19.0"" for wheelDiameter.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Here is the implementation that was designed specifically for the Transmission.mdzip sample and provides just the 2 values seen in the above image:

```java
pathInContext)
	{
		// values should be pre-collected for diagram related performance reasons. Avoid time demanding implementations (e.g. remote calls) of this method
		if (element == carSpeedElement)
		{
			return externalExecution.getCarSpeed() + " mi/h";
		}
		else if (element == wheelDiameterElement)
		{
			return externalExecution.getWheelDiameter() + "\""; // double quote to represent inches
		}
		return null; // do not provide custom value for other elements in this context
	}

	/**
	 * To be called every time speed changes to call {@link #getValueText(Element, List)} for that element again
	 * @see #invalidate(Element)
	 * @see #invalidateAll() 
	 */
	public void invalidateCarSpeed()
	{
		invalidate(carSpeedElement);
	}
}]]>
```

To have the effect, an instance of the implemented provider must be registered to the *ExternalValueProviders* service, as illustrated here:

```java
// The root context of the external execution. Values will only be visible in the Diagrams that have this element set as context
	Classifier context = Finder.byQualifiedName().find(project, "Transmission::Structure::PowerTrain", Class.class);
	if (context != null)
	{
		EngineControlUnitValueProvider valueProvider = new EngineControlUnitValueProvider(context);
		ExternalValueProviders.getInstance(project).register(valueProvider);

		// if later the provider needs to be unregistered, it should be done by calling:
		// ExternalValueProviders.getInstance(project).unregister(valueProvider);
		// Since providers are registered for specific open project, there is no obligation to unregister manually on project close.
	}]]>
```

<installation_directory>\openapi\examples\externalvalueindiagrams

#### Animating flowing information

Additionally, it is possible to display animation for the information that flows via connector from one end to another. For a fixed amount of time, a banner with the custom value will move from source end towards the target. During the full duration of the animation the connector presentation element will be highlighted in color which is controlled by a project option "Active Color".

The full code example for this feature is available in*<installation_directory>\openapi\examples\flowanimation*

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

```java
// select connector via which to animate
            ConnectorEnd sourceEnd = selectSourceEnd(connector); // pick end from which to start the animation
			String flowingInformation = "myFlowingInformation"; // hardcoded for example, should be some actual data compatible with end/connector/flow types
			FlowingInformationDescriptor descriptor = new FlowingInformationDescriptor(flowingInformation, connector, sourceEnd);

			long duration = 4000; // 4 seconds to reach the other end
			FlowAnimation.getInstance(Project.getProject(connector)).animate(descriptor, duration, finished -> {/* do something when animation completes*/});]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>When no native model execution is running in the Cameo Simulation Toolkit, some external tool might be executing the model. There is a capability to dynamically display any custom value string on parts and/or ports in structure diagrams, without modifying the model in Cameo Simulation Toolkit itself. Currently, only parts and ports that are typed with a primitive data type are supported.</p><p>For that purpose, start by extending <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation.dashboard.api.ExternalValueProvider </em>class . The returned custom values will be displayed on the diagrams representing the <em>context </em>for which the <em>ExternalValueProvider</em> instance was constructed<em>.</em> The model is not modified in any way when these values are displayed. The color in which the values are displayed is configurable via project option &quot;Runtime Value Text Color on Shapes&quot;. In the image bellow, two such values are provided and displayed in a diagram of the Transmission.mdzip sample project: &quot;30.0 mi/h&quot; for carSpeed and &quot;19.0&quot;&quot; for wheelDiameter.</p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="valuesDisplayed.png" /></ac:image></p><p>Here is the implementation that was designed specifically for the Transmission.mdzip sample and provides just the 2 values seen in the above image:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8ac38052-7ee8-43ed-8f46-f0f3fe974dd0"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[//Engine Control Unit refers to ECU block in the Transmission sample project
public class EngineControlUnitValueProvider extends ExternalValueProvider
{
	private final Element carSpeedElement;
	private final Element wheelDiameterElement;
	
	//Mimics some model execution process that happens outside this tool and produces values to display
	private final MockExternalExecution externalExecution;

	public EngineControlUnitValueProvider(Element context)
	{
		super(context);
		Project project = Project.getProject(context);
		carSpeedElement = FinderByQualifiedName.getInstance().find(project, "Transmission::Structure::ECU::carSpeed", Property.class);
		wheelDiameterElement = FinderByQualifiedName.getInstance().find(project, "Transmission::Structure::ECU::wheelDiameter", Property.class);
		externalExecution = new MockExternalExecution(this);
	}

	@CheckForNull
	@Override
	public String getValueText(Element element, List<? extends Element> pathInContext)
	{
		// values should be pre-collected for diagram related performance reasons. Avoid time demanding implementations (e.g. remote calls) of this method
		if (element == carSpeedElement)
		{
			return externalExecution.getCarSpeed() + " mi/h";
		}
		else if (element == wheelDiameterElement)
		{
			return externalExecution.getWheelDiameter() + "\""; // double quote to represent inches
		}
		return null; // do not provide custom value for other elements in this context
	}

	/**
	 * To be called every time speed changes to call {@link #getValueText(Element, List)} for that element again
	 * @see #invalidate(Element)
	 * @see #invalidateAll() 
	 */
	public void invalidateCarSpeed()
	{
		invalidate(carSpeedElement);
	}
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To have the effect, an instance of the implemented provider must be registered to the <em>ExternalValueProviders</em> service, as illustrated here:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b0b5143-d3dd-4a29-bfc0-8b89d868f934"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	Project project = <...>
	// The root context of the external execution. Values will only be visible in the Diagrams that have this element set as context
	Classifier context = Finder.byQualifiedName().find(project, "Transmission::Structure::PowerTrain", Class.class);
	if (context != null)
	{
		EngineControlUnitValueProvider valueProvider = new EngineControlUnitValueProvider(context);
		ExternalValueProviders.getInstance(project).register(valueProvider);

		// if later the provider needs to be unregistered, it should be done by calling:
		// ExternalValueProviders.getInstance(project).unregister(valueProvider);
		// Since providers are registered for specific open project, there is no obligation to unregister manually on project close.
	}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="26d35dad-dc1f-45c2-8363-fbe3b7e0ee8a"><ac:rich-text-body><strong> </strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The full code example is available in<span> </span></span><em>&lt;installation_directory&gt;\openapi\examples\externalvalueindiagrams</em></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Animating flowing information</h2></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p>Additionally, it is possible to display animation for the information that flows via connector from one end to another. For a fixed amount of time, a banner with the custom value will move from source end towards the target. During the full duration of the animation the connector presentation element will be highlighted in color which is controlled by a project option &quot;Active Color&quot;.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The full code example for this feature is available in<span> </span></span><em>&lt;installation_directory&gt;\openapi\examples\flowanimation</em></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="flowAnimation.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1f39e5ca-598c-46ef-a577-bb49460f9e71"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[			Connector connector = <..> // select connector via which to animate
            ConnectorEnd sourceEnd = selectSourceEnd(connector); // pick end from which to start the animation
			String flowingInformation = "myFlowingInformation"; // hardcoded for example, should be some actual data compatible with end/connector/flow types
			FlowingInformationDescriptor descriptor = new FlowingInformationDescriptor(flowingInformation, connector, sourceEnd);

			long duration = 4000; // 4 seconds to reach the other end
			FlowAnimation.getInstance(Project.getProject(connector)).animate(descriptor, duration, finished -> {/* do something when animation completes*/});]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729124 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00089: Displaying simulation information

- page_id: `136729124`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729124/Displaying+simulation+information
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:52.792+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Animation > Simulation information in diagrams
- labels: ['active-states-in-diagrams', 'active-state-images', 'runtime-values-in-diagrms', 'flowing-information-in-diagrams', 'animation-options']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

1768127704

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

1768127718

INLINE

1768127701

INLINE

**On this page**

4

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

1768127707

INLINE

When simulating a model, you can choose to display simulation information not only in the Simulation window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can see runtime values directly on Part shapes or a flowing item with its name on a path.

#### INFO: Types of diagrams

Types of diagrams

You can display simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram. However, the flowing information on paths can also be shown in an Activity Diagram.

##### Displaying active States

In diagrams, active States can be displayed on the shapes ofall initialized Parts a similar way they are displayed on the Variables pane of the Simulation window (in square brackets).

#### NOTE: Exception

Exception

Active States are not displayed for Parts with the multiplicity of more than one.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The diagram illustrates how active States are displayed on Part shapes.

If you do not want to see active States in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the **Show Active States on Part Shapes**animation option. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying simulation information' space='CST2024x'].

##### Displaying active State images

You can display active State images on Part shapes by applying an image to a State or by assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration from which you execute your model. When States change during simulation, State images change as well.

#### NOTE: Important

Important

If you specify State images both by applying them to States and assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration, the images of the Image Switcher will be used when running the Simulation Configuration.

To display active State images when executing a model

1. Open the Specification window of the State you want to create an image for.
2. Click the specification cell of the Image property and select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='image_library.png'] to select an image from the default image library, click [ATTACHMENT filename='custom_image.png'] to select an image from your file system, or click [ATTACHMENT filename='image_URL.png'] to specify an image URL.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Here you can see how active State images are displayed when simulating a model.

If you run a Simulation Configuration, use an Image Switcher to display active State images as described below.

To display active State images when running a Simulation Configuration

1. Open the Simulation Configuration Diagram in which the Simulation Configuration is displayed and create an Image Switcher.
2. In the Specification window of the Image Switcher, click the Represents property specification cell and select the element for the states of which you want to create images.
3. For every active State image you want to create, create an Active Image element as the attribute of the Image Switcher. Creating Active Image attributesTo create an Active Image attribute for an Image Switcher, click **Active Image** in the diagram palette, then click the Image Switcher shape.
4. In the Specification window of every Active Image specify the following properties:
  - **Image** property - select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to select an image from the default image library, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to select an image from your file system, or click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to specify an image URL.
  - **Active Element** property - select the State which the Active Image represents.
5. Drag the Image Switcher created in step 1 to the Simulation Configuration shape. This way the Image Switcher is specified as the value of the UI property of the Simulation Configuration.

##### Displaying runtime values

In diagrams, you can display the runtime values of Part properties the same way they are displayed in the Variables pane of the Simulation window. Runtime values are displayed next to property names on Part shapes instead of the default property values. Note that when you run a simulation, property types are not displayed.

Runtime values can be displayed only for the following types of properties:

- Primitive
- ValueType
- Enumeration
- Custom DataType

#### INFO: Automated Requirement verification

Automated Requirement verification

Runtime values on Part shapes are highlighted in green (the Requirement is satisfied) or red (the Requirement is not satisfied) when the automated Requirement verification is performed.

In addition, a tooltip with the text of unsatisfied Requirements or constraints appears when you hover the mouse pointer over Part or Value Property shapes. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Part shapes during model simulation.

#### TIP: Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

If a runtime value does not fit in a shape and is cut off, resize the shape as described below.

1. Select the shape that you want to resize.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. Click the Resize to Fit Runtime Values icon on the shape (displayed below). [ATTACHMENT filename='resizing_shapes.png']
  2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='resize_to_fit_runtme_values.png'] in the diagram toolbar.

In addition, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manipulating simulation information' space='CST2024x'] directly in diagrams and change simulation results in real-time. If you do not want to see runtime values in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the **Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes** animation option. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying simulation information' space='CST2024x'].

##### Displaying flowing information

You can choose to display flowing information on animated paths when executing a model. In diagrams based on a Composite Structure Diagram, the flowing item is displayed as a moving triangle with a label showing the item name (see the image below). In Activity Diagrams, the flowing item is displayed as a bubble.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this Internal Block Definition Diagram, you can see the*neutral*signal flowing from the*GearShift*to the*TCU*.

You can also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manipulating simulation information' space='CST2024x'] directly from a Part shape in a diagram and see the flowing item on a path. If you do not want to see flowing information in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the **Show Flowing Information** animation option. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying simulation information' space='CST2024x'].

##### Disabling and enabling animation options

You can disable or enable animation options, such as displaying active States and runtime values on Part shapes. The steps below explain how to change option values for the whole project and a specific Simulation Configuration. Note that by default all simulation animation options are enabled.

****

To disable or enable animation options for a project

1. In the main menu, go to Options > Project .
2. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, expand the General option group and select Simulation . [ATTACHMENT filename='project_options.png']
3. In the option specification area on the right side of the dialog, disable or enable the following animation options:
  - Show Active States on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active States are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Flowing Information - if the option set to true , the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to false , paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.
  - Show Active State Images on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active State images are displayed on Part shapes.
4. Click OK to save the changes.

To disable or enable animation options for a Simulation Configuration

1. Open the Specification window of a Simulation Configuration element. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification_of_simulation_configuration.png']
2. Disable or enable the following animation options located in the Animation category:
  - Show Active States on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active States are displayed on Part Property shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - **Show Active State Images**- Select**On Part shapes**to show active State images on Part shapes. Select**In the Simulation UI**to show active State images in the simulation UI dialog. To show images both on Part shapes and in the simulation UI dialog, select**Both**.
  - Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Flowing Information - if the option set to true , the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to false , paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.
3. Close the Specification window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="14953898-c536-4d23-b00e-f781a78e0feb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1768127704</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5adc3487-83b1-446a-9ddf-121ee78094cc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1768127718</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="224c3778-06ec-47cb-962b-0eded187eb44"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1768127701</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="88ff7ce8-78a5-4c15-a934-ca14b11a8083"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c501b5a4-5acf-4ad4-b817-39c174cfb913"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1768127707</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When simulating a model, you can choose to display simulation information not only in the Simulation window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can see runtime values directly on Part shapes or a flowing item with its name on a path.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b7ccb91d-9af8-48b0-844b-b94332515836"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Types of diagrams</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can display simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram. However, the flowing information on paths can also be shown in an Activity Diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h3>Displaying active States</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><span>In diagrams, active States can be displayed on the shapes of</span><span> all initialized Parts a similar way they are displayed on the Variables pane of the Simulation window (in square brackets).</span><br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e80180d2-eafd-427f-bbb6-394ed6909ce1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Exception</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Active States are not displayed for Parts with the multiplicity of more than one.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: center;"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_active_states.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e0558454-0f4d-4deb-acdb-a176b6e6a365"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="26d8d0af-34b2-40a1-be6b-a18b89d420e6">The diagram illustrates how active States are displayed on Part shapes.</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></h6><p>If you do not want to see active States in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the <strong>Show Active States on Part Shapes </strong>animation option. For more information, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Disabling and enabling animation options"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ac:link>.<br /><br /></p><h3>Displaying active State images</h3><p>You can display active State images on Part shapes by applying an image to a State or by assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration from which you execute your model. When States change during simulation, State images change as well.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dd7fafed-9d4e-4eb2-bb42-aa26442bd219"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you specify State images both by applying them to States and assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration, the images of the Image Switcher will be used when running the Simulation Configuration.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p>To display active State images when executing a model</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of the State you want to create an image for.</li><li>Click the specification cell of the <strong>Image</strong> property and select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_library.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to select an image from the default image library, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="custom_image.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to select an image from your file system, or click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_URL.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to specify an image URL.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_active_state_image.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Here you can see how active State images are displayed when simulating a model.</h6><p>If you run a Simulation Configuration, use an Image Switcher to display active State images as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To display active State images when running a Simulation Configuration</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Simulation Configuration Diagram in which the Simulation Configuration is displayed and create an Image Switcher.</li><li>In the Specification window of the Image Switcher, click the <strong>Represents</strong> property specification cell and select the element for the states of which you want to create images.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">For every active State image you want to create, create an Active Image element as the attribute of the Image Switcher.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3aad6edb-c8c2-4d60-af53-8ce5d842075a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Creating Active Image attributes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To create an Active Image attribute for an Image Switcher, click <strong>Active Image</strong> in the diagram palette, then click the Image Switcher shape.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Specification window of every Active Image specify the following properties:</p><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Image</strong> property - select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_library.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to select an image from the default image library, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="custom_image.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to select an image from your file system, or click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_URL.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to specify an image URL.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Active Element</strong> property - select the State which the Active Image represents.</p></li></ul></li><li>Drag the Image Switcher created in step 1 to the Simulation Configuration shape. This way the Image Switcher is specified as the value of the <strong>UI</strong> property of the Simulation Configuration.</li></ol><h3><br />Displaying runtime values</h3><p>In diagrams, you can display the runtime values of Part properties the same way they are displayed in the Variables pane of the Simulation window. Runtime values are displayed next to <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">property names on Part shapes instead of the default property values. Note that when you run a simulation, property types are not displayed</span>.</p><p>Runtime values can be displayed only for the following types of properties:</p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Primitive                                                                                                                                                                                                   </span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">ValueType</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Enumeration</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Custom DataType<br /><br /></span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a9e64253-ef5e-4f1b-b993-3655a8f6799c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Automated Requirement verification</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Runtime values on Pa<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="25dba438-119e-4e5a-b1e4-ab4411a60112">rt shapes are highlighted in green (the Req</ac:inline-comment-marker>uirement is satisfied) or red (the Requirement is not satisfied) when the automated Requirement verification is performed.</p><p>In addition, a tooltip with the text of unsatisfied Requirements or constraints appears when you hover the mouse pointer over Part or Value Property shapes.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="tooltip_for_unsatisfied_requirements.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_runtime_values.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Part shapes during model simulation.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb88eaa9-0561-4390-b038-4e380dd212c9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Resizing shapes to fit runtime values</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If a runtime value does not fit in a shape and is cut off, resize the shape as described below.</p><ol><li>Select the shape that you want to resize.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ol><li>Click the <strong>Resize to Fit Runtime Values</strong> icon on the shape (displayed below).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resizing_shapes.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resize_to_fit_runtme_values.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> in the diagram toolbar.</li></ol></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In addition, you can <ac:link ac:anchor="Changing runtime values"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Manipulating simulation information" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[modify runtime values]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly in diagrams and change simulation results in real-time. If you do not want to see runtime values in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the <strong>Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes</strong> animation option. For more information, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Disabling and enabling animation options"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ac:link>.</p><h3><span><br />Displaying flowing information</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">You can choose to display flowing information on animated paths when executing a model. In diagrams based on a Composite Structure Diagram, the flowing item is displayed as a moving triangle with a label showing the item name (see the image below). In Activity Diagrams, the flowing item is displayed as a bubble.</span></p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_flowing_information.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98443f95-0546-44d6-a002-1d4001d71780">In this Internal Block Definition Diagram, you can see the </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98443f95-0546-44d6-a002-1d4001d71780">neutral</ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98443f95-0546-44d6-a002-1d4001d71780"> signal flowing from the </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98443f95-0546-44d6-a002-1d4001d71780">GearShift</ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98443f95-0546-44d6-a002-1d4001d71780"> to the </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98443f95-0546-44d6-a002-1d4001d71780">TCU</ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98443f95-0546-44d6-a002-1d4001d71780">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></h6><p>You can also <ac:link ac:anchor="Sending a trigger"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Manipulating simulation information" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[send a trigger]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly from a Part shape in a diagram and see the flowing item on a path. If you do not want to see flowing information in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the <strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> animation option. For more information, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Disabling and enabling animation options"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ac:link>.<br /><br /></p><h3>Disabling and enabling animation options</h3><p>You can disable or enable animation options, such as displaying active States and runtime values on Part shapes. The steps below explain how to change option values for the whole project and a specific Simulation Configuration. Note that by default all simulation animation options are enabled.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p>To disable or enable animation options for a project</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, go to <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li>On the left side of the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, expand the <strong>General</strong> option group and select <strong>Simulation</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="project_options.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the option specification area on the right side of the dialog, disable or enable the following animation options:<br /><ul><li><strong>Show Active States on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, active States are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to <em>false</em>, paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.</li><li><strong>Show Active State Images on Part Shapes </strong>- if the option set to <em>true</em>, active State images are displayed on Part shapes.</li></ul></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to save the changes.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To disable or enable animation options for a Simulation Configuration</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of a Simulation Configuration element.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification_of_simulation_configuration.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Disable or enable the following animation options located in the <strong>Animation</strong> category:<br /><ul><li><strong>Show Active States on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, active States are displayed on Part Property shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><p><strong>Show Active State Images</strong><span> - Select </span><strong>On Part shapes</strong><span> to show active State images on Part shapes. Select </span><strong>In the Simulation UI</strong><span> to show active State images in the simulation UI dialog. To show images both on Part shapes and in the simulation UI dialog, select </span><strong>Both</strong><span>.</span></p></li><li><strong>Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to <em>false</em>, paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.</li></ul></li><li>Close the Specification window.</li></ol><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729859 space=CST2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00090: Distribution Extensions

- page_id: `136729859`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729859/Distribution+Extensions
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-03-05T12:56:47.255+01:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models
- labels: ['non-normative-extensions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Distributed Property is a property of a Block or a Value Type used to apply a probability distribution to the values of the property. Specific distributions can be defined by applying a Subclass of the «DistributedProperty» stereotype to a property according to[OMG SysML 1.4](http://sysml.org/docs/specs/OMGSysML-v1.4-15-06-03.pdf), E.7 Distribution Extensions. Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports three types of distributions:Uniform, Normal, and Triangular.

To set a distributed property

1. Select a property and double-click it to open the Specification window.
2. Select Applied Stereotype and click [...] to include a distribution property.
3. Specify the required properties. These include **Mean**, **Standard Deviation**, etc. for «normal» and **Min**, **Max**, etc. for other distributed properties. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click Close . The distributed property will be applied.
5. Run the simulation model. Depending on the applied stereotype, the distributed properties will be initialized with a random value, such as normal distribution, constant distribution between min & max values. You can review sampling results by running the model with association end multiplicity (e.g., 100), and keep the result with «CSVExport» for analysis.
6. Results of the distributed property «normal» (with 100000 samples) with a Mean value of 10 and a Standard Deviation value of 2. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. You can apply a «uniform» distributed property stereotype with Min and Max properties (e.g., 0 and 100) to plot a uniform distribution chart. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. You can apply a «triangular» distributed property stereotype with Min, Max, and Peak properties to plot a triangular distribution chart. 
The sample project used is *<modeling_tool_installation_directory>\samples\simulation\AircraftProjectAnalysis.mdzip*. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported SysML elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirements traceability from the Variables pane' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">A Distributed Property is a property of a Block or a Value Type used to apply a probability distribution to the values of the property. Specific distributions can be defined by applying a Subclass of the «DistributedProperty» stereotype to a property according to </span><a href="http://sysml.org/docs/specs/OMGSysML-v1.4-15-06-03.pdf" style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">OMG SysML 1.4</a><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, E.7 Distribution Extensions. Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports t</span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">hree types of distributions: </span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Uniform, Normal, and Triangular.</span></p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard">To set a distributed property</p><hr /><ol><li>Select a property and double-click it to open the Specification window.</li><li>Select <strong>Applied Stereotype</strong> and click [...] to include a distribution property.</li><li><p>Specify the required properties. These include <strong>Mean</strong>, <strong>Standard Deviation</strong>, etc. for «normal» and <strong>Min</strong>, <strong>Max</strong>, etc. for other distributed properties.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="non-normativeExt.PNG" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>. The distributed property will be applied.</li><li>Run the simulation model. Depending on the applied stereotype, the distributed properties will be initialized with a random value, such as normal distribution, constant distribution between min &amp; max values. You can review sampling results by running the model with association end multiplicity (e.g., 100), and keep the result with «CSVExport» for analysis.</li><li><p style="text-align: left;">Results of the distributed property «normal» (with 100000 samples) with a Mean value of 10 and a Standard Deviation value of 2.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Distribution.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can apply a «uniform» distributed property stereotype with Min and Max properties (e.g., 0 and 100) to plot a uniform distribution chart.</span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Unifrom_Distribution.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can apply a «triangular» distributed property stereotype with Min, Max, and Peak properties to plot a triangular distribution chart.<br />The sample project used is <em style="text-align: left;">&lt;modeling_tool_installation_directory&gt;\samples\simulation\AircraftProjectAnalysis.mdzip</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Triangular_Distribution.png" /></ac:image></span></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported SysML elements" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Requirements traceability from the Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729499 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00091: Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins

- page_id: `136729499`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729499/Dummy+tokens+of+Actions+through+Output+Pins
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:13.269+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation > Execution of incomplete or dummy models
- labels: ['dummy-tokens']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1918270852

1918270855

1918270854

Simulation Toolkit has an option to support dummy tokens of CallBehaviorActions that do not have a Behavior. Business-level abstract diagrams can be executed and animated because Simulation will automatically create new objects according to type of those Output Pins if unspecified, even though this action will break fUML semantics. You can set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option (true by default) in the **Project Options Simulation** dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Objects are automatically created according to type of unspecified Output Pins, e.g., *order*.

From the figure above, the Output Pin returns an object (*ro* is *order@203c1eec*). If the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option is set false, the Output Pin will have no value.

To set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option

1. From the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. On the left pane, click **Simulation**.
3. Under the **fUML Engine** group, set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option to **true**.
4. Select **OK**. [ATTACHMENT filename='Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin.png'] The Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin option in the Project Options Simulation dialog.

1918270850

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Execution of incomplete or dummy models' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity simulation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f2506be0-3743-47cb-8617-703db7498485"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270852</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="468b24ee-7774-4dde-b64b-a3229ce758d2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270855</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="55c592be-5669-4206-a9dd-076a634a537b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270854</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Simulation Toolkit has an option to support dummy tokens of CallBehaviorActions that do not have a Behavior. Business-level abstract diagrams can be executed and animated because Simulation will automatically create new objects according to type of those Output Pins if unspecified, even though this action will break fUML semantics. You can set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option (true by default) in the <strong>Project Options Simulation</strong> dialog.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Dummy Token of Action through Output Pin.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Objects are automatically created according to type of unspecified Output Pins, e.g., <em>order</em>.</h6><p>From the figure above, the Output Pin returns an object (<em>ro</em> is <em>order@203c1eec</em>). If the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option is set false, the Output Pin will have no value.</p><p><br /></p><p>To set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option</p><hr /><ol><li>From the main menu, click <strong>Options</strong> &gt;<strong> Project</strong>. The <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog opens.</li><li><p>On the left pane, click <strong>Simulation</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Under the <strong>fUML Engine</strong> group, set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option to <strong>true</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Select <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /></p><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin.png" /></ac:image><br /><h6>The Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin option in the Project Options Simulation dialog.</h6></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ece79763-d0bd-4ecd-ab7f-ce9f27dabee7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270850</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729460 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00092: Duration analysis

- page_id: `136729460`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729460/Duration+analysis
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:11.975+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['duration-analysis']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of calculating the minimum, maximum, and average duration of any Activity. It uses the values taken from duration constraints applied to each element in an Activity as the basis to calculate the Activity's duration.

Cameo Simulation Toolkit can perform an analysis of activity duration in the two following scenarios

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729466 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00093: Duration analysis on executed traces

- page_id: `136729466`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729466/Duration+analysis+on+executed+traces
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:12.225+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation > Duration analysis
- labels: ['executed-trace-duration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

144626827

144626829

144626828

You can also perform an analysis of Activity duration after an Activity has been simulated. Prior to the analysis, be sure that the simulation log has been configured and the value of a Record Activation tag is **true** (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation log' space='CST2024x'] for more information about the simulation log).

To see the duration of a visited element

1. Right-click the simulation log and select **Simulation** > **Analyze Duration** **of** **Executed Trace**. The **Select Execution** **Session** **and** **Activity** dialog will open (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Analyze Duration of Executed Trace Menu on the Simulation Log Context Menu. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Select Execution Session and Activity dialog.
2. Select a session and an Activity and click the **Calculate Duration** button. The result will appear in the Simulation **Console** pane. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Duration of executed trace in the Simulation Console pane.

144626826

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis on visited elements' space='CST2024x']
