<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729461 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00094: Duration analysis on visited elements

- page_id: `136729461`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729461/Duration+analysis+on+visited+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:12.039+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation > Duration analysis
- labels: ['visited-element-duration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

144449098

144449101

144449099

The duration analysis on visited elements feature is not enabled by default. To enable it, you need a TimeHandler and register it in the execution listener of the <<SimulationConfig>>. You can create the TimeHandler by creating a new Class and apply the <<TimeHandler>> stereotype.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Enabling duration analysis on visited elements in the <<SimulationConfig>>.

You can view the duration of a running Activity by using the diagram's context menu **Analyze Duration** **of** **Visited** **Elements**.

To see the duration of visited elements

1. Pause a currently running Activity by either adding a breakpoint or clicking the Pause button.
2. Right-click the diagram and select **Simulation** > **Analyze Duration****of****Visited****Elements**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Analyze duration on visited elements menu on the diagram context menu.
3. The minimum, maximum, and average duration of the visited elements together with the simulated trace listing all of the visited elements will appear in the Simulation **Console** pane. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Duration of visited elements in the Simulation Console.

144449097

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis on executed traces' space='CST2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729534 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00095: Duration constraint

- page_id: `136729534`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729534/Duration+constraint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:15.621+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation > Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['duration-constraint']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

380966344

380966346

380966345

Simulation allows you to evaluate duration constraints between Call Messages and Reply Messages, e.g., you can specify that a Call Message must get a Reply Message in 500 ms, so the actual duration of operation method execution must be compared with the duration constraint of 500 ms. The comparison is done as an evaluation of the constraint and will generate a fail value for *testcases* or stop at breakpoints on the [Constraint Failure As Breakpoint option](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/SimulationConfig+stereotype). However, it does not support the delay time of a message.

The default time unit of the duration constraint is millisecond (ms), but you can also use other time units by specifying them after the duration value, e.g., you can use 10s to denote 10 seconds. If you specify both the minimum and maximum values, Simulation will use the maximum value of the duration constraint to delay the delivery of a Message.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Sequence diagram showing how a Duration Constraint {500} can be used to evaluate between the Call Message and Reply Message.

The figure above shows that *cons* sends a Call Message to *bank* to verify an account (12345678) with an expected duration constraint of 500 ms from the Reply Message. The evaluation in the first run is failed because the actual duration (431 ms) is less than 500 ms, while another run passes because the actual duration (710 ms) is greater than 500 ms.

380966342

**Related pages**

- Message
- Internal simulation clock
- Specification window

The comparison is done as an evaluation of the constraint and will generate a fail value for <em>testcases</em> or stop at breakpoints on the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/SimulationConfig+stereotype">Constraint Failure As Breakpoint option</a>. However, it does not support the delay time of a message.</p><p>The default time unit of the duration constraint is millisecond (ms), but you can also use other time units by specifying them after the duration value, e.g., you can use 10s to denote 10 seconds. If you specify both the minimum and maximum values, Simulation will use the maximum value of the duration constraint to delay the delivery of a Message.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sequence Diagram.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Duration constraint" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Sequence diagram showing how a Duration Constraint {500} can be used to evaluate between the Call Message and Reply Message.</h6><p>The figure above shows that <em>cons</em> sends a Call Message to <em>bank</em> to verify an account (12345678) with an expected duration constraint of 500 ms from the Reply Message. The evaluation in the first run is failed because the actual duration (431 ms) is less than 500 ms, while another run passes because the actual duration (710 ms) is greater than 500 ms.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="034ebc7d-ba8c-46c8-ba73-bfcfcceaf3ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">380966342</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Message">Message</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Internal+simulation+clock">Internal simulation clock</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Specification+window">Specification window</a></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729695 space=CST2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00096: Dynamic constraint

- page_id: `136729695`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729695/Dynamic+constraint
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2024-03-14T11:07:19.329+01:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['dynamic-constraint']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The concept of dynamic constraint is to allow parametric calculation of a Constraint Property with another constraint rather than one from its type (Constraint Block). Such constraint can be dynamically obtained during the simulation from typing Constraint Block or one of its subtypes. This section uses the *ForwardContractValuation.mdzip* sample to demonstrate this concept.

To dynamically apply different constraints to a Constraint Property

1. Create a Constraint Block with all needed Constraint Parameter(s).
2. Create Constraint Blocks which inherit the Constraint Block created in step 1.
3. Specify the constraint expression of each Constraint Blocks created in step 2.
4. Type the Constraint Property with the Constraint Block created in step 1.
5. Create a behavior, for example State Machine, that will assign the runtime object of different Constraint Blocks created in step 2 to the Constraint Property.

In *ForwardContractValuation.mdzip*, we will dynamically assign a constraint to the **Valuation_Rule** Constraint Property, which is typed by the **Valuation** Constraint Block (having two subtypes: **Long** **Valuation** and **Short** **Valuation** Constraint Blocks with two different constraint expressions) as shown in the following figure. The context of this simulation will be the **System** Block.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property.

In the following figure, the state machine is used as the classifier behavior of the **System** block. The **Calculate Contract Value** **for Long** **Position** and **Calculate Contract Value** **for Short** **Position** states apply different valuations to the **System** block via its entry activity.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
System Classifier Behavior State Machine.

The **Calculate Contract Value** **for Long** **Position**'s entry activity assigns the runtime value of the **Long** **Valuation** Constraint Block to the **Valuation_Rule** Constraint Property (see the first figure below). Consequently, the values of the constraint parameters will be calculated with the constraint expression specified in **Long** **Valuation**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State.

Similarly, the second figure below demonstrates the **Short** **Valuation** dynamic assignment. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the **ForwardContractValuation.mdzip** sample model that comes with the modeling tool.

To open this sample, do one of the following

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='ForwardContractValuation.mdzip'] **
- Find in the modeling tool <*install_root*>\samples\simulation\Parametrics\ForwardContractValuation.mdzip.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying the language for the expression' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value binding' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluation with causality' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communicating with evaluators through simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Built-in Math' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Integration with external Evaluators' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Trade study analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sample project' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>The concept of dynamic constraint is to allow parametric calculation of a Constraint Property with another constraint rather than one from its type (Constraint Block). Such constraint can be dynamically obtained during the simulation from typing Constraint Block or one of its subtypes. This section uses the <em>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</em> sample to demonstrate this concept. </p><p>To dynamically apply different constraints to a Constraint Property</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Constraint Block with all needed Constraint Parameter(s).</li><li>Create Constraint Blocks which inherit the Constraint Block created in step 1.</li><li>Specify the constraint expression of each Constraint Blocks created in step 2.</li><li>Type the Constraint Property with the Constraint Block created in step 1.</li><li>Create a behavior, for example State Machine, that will assign the runtime object of different Constraint Blocks created in step 2 to the Constraint Property. </li></ol><p>In <em>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</em>, we will dynamically assign a constraint to the <strong>Valuation_Rule</strong> Constraint Property, which is typed by the <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Block (having two subtypes: <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> and <strong>Short</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Blocks with two different constraint expressions) as shown in the following figure. The context of this simulation will be the <strong>System</strong> Block. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property" ac:alt="A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint01.png" /></ac:image><br />A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property.</h6><p>In the following figure, the state machine is used as the classifier behavior of the <strong>System</strong> block. The <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Long</strong> <strong>Position</strong> and <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Short</strong> <strong>Position</strong> states apply different valuations to the <strong>System</strong> block via its entry activity. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="System Classifier Behavior State Machine" ac:alt="System Classifier Behavior State Machine"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint02.png" /></ac:image><br />System Classifier Behavior State Machine.</h6><p>The <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Long</strong> <strong>Position</strong>'s entry activity assigns the runtime value of the <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Block to the <strong>Valuation_Rule</strong> Constraint Property (see the first figure below). Consequently, the values of the constraint parameters will be calculated with the constraint expression specified in <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State" ac:alt="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint03.png" /></ac:image><br />Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136728735 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 00097: Elements simulation

- page_id: `136728735`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136728735/Elements+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:58:36.931+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation
- labels: ['element-simulation', 'simulating-elements', 'unrestored-unknown-attachment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1331316376

1331316378

1331316375

**On this page**

1331316377

You can run a model simulation through the shortcut menu and the diagram toolbar.

To run a model simulation through the shortcut menu

1. Do one of the following:
  - Right-click an element shape on a diagram and select Simulation > Run . [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0001.png']
  - Right-click an element in the Containment tree and select Simulation > Run. [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0002.png']
2. In the toolbar of the open Simulation window, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to run the model. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To run a model through the diagram toolbar

1. Do any of the following:
  - In the diagram toolbar, click the Run or Run with Context button to run a diagram whose context is Class or Behavior (Composite Structure diagram, Activity diagram, State-Machine diagram, Use Case diagram, or Sequence diagram). Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the class or the behavior which is the context of the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_from_diagram_toolbar.png']
  - In a Class or a Package diagram, select the element you want to run and click the Run or Run with Context button on the diagram toolbar. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the selected element on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_from_diagram_toolbar2.png']
2. In the toolbar of the open Simulation window, click the Start button to run the model.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729996 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00098: Engine

- page_id: `136729996`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729996/Engine
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:42.833+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Developer Guide > Java APIs
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

730383780

730383782

730383781

An Execution Engine is a core component of the entire execution mechanism. It defines how to execute a set of Element types, e.g., Elements in an Activity diagram, Elements in a State Machine diagram, and so on.

API developers can create their own execution engines with the corresponding engine descriptors and listeners, and register those engines to the simulation manager.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94e00025-b4e3-425b-a15c-f89241ded743"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">730383780</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="466a7fa5-d179-410e-9832-7731c32a159a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">730383782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="74d39ae2-ab3f-4199-8024-0fa16cf0c96a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">730383781</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>An Execution Engine is a core component of the entire execution mechanism. It defines how to execute a set of Element types, e.g., Elements in an Activity diagram, Elements in a State Machine diagram, and so on.</p><p>API developers can create their own execution engines with the corresponding engine descriptors and listeners, and register those engines to the simulation manager.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729664 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00100: Evaluating expressions

- page_id: `136729664`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729664/Evaluating+expressions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:22.239+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['expression-evaluation', 'evaluating-expressions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

402791423

402791426

1240126456

**On this page**

4

402791425

This section explains how to evaluate the mathematical equations and logical expressions in Cameo Simulation Toolkit.

Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides constraint parameters substitution in the expressions to improve the human readability of the constraint parameters. All user interface objects (including tooltips, Console panel, and Variables panel related to a constraint property connected to a value property) will display the value property name (actual binding) instead of the constraint parameter name. This feature is applicable at the user interface level only, not at the original constraint property. For example, “Width” and “Height” (value properties) are used instead of “w” and “h” (parameter properties) in constraints, as shown in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Constraint parameters substitution in expressions.

##### Parametric updates when States change

Simulation can check the object State during the evaluation. For example, if an elevator is moving, the door must be closed, or if the car is moving, the speed must be more than 0.

- Y ou can use [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action scripts APIs' space=''] (or _*context*_ ) in the Specification of a constraint in order to access the runtime object of the owner of the Constraint property.
- If the State check, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Checking States' space=''] , is used in the Specification of a constraint, the constraint will be re-evaluated on a State change with the parametric context and any objects passed as parameters into the constraint. The State change supported on simple, composite, orthogonal, and submachine.
- The Parametric update occurs after the State activation, so it depends on the selected [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State activation semantics' space=''] option in the Project Option dialog:
  - 
    - If Before entry (by default) is set, the following sequence applies: the State will be activated, the Parametric will be updated, and the Entry behavior will be simulated.
    - If After entry is set, the following sequence applies: the Entry behavior will be simulated, the state will be activated, and the Parametric will be updated.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The constraint re-evaluated on a State change in the Parametric of the context object

##### Parametric updates when Activities start or end

Simulation can check whether there is a specified Activity being executed at that time in Object_ by using the **performs(activity String)** function, e.g., *self.performs("Activity_1")* will return **true** when *Activity_1* is being executed under itself and return **false** if it is not executed under other Object_ or anywhere else. When the condition expression is evaluated at the first time, a listener for Activity execution will register the specified Activity in the *performs("Activity_1")* function. The listener will re-evaluate the condition expression when the context object starts or ends executing Activities.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4f42111-687a-4748-b5d3-81a587afae04"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791423</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c45b9e53-7c68-40ef-9eaf-7b925e0725fe"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791426</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c24489a2-4d22-42bb-9442-a563aa2cc2ce"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1240126456</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8cc367ee-3cd0-4caf-91db-cd6a844fef3f"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="66bb8bf7-3b02-4c09-b2aa-d8e6dc365783"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791425</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="Textbody">This section explains how to evaluate the mathematical equations and logical expressions in Cameo Simulation Toolkit.</p><p class="Textbody">Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides constraint parameters substitution in the expressions to improve the human readability of the constraint parameters. All user interface objects (including tooltips, Console panel, and Variables panel related to a constraint property connected to a value property) will display the value property name (actual binding) instead of the constraint parameter name. This feature is applicable at the user interface level only, not at the original constraint property. For example, “Width” and “Height” (value properties) are used instead of “w” and “h” (parameter properties) in constraints, as shown in the following figure.</p><p class="Textbody"><br /></p><h6 class="Textbody" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="evaluate_expressions.png" /></ac:image><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">Constraint parameters substitution in expressions.</span></h6><h3>Parametric updates when States change</h3><p>Simulation can check the object State during the evaluation. For example, if an elevator is moving, the door must be closed, or if the car is moving, the speed must be more than 0.</p><ul><li>Y<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2930655c-9375-4de6-a873-249e3bafc063">ou can</ac:inline-comment-marker> use <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Action scripts APIs" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[$context$]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">(or</span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> _<em>context</em>_</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">) in the Specification of a constraint in order to access the runtime object of the owner of the Constraint property.</span></li><li>If the State check, <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link ac:anchor="Checking the State of an object"><ri:page ri:content-title="Checking States" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ALH.inState]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, is used in the Specification of a constraint, the constraint will be re-evaluated on a State change with the parametric context and any objects passed as parameters into the constraint. The State change supported on simple, composite, orthogonal, and submachine.</span></li><li>The Parametric update occurs after the State activation, so it depends on the selected <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="State activation semantics" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[State Activation Semantics]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> option in the <strong>Project Option</strong> dialog:<br /><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li>If <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Before entry</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> (by default) is set, the following sequence applies: the State will be activated, the Parametric will be updated, and the Entry behavior will be simulated.</span></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fdc53a1d-dc6d-4b6c-a16e-49525609121b">If </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fdc53a1d-dc6d-4b6c-a16e-49525609121b">After entry</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fdc53a1d-dc6d-4b6c-a16e-49525609121b"> is set</ac:inline-comment-marker>, the following sequence applies: the Entry behavior will be simulated, the state will be activated, and the Parametric will be updated.</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><br /></span><br /><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><br /></span></li></ul></li></ul></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1338"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SIM-16490.PNG" /></ac:image><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="380768b9-4762-48ca-9fa4-f9a31ee56770">The constraint re-evaluated on a State change in the Parametric of the contex</ac:inline-comment-marker>t object</h6><h3 style="text-align: left;">Parametric updates when Activities start or end</h3><p>Simulation can check whether there is a specified Activity being executed at that time in Object_ by using the <strong>performs(activity String)</strong> function, e.g., <em>self.performs(&quot;Activity_1&quot;)</em> will return <strong>true</strong> when <em>Activity_1</em> is being executed under itself and return <strong>false</strong> if it is not executed under other Object_ or anywhere else. When the condition expression is evaluated at the first time, a listener for Activity execution will register the specified Activity in the <em>performs(&quot;Activity_1&quot;)</em> function. The listener will re-evaluate the condition expression when the context object starts or ends executing Activities.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729718 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00101: Evaluating strings from command input

- page_id: `136729718`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729718/Evaluating+strings+from+command+input
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:25.769+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Built-in Math
- labels: ['evaluating-strings']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can type generic mathematical equations directly in command input of the Simulation Console when it prompts for the built-in math. The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10;
- math» y = 20;
- math» z = x + y

z = 30.0000 (the calculation result) will be displayed on the simulation console.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The result of a calculation expression that ends with a semicolon (;) in the built-in Math will be assigned to the corresponding variable in the selected built-in math environment. It will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

Or, if you type, e.g., in command input of the Simulation Console, the examples are as follows

- math» a = true;
- math» b = false;
- math» c = a & b;

If **false** is the result of a calculation, it will be assigned to the variable c, but it will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

If an expression does not contain any assignment operators, the result will be assigned to the variable 'ans'. The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10;
- math» 20 + x
- ans = 30.0000 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

You can calculate multiple expressions at the same time by ending each expression with a semicolon (;). The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10; y = 20; z = x + y; a = z / x
- a = 3 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can type generic mathematical equations directly in command input of the Simulation Console when it prompts for the built-in math. The examples are as follows </p><ul><li>math» x = 10;</li><li>math» y = 20;</li><li>math» z = x + y </li></ul><p style="margin-left: 90.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline _mce_tagged_br" />z = 30.0000 (the calculation result) will be displayed on the simulation console. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7dc5773-083b-42e2-adb0-4f38479bcf86"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The result of a calculation expression that ends with a semicolon (;) in the built-in Math will be assigned to the corresponding variable in the selected built-in math environment. It will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Or, if you type, e.g., in command input of the Simulation Console, the examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» a = true;</li><li>math» b = false;</li><li>math» c = a &amp; b; </li></ul><p>If <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">false</strong> is the result of a calculation, it will be assigned to the variable c, but it will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </p><p>If an expression does not contain any assignment operators, the result will be assigned to the variable 'ans'. The examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» x = 10;</li><li>math» 20 + x</li><li>ans = 30.0000 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </li></ul><p>You can calculate multiple expressions at the same time by ending each expression with a semicolon (;). The examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» x = 10; y = 20; z = x + y; a = z / x </li><li>a = 3 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </li></ul><p> </p>
````

467754231

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

467754233

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

467754232

As described in the section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mathematical equation' space='CST2024x'], the parametric evaluator is capable of solving expressions in the mathematical equation to find the value of the target from the value of the given. We could say that the target is an unknown value that you want to find and the given is a known input value. Normally, a target is a variable on the left-hand side of an equation and a given is a variable on the right-hand side.

Sometimes, however, you know the value of the variable on the left-hand side of an equation and need to find the value of the variable on the right-hand side. You can use Cameo Simulation Toolkit to obtain the given variable if you integrate an external evaluator, which supports solving symbolic equations, to Cameo Simulation Toolkit. MATLAB with [Symbolic Math Toolbox](https://www.mathworks.com/products/symbolic.html) is one of the external evaluators you can use.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

As a prerequisite, you must integrate MATLAB with [Symbolic Math Toolbox](https://www.mathworks.com/products/symbolic.html) successfully. See also [Integration with MATLAB](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CST2024x/Integration+with+MATLAB).

If the language that defines an expression of a constraint block needs to be solved by an external evaluator that is capable of solving symbolic equations, you can specify what property is the target and the given through the **Causality** column in the **Variables** pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using MATLAB to evaluate mathematical equations.

The preceding figure shows the block object **Circle** in the **Variables** pane. It has a constraint property typed by the constraint block **Circle** **Area**. The expression of the constraint block is **{area** **=** **3.14159** *** **(radius** **^** **2)}**. The default causality of **area** and **radius** will be **target** and **given**respectively. However, you can click the drop-down list box in the **Causality** column to change the causality of **area** and **radius** to be **given** and **target**. Cameo Simulation Toolkit will then evaluate the value of **radius** from the given value of **area**.

The expression of the constraint block **Circle** **Area** in the figure shows two roots: (i) a positive value 2.8209 and (ii) a negative value -2.8209. The parametric evaluator needs only one root to evaluate the value of a radius from the given value of an area. Therefore, the **Roots** **selection**dialog will open for you to select which root you want as shown in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Roots Selection dialog.

The number of roots varies according to the expression and the multiplicity of a property, which is the target. Therefore, it is possible to select more than one root. The following scenarios show you how to work with multiple roots. 
 
**Scenario 1** 
The following figure shows the constraint **Test** **Multiple** **Root** **1** is applied to block **A**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Constraint Test Multiple Root 1 is Applied to Block A

**Scenario 2** 
The given types of causality of y and x are given and target respectively. Once Cameo Simulation Toolkit finished executing block **A** and performing a parametric simulation to satisfy the constraint **Test** **Multiple** **Root** **1**, the equation would result in three values of x and all are the possible roots for the equation. Therefore, the **Roots selection** dialog would open, allowing you to select one of these three values as the root (see the following figure).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Roots Selection Dialog.

**Scenario 3** 
However, you may select more than one value as the root. But, the number of values you can select cannot be more than the upper multiplicity. Otherwise, an error will occur and an **Error** message dialog will open. The following figure shows that *two* values were selected as the roots when in fact, the upper multiplicity of x value property was *one*. Therefore, an error occurred and the **Error** dialog would open.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Error Occurs when the Number of Roots Selected is Greater than the Upper Multiplicity.

467754229

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729906 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00103: Excel Helper APIs

- page_id: `136729906`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729906/Excel+Helper+APIs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:39.879+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides APIs to import data from an Excel file into runtime values, and export the runtime values to an Excel file. Either a «DiagramTableMapToDataSource», an Instance Table linked to an Excel file, or an[«ImportMap»](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Saving+an+Import+Map)can be used as the*mappingElement*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The provided APIs in Cameo Simulation Toolkit are given in the following pages

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides APIs to import data from an Excel file into runtime values, and export the runtime values to an Excel file. <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Either a «DiagramTableMapToDataSource», an Instance Table linked to an Excel file, or an </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Saving+an+Import+Map">«ImportMap»</a><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"> can be used as the<span> </span></span><em style="text-align: left;">mappingElement</em><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:height="178"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Excel Helper APIs.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Excel Helper APIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p>The provided APIs in Cameo Simulation Toolkit are given in the following pages</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7ca20eb3-a3ed-4464-a16f-0c04fff1269b" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729437 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00104: Executing Activities

- page_id: `136729437`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729437/Executing+Activities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:11.533+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['activity-execution', 'executing-activities']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

143637310

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

143637312

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

143637311

You can add some breakpoints to the model created when [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='CST2024x'] before executing it. This section demonstrates how to suspend the model simulation at some specific points with breakpoints. You can use either the diagram or browser context menu to add a breakpoint to an element.

The following example shows you how to add breakpoints to pins **a** and **b** of the action **print**. Once the model simulation has reached these pins, the simulation will be suspended.

To add a breakpoint to an element and simulate the model

1. Right-click an element and select **Simulation** > **Add****Breakpoint(s)**. The breakpoints will be shown in the **Breakpoints** pane of the Simulation window. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Breakpoints pane in the Simulation window. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Clicking Window > Simulation on the main menu to open the Simulation window.

2. Right-click **instance** in the containment browser and select **Simulation** > **Run** to simulate the model from **instance**, which is the InstanceSpecification of the **SumPrinter** Classifier.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. A new simulation session will be created and displayed in the **Sessions** pane of the Simulation window. The symbol of the elements with breakpoints attached will be highlighted in yellow by default. Click the **Start** button on the **Simulation** window toolbar to animate the simulation on the **Print-Sum** Activity diagram.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

4. The simulation will be suspended when pin **a** or **b** of the **print** action is activated. You can hover your mouse pointer over the active element to see its runtime value.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

5. Click the **Resume** button on the **Simulation** window toolbar to continue the simulation.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

6. The simulation will be suspended again when pin **b** is activated. Click the **Resume** button to continue the simulation. In the **Console** pane of the **Simulation** window, you can see the printed value of 10, which is the summation between 2 and 8.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If you do not want to display animation (silent simulation), you can create **Simulation****Configuration** to customize the simulation, select **instance** as the **simulationTarget**, and set **silent** to **true**. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space='CST2024x'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space='CST2024x'] for more information.

143637308

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space='CST2024x']

Right-click <strong>instance</strong> in the containment browser and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong> to simulate the model from <strong>instance</strong>, which is the InstanceSpecification of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Classifier.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Executing the InstanceSpecification of the SumPrinter Classifier" ac:alt="Executing the InstanceSpecification of the SumPrinter Classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ExecuteInstanceSpecification.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">3. A new simulation session will be created and displayed in the <strong>Sessions</strong> pane of the Simulation window. The symbol of the elements with breakpoints attached will be highlighted in yellow by default. Click the <strong>Start</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to animate the simulation on the <strong>Print-Sum</strong> Activity diagram.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="A Simulation Session in the Sessions Pane of the Simulation Window" ac:alt="A Simulation Session in the Sessions Pane of the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0079.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">4.  The simulation will be suspended when pin <strong>a</strong> or <strong>b</strong> of the <strong>print</strong> action is activated. Click the <strong>Resume</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to continue the simulation.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Resume Button in the Simulation Window" ac:alt="The Resume Button in the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ResumeToolbarButton.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">6. The simulation will be suspended again when pin <strong>b</strong> is activated. Click the <strong>Resume</strong> button to continue the simulation. In the <strong>Console</strong> pane of the <strong>Simulation</strong> window, you can see the printed value of 10, which is the summation between 2 and 8. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Console Pane of the Simulation Window Showing the Printed Result of the Summation" ac:alt="The Console Pane of the Simulation Window Showing the Printed Result of the Summation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110195_PrintSum.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="655cdf81-caa8-4949-af0d-1e20a9ec966e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you do not want to display animation (silent simulation), you can create <strong>Simulation </strong><strong>Configuration</strong> to customize the simulation, select <strong>instance</strong> as the <strong>simulationTarget</strong>, and set <strong>silent</strong> to <strong>true</strong>. See <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Simulation Configuration and UI modeling" /></ac:link> for more information.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="491b3a64-616a-4592-8c9d-fce1054abbc5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637308</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Simulation Configuration and UI modeling" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136729588 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00105: Executing an interaction model

- page_id: `136729588`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136729588/Executing+an+interaction+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:18.468+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation
- labels: ['interaction-model-execution']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section illustrates the steps to simulate the interaction model mentioned in section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='CST2024x']. 
 
To simulate the interaction model in section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='CST2024x']

1. Right-click the package **Model** in the containment browser and select **Create Element** > **Package**. Name the package, for example, InstancePackage. The following figure shows the Containment tree containing the objects within its hierarchy. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the **InstancePackage** and select **Create** **Element** > **Instance** **Specification**. Name the instance specification, for example, Sys. The following figure shows the creation of an InstancePackage element from its shortcut menu.. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the instance specification Sys and select Specification to open the specification dialog.
4. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] browse button in the **Classifier** row to open the **Select Elements** dialog (see the first figure below) and select class **System** as the classifier of the instance specification **Sys** (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The following figure shows the elements that can be selected in the **Select Elements** dialog. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Repeat steps 2 and 3 above to create the other instance specifications named **aa1** and **bb1** of the classifiers **A** and **B** respectively. The following figure shows the Containment tree depicting objects within its hierarchy. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Right-click the instance specification Sys and select Simulation > Run (see the first figure below). The Simulation window will appear. [ATTACHMENT filename='InteractionSimulation29.png']
7. Click the **Start** button in the **Simulation** window. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Simulation **Console** shows the results of a simulation and the constraints which have passed or failed. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730111 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00106: Executing the StopWatch class

- page_id: `136730111`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730111/Executing+the+StopWatch+class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:46.894+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The StopWatch Class is now ready to be executed. You can execute the StopWatch Class either from the containment browser or from the diagram.

To execute the StopWatch Class

1. Either (i) select the StopWatch class in the containment tree and click the Run button on the Simulation Control main toolbar or (ii) right-click the StopWatch class and select Simulation > Run . [ATTACHMENT filename='shortcutmenu_run.png']
2. The Simulation window will open. It contains four tabs: (i) Sessions , (ii) Console , (iii) Variables , and (iv) Breakpoints tabs. At this point, a simulation session to execute the StopWatch Class will be created. You can see this session in the Sessions tab of the Simulation window. [ATTACHMENT filename='simulation_window.png']
3. Click the **Start** button [ATTACHMENT filename='start_button.png'] on the Simulation window toolbar.

Once you have clicked the **Run** **Execution** button, the Object of the **StopWatch** Class will be created and shown in the **Variables** tab. The Classifier Behavior of the **StopWatch** class and the **StopWatch** State Machine will be executed respectively. A new session to execute the **StopWatch** State Machine will be created under the simulation session of the **StopWatch** class, and the **StopWatch** State Machine diagram will be open and ready for the simulation.

The **StopWatch** State Machine execution will start from the initial stage and automatically move to the **ready** state because the transition which connects between the initial stage and **ready** state does not have a defined trigger. When the **ready** State is entered, the **reset** Activity, which is assigned as the entry to the **ready** State, will be invoked. This Activity also calls the **resetTime** activity of the StopWatch Class for setting the time value to be zero. At this point, a new simulation session will be created to execute the **resetTime** Activity, and the **resetTime** Activity diagram will be open and animated.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 The execution of the **resetTime** Activity will be terminated once it has reached the final stage. When the simulation stops, the session executing the **resetTime** Activity will be closed, the StopWatch State Machine diagram will be reactivated, and the simulation will wait for an Event signal at the ready State. You can browse the **StopWatch** object in the **Variables** tab to see the time value.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 Now we want to trigger the StopWatch object from the ready State to the running State. To do this, we need to send a start signal to the StopWatch object. 
 
To send an event (start) signal to the object of the **StopWatch**Class

1. Select the StopWatch node in the Variables tab. The signals that can be sent to the StopWatch will be listed in the Trigger drop-down menu.
2. Select the start signal from the Triggers drop-down menu (see the following figure).

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can click the arrow button on the left or right-hand side of the **Simulation** window toolbar to scroll the toolbar to the left or to the right.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Once the start Signal has been sent, the **StopWatch** will go to the **running** State. At the entry to the **running** State, the **increase** Activity will be defined; therefore, it will be invoked. The **increase** Activity will then call the **increaseTime** Activity of the **StopWatch** Class to increment the time value by one second. A new simulation session will be created to execute the **increaseTime** Activity, and the **increaseTime** Activity diagram will be open and animated. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 The **increaseTime** Activity will be completely executed when its Final stage has been reached. Once the execution is complete, the simulation session of the **increaseTime** Activity will be closed and the **StopWatch** State Machine will be activated again. Now you can see in the **Variables** tab that the time value of the StopWatch object has been incremented by one.

The StopWatch object will still be at the **running** State and the time Event will be triggered at this State every second. Therefore, a new simulation session will be created to execute the **increaseTime** activity every second, and the time value will increment by one every time the increaseTime Activity is executed.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 While the StopWatch object is at the **running** State, we can send a **split** signal to trigger the object to the **paused** State or send a **stop** signal to trigger the object to the **stopped** State. 
 
 To send a split signal to the StopWatch object

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the split signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the paused State.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 You can change the paused StopWatch object to the **running** State by sending an **unsplit** signal to the object. 
 
To change the StopWatch object from a paused state to a running State

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the unsplit signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the running State, and the time value will continuously increase. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6240bec3246339c7c6e2a46d39e55614.png']

To send a stop Signal to the StopWatch object

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the stop signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The State of the StopWatch object will be changed to the stopped State. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavaf4fd7ef425f080b2957e3bda2118678.png']

At the **stopped** State, if the **reset** signal is sent to the StopWatch object, the object will go to the **ready** State, and the time value will be reset to zero at the entry to the **ready** state by calling the **resetTime** Activity. If the **stop** signal is sent to the object again, the object will go to the final stage, and then the simulation will stop and all of the simulation sessions will be closed.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The StopWatch Class is now ready to be executed. You can execute the StopWatch Class either from the containment browser or from the diagram. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To execute the StopWatch Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Either (i) select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class in the containment tree and click the <strong>Run</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation </strong><strong>Control</strong> main toolbar or (ii) right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Shortcut Menu Run" ac:alt="Shortcut Menu Run"><ri:attachment ri:filename="shortcutmenu_run.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window will open. It contains four tabs: (i) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sessions</strong>, (ii) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Console</strong>, (iii) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Variables</strong>, and (iv) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Breakpoints</strong> tabs. At this point, a simulation session to execute the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class will be created. You can see this session in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sessions</strong> tab of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Simulation Console" ac:alt="Simulation Console"><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulation_window.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <span style="line-height: 20.0px;"><strong>Start</strong></span> button <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="start_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once you have clicked the <strong>Run</strong> <strong>Execution</strong> button, the Object of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class will be created and shown in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab. The Classifier Behavior of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be executed respectively. A new session to execute the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be created under the simulation session of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class, and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine diagram will be open and ready for the simulation.</p><p>The <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine execution will start from the initial stage and automatically move to the <strong>ready</strong> state because the transition which connects between the initial stage and <strong>ready</strong> state does not have a defined trigger. When the <strong>ready</strong> State is entered, the <strong>reset</strong> Activity, which is assigned as the entry to the <strong>ready</strong> State, will be invoked. This Activity also calls the <strong>resetTime</strong> activity of the StopWatch Class for setting the time value to be zero. At this point, a new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity, and the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram will be open and animated.</p><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Executing the ResetTime Activity" ac:alt="Executing the ResetTime Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0d2d0cab3d8ad736e9f36d62ab703bb2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The execution of the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity will be terminated once it has reached the final stage. When the simulation stops, the session executing the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity will be closed, the StopWatch State Machine diagram will be reactivated, and the simulation will wait for an Event signal at the ready State. You can browse the <strong>StopWatch</strong> object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab to see the time value.</p><p><br /><ac:image ac:title="(Upper) The Reset Activity Invoked at the Entry of the Ready State and (Lower) Browsing the StopWatch Object in the Variables Tab for the Time Value" ac:alt="(Upper) The Reset Activity Invoked at the Entry of the Ready State and (Lower) Browsing the StopWatch Object in the Variables Tab for the Time Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav710b7b9c304fbabadfdd01d17a76b53d.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Now we want to trigger the StopWatch object from the ready State to the running State. To do this, we need to send a start signal to the StopWatch object. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To send an event (start) signal to the object of the <strong>StopWatch </strong>Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> node in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab. The signals that can be sent to the <strong>StopWatch</strong> will be listed in the <strong>Trigger</strong> drop-down menu.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">start</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu (see the following figure). </li></ol><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="356a0572-f189-4c33-9671-47aa551d6212"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can click the arrow button on the left or right-hand side of the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to scroll the toolbar to the left or to the right.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Selecting the Start Signal from the Triggers Drop-down Menu" ac:alt="Selecting the Start Signal from the Triggers Drop-down Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav26b7e0c79977913f0326c7502218e610.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once the start Signal has been sent, the <strong>StopWatch</strong> will go to the <strong>running</strong> State. At the entry to the <strong>running</strong> State, the <strong>increase</strong> Activity will be defined; therefore, it will be invoked. The <strong>increase</strong> Activity will then call the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class to increment the time value by one second. A new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity, and the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity diagram will be open and animated.<br /> <ac:image ac:title="The Execution of the IncreaseTime Activity (Upper) and the Time Value of the StopWatch Object Incrementing by One (Lower) " ac:alt="The Execution of the IncreaseTime Activity (Upper) and the Time Value of the StopWatch Object Incrementing by One (Lower) "><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav01fbe03f0d8da98602e00cbb751b5fb1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity will be completely executed when its Final stage has been reached. Once the execution is complete, the simulation session of the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity will be closed and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be activated again. Now you can see in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab that the time value of the StopWatch object has been incremented by one.</p><p>The StopWatch object will still be at the <strong>running</strong> State and the time Event will be triggered at this State every second. Therefore, a new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>increaseTime</strong> activity every second, and the time value will increment by one every time the increaseTime Activity is executed.</p><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Activating the Transition to Self Every Second" ac:alt="Activating the Transition to Self Every Second"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4170bb121ff21494de21372aff8918de.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />  <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> While the StopWatch object is at the <strong>running</strong> State, we can send a <strong>split</strong> signal to trigger the object to the <strong>paused</strong> State or send a <strong>stop</strong> signal to trigger the object to the <strong>stopped</strong> State. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To send a split signal to the StopWatch object</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">split</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paused</strong> State. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Paused State" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Paused State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaf5f9748fcb0d4a3e1491cfa121f2468.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /> You can change the paused StopWatch object to the <strong>running</strong> State by sending an <strong>unsplit</strong> signal to the object. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To change the StopWatch object from a paused state to a running State</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">unsplit</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">running</strong> State, and the time value will continuously increase. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object from the Paused State to the Running State with Unsplit Signal" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object from the Paused State to the Running State with Unsplit Signal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav6240bec3246339c7c6e2a46d39e55614.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To send a stop Signal to the StopWatch object</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The State of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> State.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Stopped State by Sending the Stop Signal" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Stopped State by Sending the Stop Signal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaf4fd7ef425f080b2957e3bda2118678.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> At the <strong>stopped</strong> State, if the <strong>reset</strong> signal is sent to the StopWatch object, the object will go to the <strong>ready</strong> State, and the time value will be reset to zero at the entry to the <strong>ready</strong> state by calling the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity. If the <strong>stop</strong> signal is sent to the object again, the object will go to the final stage, and then the simulation will stop and all of the simulation sessions will be closed. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136730126 space=CST2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00107: Executing the StopWatch instance specification

- page_id: `136730126`
- space_key: `CST2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/CST2024x/pages/136730126/Executing+the+StopWatch+instance+specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T07:59:47.267+02:00`
- ancestors: Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In the previous section, we have shown you how to execute the StopWatch model from the StopWatch Class. You can also use Cameo Simulation Toolkit to execute the model from an InstanceSpecification. With this feature, you can specify values to the slots of an InstanceSpecification. These values will be used as the input values for the execution such as using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction to read the values.

In this tutorial, our StopWatch contains only one value property, which is time. The time value will always reset to zero whenever the system enters the ready State. If we create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch and use it for model execution, the result will be the same as the model execution of the StopWatch Class.

To create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch

1. Open a System Class diagram.
2. Click Instance on the Class Diagram toolbar and click the System Class diagram. A new InstanceSpecification will be created and the Select Classifier dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-classifier-a.png']
3. Select the StopWatch Class and click OK .
4. On the Class diagram, name the created InstanceSpecification "stopwatch" . Now we will need to create values and assign them to the slots.

To create values and assign them to the slots

1. Right-click the symbol of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification and select Specification to open the Specification window of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification.
2. Select the Slots node on the left-hand side of the dialog.
3. Select the Structural Feature of the slot to which the values will be applied. In this case, select time:Integer[1] . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav9a7cf25ce112886b657a3d1ed123cab8.png']
4. Click the Create Value button. A new integer value will be created for the slot. If an Integer value is selected, a zero will be created by default. You can modify this value by typing a new value into the Value box. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav26add523b109fefb885b3d0398fc018f.png']
5. Click Close to close the Specification window.

To start executing the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, right-click it either on the **System** Class diagram or in the containment browser and click **Execute**. A simulation session will be created in the **Sessions** tab of the Simulation window to execute this instance. The steps to run the execution are the same as those to execute the **StopWatch** Class.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In the previous section, we have shown you how to execute the StopWatch model from the StopWatch Class. You can also use Cameo Simulation Toolkit to execute the model from an InstanceSpecification. With this feature, you can specify values to the slots of an InstanceSpecification. These values will be used as the input values for the execution such as using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction to read the values.</p><p>In this tutorial, our StopWatch contains only one value property, which is time. The time value will always reset to zero whenever the system enters the ready State. If we create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch and use it for model execution, the result will be the same as the model execution of the StopWatch Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a <strong>System</strong> Class diagram.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Instance</strong> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class Diagram</strong> toolbar and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">System</strong> Class diagram. A new InstanceSpecification will be created and the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Classifier</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Select Classifier Dialog" ac:alt="The Select Classifier Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-classifier-a.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li><li>On the Class diagram, name the created InstanceSpecification <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;stopwatch&quot;</em>. Now we will need to create values and assign them to the slots.</li></ol><p> </p><p>To create values and assign them to the slots</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the symbol of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open the Specification window of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopwatch</strong> InstanceSpecification.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Slots</strong> node on the left-hand side of the dialog.</li><li>Select the Structural Feature of the slot to which the values will be applied. In this case, select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">time:Integer[1]</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Specification Dialog of the StopWatch InstanceSpecification" ac:alt="The Specification Dialog of the StopWatch InstanceSpecification"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav9a7cf25ce112886b657a3d1ed123cab8.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> button. A new integer value will be created for the slot. If an Integer value is selected, a zero will be created by default. You can modify this value by typing a new value into the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> box.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Setting a Slot Value in the Specification Dialog" ac:alt="Setting a Slot Value in the Specification Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav26add523b109fefb885b3d0398fc018f.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> to close the Specification window. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To start executing the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, right-click it either on the <strong>System</strong> Class diagram or in the containment browser and click <strong>Execute</strong>. A simulation session will be created in the <strong>Sessions</strong> tab of the Simulation window to execute this instance. The steps to run the execution are the same as those to execute the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Simulation Window during the Execution of StopWatch Model from InstanceSpecification" ac:alt="The Simulation Window during the Execution of StopWatch Model from InstanceSpecification"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav1ed8cb9f4b7f147c9cceb298df636342.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p><p> </p>
````
